column176: In the early 1900s
column177: a man named T.W. \nRosborough \"arranged to meet with civic leaders \nfrom the town of Amity
column178:
located... directly east of 
the timberlands that the company would buy. To 
these men he described his plans for building a 
lumber manufacturing plant
column179:
stating that he would 
like to buy land at Amity for the mill. The town 
would benefit from having mill workers who would 
spend their wages with the local merchants.
   "'We would rather you not locate here
column180:
' the town 
leaders told Rosborough. 'We do not want a 
sawmill.  We won't sell you land to build a mill.' 
They did not want transient mill workers
column181: nor did \nthey want blacks.\n   There were no black people in Amity
column182:
only a 
lingering hostility said to have stemmed from an 
incident in the past when a black person %u2014 or 
someone having Nero ancestry %u2014 had caused 
trouble there while masquerading as a white."  So 
Rosborough built his mill and a town (Rosboro) four 
miles north of Amity.
   Whites in the area (Amity) threatened 
Rosborough's black workers. "Blacks were to work 
only at the mill
column183: not in the logging operation
column184:
for 
loggers had to live in camps in the woods where 
blacks would be even more vulnerable to harm from 
neighboring whites. The first black emlooyees to 
arrive at Rosboro had to live in tents. Since threats 
kept circulating
column185: Rosborough built a high board \nfence around them
column186: with gates that could be locked \nagainst intruders
column187:
and employed trustworthy white 
men to stand guard over the compound at night.  
After a while the threats died down and the 
workmen and their families moved into the new 
houses in Rosboro's black neighborhood
column188: \" Nigger \nTown.   \n   \"One night
column189: men on horses rode through Rosboro \nand dropped leaflets saying that the blacks were to \nget out
column190:
or they would get hurt. Rosborough 
promptly called the leaders of the black community 
into the lumber company office and handed them 
several rifles. If any outsider came into the Quarters 
to harm the blacks
column191: he told them
column192: they were to \nprotect themselves. ('Old Man Rosborough didn't go \nfor that
column193: beatin' his niggers
column194: ' says a white who \nworked at Rosboro.) In time the furor died down
column195:
but the blacks long remembered what Rosborough 
had done for them.
   "Rosborough
column196: however
column197:
remained a person of his 
own time; he never attempted to change the 
prevailing separation of the races. He did feel 
responsible for protecting his black employees
column198: one \nreason being that he wanted the best sawmill men \nhe could find
column199: regardless of color... One Rosboro \nresident recalls
column200:
'The niggers done most of the 
stacking of the lumber.  Most of the work they done 
was drudgery work that white people wouldn't do.'  
That was true
column201: but as a lumber journalist once \nexplained
column202:
'There has never been as precise a 
separation of jobs into "white man's job" and 
"Negro job" in the lumber industry as in many other 
industries.' Rosborough realized that employing 
blacks made economic sense
column203:
but he also liked 
black people."

California used to have a sign that said something to the effect that non whites were to be out of town by sunset. I remember my family talking about it when I was a kid. I don't know when they took the sign down but I'll bet it wasn't until the 60's. I assume it meant Indians too."

"Taft was a  virulently racist place at the time [1950s and 60s]
column184: and African Americans were especially despised... I do not know if the strictures barring nonwhites in Taft were official or merely de facto. The existence of an 'unwritten law' against the presence of African Americans after sundown
column185: however

column188: it was football. \r\nIf memory serves
column189:
this interlude with tolerance lasted a year or two until some incident resulted in a near riot and the expulsion of resident black

column176: Email 1\/2008\n\nI was born to a working-class family in Darien
column177:
CT in 1951 and spent most of my life up into my late 20's there. 

It was well known -- though never spoken of above a whisper -- that no-one could sell a house to either a black family or a Jewish family. 

One Jewish family moved into Darien sometime in the early '60s and one of the daughters was in my class.  The first black -- or actually mixed race -- family moved into a neighborhood almost on the Stamford-Darien border right about 1970. I became good friends with them since the son belonged to an amateur theatrical company I was a member of and around that time I also joined the Baha'i community that the family belonged to. As I recall
column178:
the children was mercilessly hounded by the high school ruffians and this is probably why they moved out of Darien 
within a year or so.
***

email 1/2008
I believe there were some neighborhood kids (15-17yo - ish) and they banged on the door and ran. The bag had excrement in it - the intent being that you step in it as you stomp out the fire.I also seem to recall (we are talking about 35 yrs ago so I am a bit foggy on it) that the family said they had alot of calls with hang ups. I think Burt (the son) was accepted in school since he was very charming and athletic - but also I am sure for the sheer novelty of this tall dark skinned afro-ed young man in our midst! column196: Mose Norman and Julius \n'July' Perry

Florida. Many sources have concluded

   "On November 2nd
column201: Norman and Perry go to \nthe Ocoee polls to cast their votes. Though Norman \nhad paid his poll tax
column202: he does not see his name on the \nregister
column203: he and Perry are turned away and told not to \ncome back. Mose Norman returns to the polls later \nthat evening with a shot gun. An altercation ensues
column204:
and Mose Norman is pistol whipped and sent away a 
second time. Mose Norman is never heard from again.
   "Word spreads rapidly of the altercation at the polls 
and later that evening word of a race riot has spread 
as far away as Georgia.

column205: over 250 Klansmen from around the state of Florida and white Ocoee residents have collected inside the town and begin a burning spree in the Black section of town. The Black citizens flee for their lives into the orange groves

Blacks would not inhabit the city until sixty one years later in 1981.\"\n\n\tA total of 330 acres plus 48 city lots owned by 18 Black families living in Ocoee

column176:
Georgia Historian:
"Will Holmes wrote article(s?) about whitecapping 
several years ago. I believe he dealt with all white 
towns. 
"I found that different counties responded 
differently to the whitecapping movements of the 
early twentieth century. Some cast out all blacks
column177: \nsuch as Forsyth County. Others did not. I am giving \nparticular attention to Banks County
column178:
which 
quashed an eviction movement in the late 1910s 
after an African American was accused of raping 
and murdering a white prostitute. Banks had a large 
black landowning community with close ties to 
white leaders who came to their defense. One 
elderly black woman I interviewed remembered her 
white neighbors warning her landowning father to 
let them use violence to defend the black family  
and not to respond to attacks with violence 
themselves. Unfortunately
column179: there are no existing \ncopies of the county newspaper for this period
column180:
but 
I've gotten at the story through oral interviews. 
"I wonder if you have run across counties that 
struggled over eviction and did not take that path? 
That phenomenon seems as interesting a topic of 
exploration as the question of 'successful 
whitecapping.' A study contrasting the two patterns 
might reveal a more interesting story of class and 
ideological divisions within racial groups than 
would a study of only one pattern. Perhaps a 
conversation with old courthouse men or elderly 
black preachers in a county adjacent to an all white 
county? 
	
"I interviewed Charity Scott
column181: a black woman born in \n1911
column182: in 2001.  She remembered the event about \nas you describe it briefly below.  It was initiated
column183:
in 
her memory by an accusation of rape against a 
black man who had just been released from the 
chain gang.  The victim was a white prostitute who 
my interviewee knew personally.  A poor girl who 
would come by her parent's house for food  and 
would eat at the table of this black home.  Scott 
doubted the guilt of the accused man.  She said 
that whites were trying to run off blacks as a result.  
Her family were landowners and were protected by 
white neighbors  and warned not to shoot back if 
anyone shot into the house.  The families knew 
each other well
column184: helped each other out with work
column185:
and even dined together.  (I'm finding this very well 
hidden pattern of what I think you called 'anti 
racism' in other places as well.)  Scott said that the 
big men in the county put down the movement.  
Protection given blacks by white elites in north 
Georgia fits Fitzhugh Brundages' take on 
whitecapping in his first study on lynching.)  It's a 
lively interview.
"The second interview was with Hoyt Duncan
column186:
a 100 
year old middle sized white farmer from Banks.  He 
denied that an effort had been made to evict blacks.  
Then
column187: he said he ought to know
column188: that he'd been on \nthe jury of the murder case. \n\"One other thing.  They both seemed credible
column189: so \nI'm wondering if
column190: as they came from opposite ends \nof the county
column191:
they are each describing local 
conditions around their farms.  It seems likely.  
Duncan remembered that the brothers of the 
murdered girl were poor whites
column192: and rough \ncharacters.  He remembered that they'd come to the \ncourthouse and tried to stir up a lynching
column193: but were \ndispersed.  Maybe they went riding around Scott's \nneighborhood
column194: planning to begin a mass anti black \nmovement there?  At any rate
column195:
blacks weren't 
evicted.  There are four black churches in the 
county.  There never were really large numbers of 
blacks in Banks County
column196:
although it had one of the 
highest percentages of blacks who owned their own 
farms in the northern third of the state.  When I 
asked black residents why their neighbors or family 
members left
column197: it was always for economic motives.  \nThe county really is poor.  There's nothing to do \nthere
column198: except farm
column199: and once the cotton prices \ntanked
column200: well
column201: there really wasn't any reason at all to \nstay. \n\"This summer
column202:
I also ran into two elderly black 
brothers who claimed to have owned land along the 
southern boundary of Forsythe County.  They said 
they never had any trouble
column203:
and they do have a lot 
of money.  They had parcels of land all over from 
middle Georgia to Forsythe.  They hit the bigtime 
when a pig farm they ran in Atlanta was bought for 
housing plots.  They always had close ties to 
powerful whites.  Perhaps they are one of those 
cases in which green trumped black. Since I've 
started doing interviews in North Georgia
column204:
I've 
heard a few stories of black men who were 
threatened by mobs for stopping a truck at a truck 
stop there or some such offense to the low lifes."

column176: email 1\/8\/2008: \r\n\r\nMy late father told me on several occasions

In June 1892
column177: Bonners Ferry expelled its Chinese \npopulation. Some of the Chinese population may have \nreturned shortly after
column178: as a restaurant opened in July \nof that year advertised \"No Chinese Employed\".

column176: In December 1891
column177: white residents of Clark Fork ran \nits 22 Chinese residents out of \ntown.
Chinese people made up between one-\nquarter and on-third of Idaho's total population. Anti-\nChinese attitude peaked in 1885 and 1886
column178: often led \nby the Knights of Labor
column179:
an early labor union.

Chinese immigrants first came to Idaho as railroad and 
mine workers. Many of these workers settled 
permanently in Idaho and took other jobs. In 1882
column180: \nthe Chinese Exclusion Act was passed in Congress
column181:
which allowed the federal government to severely 
restrict Chinese immigration. The act was renewed in 
1892 and again in 1902
column182: and not repealed until 1943. \nThe 1943 immigration laws established a quota on \nChinese immigrants (105 a year)
column183:
which was not lifted 
until the 1965 Immigration Act.

In 1886
column184: a large anti-Chinese convention was held in \nBoise
column185: and violent expulsion of Chinese people spread \nthrough the state.

column176: In December 1891
column177: the town of Hoodoo attempted to \nrun out its Chinese population. Shortly before that
column178:
a 
local judge had ruled that the lease of mining land to 
Chinese Americans was invalid. he was overturned by 
the state Supreme Court in 1893 and some Chinese 
returned to the area.

Hoodoo no longer exists as a town
column179: acc. to the census.  It had a post office in the 1890s but no longer does.

column180: \"The town's policy put it on conflict with President Roosevelt's Executive Orders against discrimination in defense industries because important war plants of Olin Industries were there [Western Cartridge Company].  The company claimed it could not hire Blacks because for over 50 years the town had not permitted Blacks to live or work there.\"\r\n\r\nAccording to Ross
column181: quoting F. W. Olin

column279: but not 1978).\n\tThe post office brought in the African American postmaster
column280: probably late '70s or early '80s. He was a \"joiner\"...got into everything he could...Kiwanis and Exchange Club
column282: etc. Everyone bragged how enlightened they'd become \"since we now have a black member\". Only thing is...everyone bragging had the same black member! There were bylaws in Kiwanis and Exchange Club (maybe still are
column283: I'm not sure) against belonging to more than one like service club. But neither Kiwanis nor Exchange wanted to enforce the bylaws by not allowing the black member to belong

column285: he was the only person in town to ever have dual membership. He retired from the Goshen Post Office
column286: lived a number of years in the same house just outside city limits before dying about a year or two ago.\n\tAlso
column287: a friend of mine told me that when he wanted to buy his first home (early '70's)
column288: there was collusion between local banks and real estate people not to do mortgages for African Americans.\n\tThankfully
column289: we've grown some since those days. But it was still a struggle to pass an ordinance recently to establish a Human Relations Commission and hire a director. There was widespread support among the Mennonite Community and progressive minded people. We negotiated two months with Chamber of Commerce
column290: trying to get its endorsement. We were only able to get to a point where they would not oppose it
column291: but also would not endorse it. None of the three Republicans voted for the ordinance
column292: despite changes that were made to \"soften\" it...concentrate more on education
column294: and despite many letters that were written to them and folks packing the council chambers both nights that the ordinance was voted on.\n\tA less formal Human Relations Commission had been established in 1996
column295: but there was no ordinance providing for statutory powers. It came together as a result of urging by the mayor at that time
column296: and was a joint venture between City of Goshen
column298: and the Chamber of Commerce in reaction to a couple appearances of the Ku Klux Klan in Goshen. When the Klan came to Goshen the first time
column299: a group got together and had an alternative \"Diversity Day\" rally that day. That celebration lives on and grows each year. The second time the Klan came
column300: people organized a \"Pledge Against Prejudice. People could pledge any amount of money per minute that the Klan demonstrated. The proceeds were then split between the local Human Relations Commission to help fund \"Diversity Day\" and the Southern Poverty Law Center. Since they offered to do it every time they came
column301: I think that may have pissed the Klan off
column302: as they haven't been back since.\n\tAlso in reaction to the Klan
column304: claiming that anonymous speech need not be tolerated. The Klan showed up in robes at the City Council meeting
column305: but they weren%u2019t allowed to speak unless they followed the rules of the council and gave name\/address for the record
column306: and removed the hood to speak. Their leader complied
column307: with agreement from news media that his face would not be shown on TV. He tried to give a fictitious name
column308: but the Police Chief in the back of the room shook his head
column309: so he was asked for his real name and he gave it. His real name appeared in the newspaper
column310: and supposedly he was fired from his job. There was widespread support for the ordinance
column312: three Democrats). The Indiana Civil Liberties Union challenged the ordinance. It cost the city $50
column313: 000 in legal expenses to lose the case. Attorneys recommended not appealing the decision
column314: as they would probably lose the appeal

column183: \n%u201CI'm writing to relay the story about my trip with my parents to Greenwood Indiana (circa 1960). I recall my parents had gone to Greenwood to pick up a puppy from one of my mother's co-workers who lived there. I overheard the grownups talking about how we had to get out of town because Black people were not allowed in town after dark. I remember being terrified sitting in the back seat of our car holding my new puppy as we drove from Greenwood to Indianapolis. I believe this memory is behind my fear of driving rural highways and traveling through small rural towns (particularly in Indiana). I confirmed with my mother that the town was Greenwood Indiana. She mentioned that there was one Black family
column184: The Johnsons that lived in that town. One of the family members was also a co worker. My mother reported that the Whites in that town \"just loved\" that Black family. Apparently so
column185: because they did not come to any harm.%u201D\n

In April 2007 Huntington University wanted Huntington to declare itself an inclusive city. It did. 
 
Huntington restored an old slave house said to have been used to capture runaway slaves and transport them back to the south. September 28
column178: dedication of the Lambdin P. Milligan Slave House. Milligan was a sympathizer to the South
column179: a member of the %u201CKnights of the Golden Circle
column180: %u201D and used his house to hold slaves until he could return them back to their \"owners.\" He also attempted an armed uprising
column181: and was arrested in 1864 for treason by Federal authorities. Milligan claimed illegal imprisonment (the military didn't have the right to try him for any alleged crime while the civil courts were functioning)
column182: and was set free. Acc. to a local account
column183:
"He was welcomed back in Huntington with applause because justice was done: the wrongfully accused was set free."  
 

Huntington had 33 blacks in 1990 and 74 in 2000
column184: but that may include college students
column185: etc.  \n\tCounty had 69 blacks incl. 13 households in 2000.\n\tDuring the race riots of the 1960s
column186: and particularly following Martin Luther King's assassination
column187: the owner of a store that sold guns would get phone calls warning him of black \"motorcycle gangs\" on their way to Huntington from Ft Wayne to attack the all white town as well as his business to steal the guns.  No attack ever came but it illustrates the paranoia.\t\n\n\tOne Huntington resident accompanies students to larger cities in Indiana.  Students ask questions like
column188: will we get mugged? \"That black (or as frequently
column189: \"colored\") guy has on a red t shirt
column190: is he in a gang?\" Or

column192: is this neighborhood safe?\".  Is that guy a rapper? And just generalized stupid behavior
column193: fake ghetto accents
column194: caricaturized walks and behavior.  I am still surprised that I need to tell these kids that the term \"colored\" is considered offensive.  And several years ago

column196: I discovered that if I didn't remind them of that before we went to a \"big\" city that they would use those kinds of terms and use them loudly.  Or they would ask an African American what gang they were in.  Or just point and laugh out the bus window.  I said that I think that growing up in an all white community is detrimental for the white kids.  I believe that that kind of upbringing allows people to think of minorities as an 'other'.  It allows you to suspend your normal respect for people.  Some of these kids don't see a person walking down the street
column197: they see what amounts to a character.  They would never consider being so disrespectful to someone
column198: they just haven't thought about what they are doing as disrespectful because they are seeing a unique 'other' and not just the guy down the street. \nHuntington County had a sizable migrant community and I think that I know why Mexicans didn't count.  In Huntington
column199: they were transitory.  Hispanics only lived in the area for part of each year.  As such
column200: they didn't present a threat to change the community.  Those few families that choose to stay year round were accepted (at least a little) because they were known before they took up permanent residence.  \n\nAccording to a former Huntington resident
column201: \"Huntington was a \"sundown\" town.  If an African American family drove through
column202: since the route took them down the main street of Huntington
column203: there was always a policeman on the lookout
column204: who would stop them and tell them to get out of town now.  Yet
column205: when I was a boy (1920s-1930s) there was a black couple (husband\/wife) who were servants to William Schacht
column206: owner of a rubber plant in town.  They lived in the Schacht house but their movements were circumscribed.  They couldn't go downtown
column207: a few blocks away
column208: without problems. There was also another elderly black man living there%u2014Rags (of course)
column209: who washed windows in the downtown area.  He
column210: too

was tolerated but watched.%u201D

 %u201CIn the early 70's Marion junior high school students were denied service at a Huntington McDonalds. I was not present

An Indiana resident recalls 
%u201CMy husband
column196: and I were in the market for a home.  With him working in Franklin
column197: IN and me out here in Greencastle

column206: she told me I was more than welcome to call her with any questions or concerns and gave me her business card.  When I took out my wallet to put away her card
column207: my picture fold fell out onto the bar and opened up to a portrait of some very good friends  good friends who happen to be engaged and Japanese and African American.  She looked at the photo

column213: my husband told some folks at his plant I was viewing a house in Martinsville that day.  He was quickly informed that was a bad idea. Needless to say we built a home in Indy!!%u201D\n\n\tThere also are many articles about Carol Jenkins
column214: and the arrest of her killer 34 years later.\nSee
column215: for example
column216: Bruce C. Smith%u2019s articles in The Indianapolis Star
column217: May 19
column218: 2002:\n\t%u201CUntil recently
column219: accountant Mary Ann Land never had been outspoken about the racist reputation that she thinks is unfairly hung on her hometown of Martinsville.\n\tHowever
column220: since the arrest of a suspect in the 1968 killing of a young black woman in Martinsville
column221: Land and other natives of the nearly all white city are starting to speak out about erasing the stigma they think is unfair.  But leaders of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People don't believe the arrest cleanses the city.\n\tCarol Marie Jenkins
column222: a 21 year old black woman from Rushville
column223: was killed while selling Collier encyclopedias door to door along Morgan Street in Martinsville on Sept. 16
column224: 1968.\n\tKenneth C. Richmond
column226: who lives in an Indianapolis nursing home
column227: was arrested May 8 and charged with murder. Richmond is white.\n\tHis daughter
column228: who was 7 years old at the time of the killing
column229: came forward and told police she saw him do it.\n\tPastors are preaching that it may be the beginning of a healing process    a softening of hardened hearts. But it may not happen quickly.\n\t\"We are not at the end of the road
column230: but at a fork in the road
column231: \" said the Rev. J. Christy Wareham of the Presbyterian Church in Martinsville.\n\t\"Does racism happen in other places besides Martinsville?\" asked Land
column232: who was born and raised in Martinsville. \"Yes
column233: it does. Have there been bigots here? Yes. Are there bigots here now? Not so many. There are ignorant people and bad people everywhere. But for 33 years
column234: our community has been the focus of national attention because of this act (Jenkins' murder).\n\t\"An article in the paper today reminds me that the press won't let up. How long will we have to live like this? Probably for the rest of my life
column235: and I hate that. I don't think it's fair. If I look at the whole world
column236: I have to ask why did this happen to my hometown? Maybe it is to make this community a little stronger.\"\n\tMartinsville Mayor Shannon Buskirk said Richmond's arrest finally can bring peace and relief to the Jenkins family.\n\tBut he also declared May 8 as \"a great day for Martinsville\" because that was the day the world learned that a stranger passing through the city may have been responsible for the killing
column237: not a native.\n\tSince Richmond's arrest
column238: Buskirk's message has received worldwide media coverage.\n\tThere have been long reports on national television network news shows and National Public Radio. Radio stations from Syracuse
column239: N.Y.
column240:
to the British Broadcasting Corp. in London have made it a talk show topic.
	National newspapers including The New York Times have sent reporters to Martinsville.
	The Jenkins family has hired Sherma Wise of Media Wise Communications Group in Indianapolis to screen requests for media interviews.
	She agreed to interviews with CBS and People magazine but declined NBC's "Dateline" because she had already agreed to appear on a segment of "60 Minutes." Wise is noncommittal about book deals.
	Buskirk's and others' attempts to quell the racist legend of Martinsville don't satisfy Roderick E. Bohannan
column241: president of the Greater Indianapolis branch of the NAACP.\n\tThe NAACP sent a telegram soon after Jenkins' murder asking for a federal investigation and suggesting Jenkins' murder may have been a hate crime similar to other killings of blacks in the South.\n\t\"I saw the comments from the mayor that this arrest takes the albatross from around the city's neck because it was a transient
column242: \" Bohannan said. \"While it may be an important step in solving the crime for the family
column243: it does not solve it completely for the city.\n\t\"I know they are trying to make strides
column244: \" he added.\n\tBut there have been other episodes.\n\tIn recent years
column245: Martinsville High School was banned by the Indiana High School Athletic Association from holding home sporting events for a year after alleged racial name calling around a visiting team's bus.\n\tMartinsville Assistant Police Chief Dennis Nail wrote a controversial letter published in the local paper after the Sept. 11 attacks complaining about \"queers
column246: \" \"Buddy Buddha
column247: \" and \"Hadji Hindu.\"\n\t\"Will this arrest exonerate Martinsville? It would be nice if it would

column249: \" said high school French teacher Lynnette Liberge
column250: who led a drive that gathered hundreds of local signatures for an advertisement in rebuttal to Nail's letter.\n\t\"What I hope is that people will feel more comfortable if they have been concerned about coming to our city

column252:
but it is no different from a lot of other little towns in America."

A North Manchester resident writes
column177:
%u201CThe town for last half of Twentieth century is rather sharply divided between the college educated who have a more worldly perspective and the working class/many functionally illiterate group with a strong Kentucky influence..
	Settled by many Germans of antislavery tradition... some Quakers... a Station of the Underground in town which was not supported by everyone but anyone trying to catch escaping slave met a stone wall of resistance...No slave was ever caught after they reached N. Manchester.
	There were a few blacks in area from early times... one or two "our blacks" in town who were generally popular.
	Klan was active in 1910s and 20s.... met in town though I know of no violence in this particular area... much violence in areas not far away.. and the influence of Klan in Indiana is matter of public record.  Current activity in surrounding area... students in my classes told how they were recruited in their high schools. Many came to college with considerable fear of blacks and prejudice.
	There may have been a "sundown" sign outside of town... no easy proof... but surely that would have been the attitude for 100 years or more...but always excepting "our blacks" maybe a student who came to Manchester College or someone "sponsored"  by a respected member of the community. column202: 213.\n\n\tAn Indiana resident remembered that her mother attended Valparaiso University in Valparaiso IN.  She's always told hert that
column203: in the late 1940s
column204: two of her fellow classmates were required to carry a note from the Valparaiso mayor if they ventured off the campus after sunset.  The note gave them permission to be in town.  These classmates were African Americans.\n\tOne of the first two blacks to attend Valparaiso disputed having to use a pass
column205: but stated:  \n\t\"Initially
column206: we were scheduled to register in September
column207: 1946.  Our minister at the time
column208: the late Rev. Joseph G. Lavalais

column214: there would have to be a meeting of the city council to change that ruling.\"\n\tIn the twentieth century Valparaiso and Morgantown
column215: Indiana

Message: I graduated from Colby Community High School in 1959.  I am sure there were no signs at the city limits stating that blacks (negroes) were not allowed to stay overnight.  

I do remember that Marion Anderson was scheduled to sing in Colby with the Community Concert series
column177: but canceled when she learned she would not be allowed to stay overnight.  \n\nI also recall that Colby was a meal stop for the Greyhound bus.  Blacks were allowed to come into the restaurant and order
column178: but somehow never got their food before it was time for the bus to leave.  \n\nWe saw occasional Hispanics during harvest time -- who were working with custom-cutting crews
column179: but I think they stayed outside the city limits.

email 11\/12\/2007:\r\n\r\nIt was a pleasure and somewhat of a surprise to realize that Denton
column177: Kansas
column178: during my childhood in the 30's and 40's was not alone in its rascism.  The one hundred or so inhabitants located in northeast Kansas near St. Joseph and the Missouri River
column179: were almost pure Anglo Saxon from Lincolnshire
column180: emigrating in the 1880's.  My dad was born in Denton in 1885 and the family attended reunions annually in the summertime at the Methodist Church just across the street from my grandfather's town home.  The homestead was just outside the townsite along the Rock Island Railroad that connected St. Joseph and Topeka.  My father rode the train to Topeka and then south to Baldwin City where he attended Methodist Baker University
column181: becoming an accountant.  The Dentons converted to Methodism  in England and suffered discrimination
column182: leading to the decision by a number of brothers to migrate into the western United States where the U. S.! Cavalry had put native American Indians into reservations near Horton
column183: making valuable and rich farmland free for the taking.  Having suffered discrimination and prohibition from higher education or land ownership in England
column184: the Dentons were no more tolerant in the new country.\r\n\r\nAmerica was good to the Dentons.  My father migrated to St. Joseph where he first worked as an accountant for the meat processing plants there.   With the coming of the automobile
column185: he risked his career to work as finance officer for an automobile dealership that opened just across the street from the St. Joseph City Hall.  He stayed until retirement while siring six children
column186: always reminding us of our heritage with frequent returns to the family roots in Denton.\r\n\r\nOnce when I was in high school
column187: probably in the late 40's
column188: I became bored during the Sunday afternoon family gossip sessions
column189: and walked from the Church a block or two over to Kansas Hghway 20
column190: which connected Troy
column191: the county seat
column192: with Atchison to the south.  Not far from the highway sign identifying the town's city limits as 'DENTON  pop. 98' was another curious sign which read as I remember:  \" Do not let the sun set in this town if you are Colored\".  When I asked about it in embarrassment
column193: I was told essentially:  MYOB
column194: it is a local problem.  But as I already knew
column195: the schools were segregated in St. Joseph
column196: and the Jewish girl who had a crush on me was told she could never marry a Gentile.   While on a passenger ship to Germany where I was a Fulbright Scholar at the University of Cologne in the late 50's
column197: I met an exceeding bright and I thought beautiful Black girl also on a scholarship to Europe.  We were told to 'cool it' ! and she finally broke it off saying:  It will never work for us....   Even the  girls in Cologne at the University in a town which was nearly 100 White and Catholic shunned me as a 'foreigner' and a 'protestant'.  In Cologne
column198: however
column199: I responded to a newspaper ad offering a room for rent in a private home near the University.  The welcome was warm and familial.   Later
column200: the man of the house
column201: a 'professor' at a local 'Hoch Schule---like a Community College
column202: said he wanted me to live in the house because:  'I hate Americans
column203: who shot and wounded me in  the 1945 occupation of Cologne.  I am trying to deal with my hate because it poisons my life and limits my skill as a teacher.'  He became like another father.  A case where hate turned into love.\r\n\r\nIn those days
column204: we referred to Blacks as Colored
column205: and were not permitted to say the word Nigger
column206: or Negroe
column207: except in limericks such as:  Eenie
column208: meenie
column209: miney
column210: moe; Catch a Nigger by the toe; If he hollers
column211: make him pay; Fifty dollars every day\".  A nonsense rhyme
column212: but it lives even today.  My grandchildrn sing it as five years old
column213: substituting 'Tiger' for 'Nigger'.  Of course
column214:
the original word still rings involuntarily in my head.

The folks in Denton were not narrow in their bias.  Native Americans were considered inferior and alcoholics.   (Most of the Dentons as Methodists were abstainers and great supporters of prohibition; The women were members of WCTU)  Jews were considered immoral if not murderers for killing Jesus.  Anyone who spoke Spanish  were Catholic (Papist and Un-American) or low life farm hands who lived in barns or shacks. 

We have come a ways since the 60's
column215: but still have a long way to go.  You are helping us to understand our past so that we can deal better with our future.   In a way
column216: like my second father
column217: Mathias Weber
column218: the wounded teacher in Cologne.  Next time I am in Denton
column219: I will see if the sign has been saved as a part of their history
column220: or if it was just destroyed
column221: either by the city after my inquiry
column222: or by some one who  came by in the night and spirited it away.  I will ask if anybody remembers....\r\n

Testimony of a former resident:\n\"I DO know that Douglass
column177: Kansas
column178: still was [sundown] in 1969.  My sister and I ran afoul of it when we were out for an evening walk. A carload of young black men had gotten off the highway and gotten lost; they needed directions to get out of town and on their way to Wichita.  We politely gave directions
column179: and by the time we made it home law enforcement was already there to ask us about these men.  My dad
column180: pastor at the Christian Church
column181: actually broke the law when he invited a choir from a Wichita Baptist Church to come and sing at an evening Holy Week service--and didn't tell anyone they were black!  There was a real threat of violence that evening.... \n\tWe lived in Douglass from 1967 to 1970...I can't remember the date of the choir incident.  It seems like it was about 1969
column182: but I could be off a year or so.  Douglass was only a half hour away from Wichita
column183: so the choir did leave after the service.  I gather the threat of violence was very real.  I was in Junior High at the time
column184: so it is possible I was romanticizing the event.  But given the earlier incident which brought to police to our house
column185:
I'm pretty sure that the possibility of violence did exist.

Testimony of the former pastor:
"I was pastor to the First Christian Church (Disciples of Christ) in Douglass
column186: KS.  At that time I had gone back to Seminary to complete some graduate studies.  This story all began about 1969 when the leadership of the congregation of the First Christian Church (Disciples of Christ) of Douglass
column187: KS came up with a plan for a weekend meeting with one of the faculty of Phillips University of Enid. OK as the guest speaker.  It was planned as a meeting for Friday
column188: Saturday
column189: and Sunday evenings
column190: and including Sunday morning. \n\tThe arrangements were complete to have a faculty member of the undergraduate School of the Bible from Phillips University to be the speaker.  Plans were made for special music from the congregation. \n\tI knew that the Black Congregation in Wichita
column191: KS North Heights Christian Church had a program going where they would do a concert of \"black music\" as guests of a congregation on the basis that a special offering would be received that would go to the \"organ fund\" of the North Heights church.  I went to the persons who were planning for the event and suggested that we might invite the choir to sing for us on Sunday evening
column192: which was the closing service for these meetings. \n\tThe male member of the congregation who was arranging for the music called the pastor with an inquiry and asked if the choir from North Heights church would be free and willing to come to Douglass
column193: KS to do a concert and we would be more than glad to receive the special offering for their funds.  The date had been shared.  It was intended as an inquiry to gather information.  This phone call had been made a few months in advance of the special meetings. \n\tHe let me know early on that the pastor had responded that they would be glad to come to Douglass for the meeting.  Later he visited with one of the other men of the church
column194: a man who had been a member of the Douglass community for most of his life.  The man responded with something like
column195: \"You cannot have the choir at the church for this service that is being planned.  For many years Douglass has had an ordinance stating that
column196: 'No black person (probably nigger) can remain in the city limits after the sun goes down.'  This would be real trouble!\"\n\tBut it is important to bring to mind that the man who made the original call did not call the pastor from Wichita back and express a change in plans.  Neither did he confirm the arrangements.  Time went by.  Spring arrived.  The meetings began on Friday night.  We had pretty good attendance.  The Sunday morning service went well also. \n\tIn reflecting back on the events from that point on we now know that members of the church
column197: and the choir
column198: arrived in Douglass by buss and in cars in the latter part of the afternoon
column199: some time before the meeting was to begin.  The sun was still up. \n\tThey then decided to drive around town to look the city over.  They drove through the streets
column200: both business and residential areas.  They drove slowly
column201:
looking at the homes.
	I heard later that the phones in town had begun to ring.  It must have been like a really busy phone company.  Some people passed on the message that a group of black people had arrived in town to look for places to live.  At that time there were no black persons living in the city at all.  Douglass was only about 20 to 25 miles out of Wichita and was a bedroom community for persons who worked in Wichita. 
	I went to the church early as I always did when special meetings were being held.  One always needs to be sure that everything is ready.  Before long there were lots of people arriving early.  They were coming in large numbers early.  It happened that they were black.  It seems that the pastor had put that engagement on his calendar and since no one had called to have him erase that date they assumed that it was a go. 
	Some of them very large black persons.  They came in
column202: used the rest rooms
column203: and wandered all over the church building exploring each corner bubbling with excitement.  They rehearsed a little. They greeted persons who were coming in from the community.\n\tThey did the concert.  They enhanced the evening meeting.  And we had plans for refreshments after the meeting
column204: with dialogue with the guest speaker.  The visitors from Wichita stayed for the after session.  It was bad enough for some that they had been there for the regular service.  But there were a few persons who were really angry that they did not leave after the service
column205: and GO HOME!  During the dialogue there were a few young men who expressed concern about the difficulties that rise for racial minorities. \n\tI escaped the heavy axe of anger because I had not made the original phone call.  I had not been a part of the arrangements.  I had made the suggestion to the persons making arrangements.  But they had failed to use proper procedure to complete the arrangements.\" \n

I grew up in the Mennonite town of Hillsboro
column177: Kansas. A story has circulated that Hillsboro was a \"sundown town.\" Yet
column178: unless we have missed it
column179: you make no mention of that in your book. \n\nThe story is that until Hannington Pamba (Congolese) came as a student (1950s) recruited by a Mennonite Brethren missionary to come play soccer (football) at Tabor College
column180: no blacks could spend the night in the town. \n\nAlso
column181: it is my understanding is that in the early 1920s Hillsboro regulations insisted that African-Americans could not be lodged overnight in Hillsboro. The issue came to a head because the foreman and\/or some of the crew of our road paving project was\/were African American. The downtown streets were laid with brick at this time
column182: a very laborious time-intensive job
column183: 1922; you can still see some of our brick-paved streets. \n\nIn Hillsboro
column184: Kansas lived  a couple who could have no kids. So
column185: they adopted two Korean girls. Well
column186: they turned out to be beautiful girls--but one especially was very dark. When they got older things became messy. I remember Alden coming over and talking to my father about how Mennonite fathers and others in the community were telling him and his wife not to let their sons date his daughters as it was unchristian for races to be mixed. I remember Alden's disgust--\"If your sons want to date my daughters it is not my fault. That is not my issue. You have the problem.\"\n

column205: smiling defiantly

column207: why don't you play track 5 now?'  Jake blanched and looked down and said nothing.\"   \n\tStudents did not go to Club Insomnia

column214: ' she said.  'I think I would enjoy going to South America.  It would be real cool to see a different way of life.'  For many students

column176: Message: 01\/07\/2008\nI lived in a town called Oberlin Kansas. I don't believe that it was a technical sundown town
column177: but there was a family of migrant farmers who had car trouble. My dad got the Spanish teacher so they could\ncommunicate. During this time the Under Sheriff came by
column178: and told them to be out of town by sundown. He was also an arrogant jerk. I don't believe that it was a technical sundown town. My dad also lived around this town during the 60's. I think the main reason there were no black people in that town was that the economy of Oberlin was really fading. The main source of income was farming. The towns that had a better economy and non farming jobs. I also had a friend that was a Highway Patrol officer
column179: and a classmate's father was a part of the city police. They didn't share those beliefs. Most people that graduated from H.S. did not stay there. I think it was mainly just a bigoted under sheriff.

column182:
KS (Cheyenne County) on US 36.  It was probably about 12x15 feet and I do not remember seeing any sponser's name on it.  I can remember that it looked as if it had been there some time.  I wonder who took it down and if it might be in someone's barn somewhere.  I remember it because it shocked me to see something like that. 
At that time I am sure there weren't any black people living in St Francis.  I can remember seeing a black guy in the back seat of a car there.  He was on the floor in the backseat and quickly peeked up when we passed the car he was riding in.  I remember the look of terror in his eyes when he saw that I noticed him.  I always have wondered where he was going and why he was there in that dusty little town.   
     I have a lot of relatives who still live in 'Saint'. I was probably around 13 at the time which would have put the date at 1955. I believe that most of my older relatives (my mom's side of the family)living in that area were racists since I can recall some of the comments that they made. Those comments were common at that time and these people were somewhat uneducated farm people so it is understandable (to me anyway)..."

column176: Orville Hubbard
column177: former mayor of Dearborn
column178: was vocal and well-known in his successful attempts to keep Dearborn \"clean\"
column179: meaning \"white\". In a 1956 interview with an Alabama newspaper Hubbard stated \"They can't get in here. Every time we hear of a Negro moving in... we respond quicker than you do to a fire.\" A 1956 US News & World Report article reported that the city shut off the gas of a black man who moved to Dearborn and refused to collect his garbage.\n\nBlacks were allowed to reside in Dearborn as live-in domestic workers
column180:
but not as independent 
households. Dearborn's black population through 1970 is mostly female.

In the mid 1960s
column181: the NAACP staged a series of fair housing marches in suburban Detroit
column182:
with the first march taking place in Dearborn. Marchers were pelted 
with rotten eggs and rocks and called numerous racial slurs.

In 1985
column183:
Dearborn passed an ordinace limiting use of the public parks to Dearborn residents only. This was widely seen as a measure to keep the parks all white.

"When Dearborn came out with the resident only law I was perturbed. I didn't live in Detroit nor am I an African American. Everyone knew what the rule really meant though."I came home from work one evening and caught the mayor of Dearborn on TV reading a statement as too what the ordinance meant. He said the rule was not made to keep blacks nor just the people of Detroit 
out. That the rule was not discriminatory but was also made to keep people of other cities out also like people from 'Taylor'."He said Taylor specifically and solely. Which did really upset me. I'm from Taylor... Taylor is a working class neighborhood. It does have an African American population but largely is a white city. It is made up of mostly small home once called starter homes. It does have Federally subsidized apartments... I believe this is the reason Dearborn wished to keep us out of their parks."
-former Taylor

column176:
The Detroit metro area has the highest level of 
residential racial segregation in the county. Nearly 
9 out of 10 black residents of the area live in 
Detroit proper
column177: Highland Park
column178: Inkster
column179: Pontiac
column180:
or 
Southfield. The level of residential integration has 
actually fallen since 1980.

Detroit is highly segregated even in contrast with 
other metro areas with large black populations in 
the central cities. 75% of Detroit metro area blacks 
live in the city proper
column181: compared with 22% in the \nAtlanta metro area
column182: 27% in the Washington
column183: D.C. \nmetro area
column184: and 60% in the Baltimore metro area.\n\nAfter the passage of the Fugitive Slave Act in 1850
column185: \nDetroit and other towns in Michigan became \nfrequently used stops on the Underground Railroad
column186: \ngiven the state's proximity to Canada. Detroit \nschools integrated in 1869. By the 20th century
column187:
racial egalitarianism had faded in Michigan. Henry 
Ford deliberately housed his workers in segregated 
in segregated neighborhoods of Detroit and other 
towns in the area and allowed blacks to do only the 
most dangerous and physically demanding jobs in 
his plants. In the 1920s
column188: Michigan had more Ku \nKlux Klan members than any other state. The peak \nmembership was about 875
column189: 000.\n\nIn the 1968 and 1972 presidential elections
column190:
segregationist George Wallace won every white 
ward in the Detroit area. He also won the state's 
Democratic primary
column191: with 51% of the vote. \n\nIn the 1970s
column192: Detroit lost a $500 million grant for \npublic transit development
column193:
because the suburbs 
refused to cooperate.

In 2006
column194:
the National Fair Housing Alliance 
conducted extensive tests to determine if Detroit 
area realtors were steering home buyers based on 
race. 145 pairs
column195: consisting of one white and one \nnon-white pair
column196: attempted to buy homes through \n73 real estate offices in 12 areas of the metro. As \nreported in the Detroit Free Press
column197:
the study 
concluded that 87% of the pairs were steered. White 
testers attempting to move into interracial 
neighborhoods were told the schools were bad
column198:
but 
black or Latino pairs were told the same school 
were good. Non-white testers were shown less 
homes than white testers.
In each case
column199: the black or Latino testers had a \nhigher income
column200: lower debt
column201: and more money for a \ndown payment. Despite this
column202: many were denied \nservice outright
column203:
and others were asked to provide a 
pre-approval letter from a financial institution or 
other financial information. White testers were not 
asked for this information.
One couple
column204: who was not a part of the test but was \nfeatured in the Detroit Free Press article about the \nstudy
column205:
reported that their real estate agent 
repeatedly refused to show them houses in the 
exclusive Grosse Pointes and instead urged them to 
move to interracial Harper Woods.

column176:
The city Grosse Pointe is one of the five Grosse 
Pointes that comprise one of the most exclusive 
suburban areas in the country.

While Grosse Point appears to have a black 
population through the 1980s
column177: these are all live-in \ndomestic employees. In 1940
column178: for example
column179: only 26 of the 140 black people are male. The decline in black population from 1940 to 1980 represents the decline in live-in domestic workers in America
column180:
rather than an increased whitening of Grosse Pointe.

The Grosse Pointe area was well-known for the 
point system
column181: in which those deemed undesirable (non-Protestants
column182: Eastern Europeans
column183: etc.) had to amass more points in order to purchase a home. Realtors and the Grosse Pointe Property Owner's Association would sometimes hire private detectives
column184: who would find answers to various questions
column185: including \"Appearances - swarthy
column186: slightly swarthy
column187: or not at all?\" and \"Accents - pronounced
column188: medium
column189: slight
column190: not at all?\" The maximum score for the survey was 100
column191: with most prospective residents needing a score of 50.\n\nHowever
column192: according to Michigan Attorney General Paul Adams
column193: \"a Pole is expected to have five additional points. An Italian
column194: Yugoslav
column195: Greek
column196: Syrian
column197: Lebanese
column198: Armenian
column199: Maltese
column200: Rumanian
column201: or other southern European is required to have 15 additional points. A Jew is required to have 35 additional points and his points are more difficult to achieve because of penalties in a special marking system for Jews. Orientals and Negroes are not considered at all.\"\n\nIn 1960
column202: William E. Bufalino Jr.
column203: a Jewish attorney \nfrom Detroit
column204: sued the city for libel because he was branded \"swarthy\" and denied a home. The state of Michigan ordered the suburb to abandon the system within 30 days. However
column205: there is some indication that it persisted officially until 1961
column206: and possibly unofficially after that.\n\n\"'The most desirable neighborhood for the raising of children
column207: according to these Grosse Pointe real estate dealers and brokers
column208: ' Rabbi [Leon] Fram said
column209: 'is one in which the children shall never see a Negro except in the role of a porter or a shoe shine boy
column210: never encounter any human being who believes in a faith other than Christianity
column211: never hear a foreign accent.'  He quoted the minister of a Grosse Pointe church as stating that 'Jesus Christ could never \nqualify for residence in Grosse Pointe.'\"\n-\"Klan Standards Prevail in G.P.
column212: Rabbi Charges\"
column213: \nDetroit News
column214: 14 May 1960\n\nThe suburb's first black family
column215: which moved in in \n1966
column216:
had to have a white friend buy a home and 
then sell it directly to the family. Members of the 
Open Housing Committee helped ensure the 
family's safety by staying in their home. Another 
black family moved in shortly after to a warmer 
welcome. However
column217: within a year
column218: both families had left the Detroit area. In 1968
column219: Martin Luther King
column220: Jr.
column221: spoke at a Grosse Pointe high school and was frequently interrupted by hecklers.\n\nIn 1983
column222: the tennis team including two African \nAmerican girls from Holy Name Catholic School in Birmingham
column223: MI
column224: itself an incredibly white suburb
column225: visited Grosse Pointe to compete in a match. The Grosse Pointe coach informed the Birmingham coach that Grosse Pointe could not play that day. Asked why
column226: the coach said \"you have two niggers with you.\" After an argument
column227: the Birmingham team was finally allowed to play
column228:
which may have been the first time blacks were allowed to use the Grosse 
Pointe Tennis Club.

I grew up in Royal Oak 1941-67.  I heard two stories -- The first story is there was a law which stated any Negroes on the streets after sundown would be arrested.

The second story
column177: in the 1950s there was one black family on 13 Mile Road who were not allowed to go to Royal Oak Schools

20 of Royal Oak's 24 blacks in 1960 were female
column179: indicating that this population consists mostly or entirely of live-in domestic workers.\n\nA prominent leader in the Macomb County
column180: Michigan chapter of the NAACP shared with us that in the early 1950's
column181: there was a 5:00 p.m. curfew for African Americans to leave town.  At the time
column182: her father worked for a construction company and an incident occurred where he was stuck and buried in a cave-in while working in Royal Oak.  His co-workers were aware of the curfew and informed one another \"you know he has to be outta town by 5:00 p.m.\"  The man's daughter expressed that the co-workers were \"more concerned with getting him out of town by 5:00 than helping him out of a dangerous cave.\"  As a result
column183: the man never returned to that particular workplace.  \n\nA Michigan native emailed us:\"People of all races come to various events in Royal Oak
column184: dine there
column185: and go to the many bars and nightclubs there.  As a black person
column186: I feel little to no danger of racism when in Royal Oak
column187: even late at night.  As Southfield has become more African-American
column188:
the suburbs that touch it have become more accustomed to dealing with other races."

column176: In the 1850s
column177: Hezekiah Smith
column178: a former slave founded a black colony in Spring Lake. According to a May 4th
column179: 1915 article in the Grand Haven Daily Tribune \"the negroes soon aroused the enmity of these people by making raids on their coops and their crops. The companies suffered through the disappearance of lumber... The blacks were sometimes caught in their \ndepredations and summarily punished without resort to the court.... But still the stealing went on and every once in a while one of the men would announce the loss of another chicken. The situation was really desperate.\" White residents decided to dress up in old army uniforms and inform the black residents (\"the lazy negroes
column180: who were taking their morning bask in the warm sunshine\") that they had to leave.\"Starting up like frightened sheep as soon as they saw McCarthy and the others coming
column181: the negroes made a dash for the home of Hezekiah Smith...\" The white \nvigilantes gave him the document they had prepared
column182: but he could not read. When the leader of the mob read it
column183: the blacks \"started to cry and moan... All that day the negroes could be seen packing their belongings into sacks and boxes

column176: \tI am writing from Itasca County
column177: MN
column178: which contains a piece of Minnesota's Mesabi iron range.  In 1905
column179: John C. Greenway
column180: who at the time was well known due to his status as former \"Rough Rider\" and his close personal connection with Theodore Roosevelt
column181: became General Superintendent of the Canisteo mining district.  Not impressed with the dirty little town of Bovey
column182: Greenway decided to build a \"model town
column183: \" which he named Coleraine in honor of Oliver Mining Company president Thomas Cole. At any rate
column184: Coleraine was Greenway's town.  Though his paternalism was said to be \"soft
column185: \" he was in complete control
column186: and no one could live in Coleraine without his approval.  And Greenway was determined that only the \"best classes\" of people should live in Coleraine; this of course meant native stock Americans or those of western European origin.  Just a few miles to the east of Coleraine
column187: the Mesabi took on a decidedly more ethnic flavor
column188: with numerous Serbs
column189: Finns
column190: and others
column191: but Coleraine was known as an \"American\" town.\n\tToday
column192: of course
column193: the process of assimilation has erased many distinctions formerly considered to be of great import.  Yet
column194: a vestige remains.  There is a segregated cemetery overlooking Trout Lake near Coleraine.  One half of the cemetery contains lots of typical \"American\" names
column195: but off to one side is a section quite distinct.  Here are found all the \"iches;\" that is
column196: all the Serbs
column197: whose names often ended in \"ich.\"  Segregation persists even after death.  \n\n \tGlad to hear that historical events in Itasca County may be of interest to you. I will send anything I find regarding blacks
column198: though I suspect that since there were (and still are) virtually no blacks here
column199: the issue of black employment may never have arisen.\t\n \tAs far as slavs and others of eastern European origin
column200: what took place was a pattern of sometimes subtle and sometimes more overt discrimination.  Of course
column201: such discrimination was not codified in the laws
column202: but the reason I thought of Greenway immediately is because
column203: as General Superintendent of the Canisteo
column204: he WAS the law in Coleraine to a very large extent.\t\n \tMuch of what I know about the situation in Coleraine comes from a book written by retired history professor who wrote a book in the 1970s titled _\nJohn C. Greenway and the Opening of the Western Mesabi_.  In this book
column205: he documents the racial attitudes of Greenway
column206: though that is not his major focus.  Here is an interesting quote:\t\n \t\"Because of Greenway's selection process
column207: Coleraine also had an ethnic composition which set the town apart from others on the Mesabi Range.  Greenway reflected a typical turn of the century American middle class racial bias.\"  He goes on to demonstrate Greenway's racial attitudes by refering to events in his life in which such views become explicit.\t\n \tHe also cites the diary of a man by the name of W.L.O. Bartlett
column208: who lived in Coleraine during the Greenway years.  The following excerpt is significant:\t\n \t\"About August 3
column210: I arrived at Coleraine...Previously in July
column211: I had visited Hibbing
column212: Virginia
column213: and other towns with George Mitchelson
column214: leaf tobacco man from St. Paul.  At that time I thought the range was so foreign that I classed it as out of the U.S.A.  However
column215: there were lots of good Americans scattered thru the towns
column216: and I decided to start a cigar factory in Coleraine about September
column217: 1908.  In the spring I opened a retail as well and did fairly well.  This was in the iron mining pioneering of this district.  Mines were just starting and things were booming some...Bovey joins on to Coleraine and was and is somewhat foreign.  Coleraine was and is to this day the only really U.S.A. citizen town on all the Range.\"\t\n \tAs an aside that may be relevant insofar as it further demonstrates the prevalence of anti-slav and anti-immigrant attitudes in this region
column218: I have done limited research regarding the impact on Itasca County of the great Mesabi strikes of 1907 (organized by the Western Federation of Miners) and 1916 (organized by the IWW).  It is interesting to read editorials in the Grand Rapids newspapers in which again and again it is stated that the strike on the eastern Mesabi was possible only because IWW agitators were able to stir up the uneducated immigrants who made up much of the work force and who were unfamiliar with American ways.\n---\n\tIn regard to Coleraine.  When John Greenway arrived here in 1905 to take up the enormous challenge of opening mines in the wilderness of the western Mesabi he found the newly developed little town of Bovey then boasting 26 saloons with a population of 500
column219: open prostitution
column220: wide open gambling and unsanitary conditions leading to such as typhoid.  Needing to attract reliable family men to work in his upcoming large operation he decided on the need to build a new town. As he thought that through he hit on the idea of a model town
column221: something new to the steel corporation and certainly new to Greenway.\nThe plans he developed called for rigid zoning requirements
column222: wide streets
column223: curbs
column224: boulevards
column225: lots three times the size of those in adjacent Bovey
column226: brick buildings in the downtown area and free lots for churches.  The Oliver Iron Mining Company (Greenway's employer) backed by the steel corporation built houses
column227: varied architecture at Greenway's insistence
column228: they could be purchased or rented at rates affordable by working men and even grass and flower seed was supplied. Greenway had full control over the town essentially dominating all aspects of the place.  In this model town Greenway wanted model citizens. One had to apply to establish a business or to build a home or inhabit one of those for rent or sale. Greenway's papers (in the AHS in Tucson) include correspondence in regard to the selection process:  \"If you desire to purchase lots
column229: you should forward us letters of endorsement
column230: as we do not plan to sell lots to anyone other than those who can come well recommended and who will make good citizens.  If you are interested to this extent
column231: kindly forward us some information that will let us know who you are and the reputation you bear in the community in which you live.\"  (signed Greenway) So
column232: who would be in Coleraine was in the hands of Greenway. Thirty years ago I searched his papers for clues as to his racial attitudes and didn't find much.  He referred to a local as \"a good Jew.\" After the battle of San Juan Hill where his close lifelong friend TR lamented he wanted to be the first officer to the top but Greenway had longer legs and outran him
column233: Greenway took a Spanish prisoner and was leading him around as a kind of trophy until the fellow was forcefully taken from him. Greenway once referred to Cleopatra as attractive but \"of the wrong color.\"  Not having to offer explanation as to who was allowed in Coleraine and who not
column234: Greenway could choose as he saw fit. (He was a Southerner with relatives who had fought for the Confederacy
column235: a graduate of Yale where he played a significant athletic role
column236: a hero of the Spanish American War
column237: one who shared strongly in the ideology of TR
column238: they desperately tried to resurrect the Rough Riders in WW II
column239: and he went on from Minnesota to accomplish great feats in copper mining in Arizona and today resides next to Daniel Webster in statuary hall in Washington

column242: a list of inhabitants living in Coleraine in l908 shows a large number of \"sons\" but few \"ichs\" or \"ics\" as there were in neighboring Bovey and elsewhere on the Range.  The cemetery the two towns shared originally had a Bovey section where the stones are sometimes in Cyrillic script and show Eastern European names and a Coleraine section reflecting the population there.  The Oliver kept records in regard to its employees and they indicate a higher percentage of American born and northern Europeans in the district where Greenway served as superintendent than elsewhere on the Range and it can be assumed more of them lived in Coleraine than in the other towns in the district.  The All American town designation comes from the diary of a 60 year resident who in 1931

Its members shall be white male persons over 21 who are residents of Coleraine or the vicinity.\"\n   Bovey and Coleraine remain side by side but efforts in the way of sharing services or combining have not amounted to much; the two towns remain quite different from each other.  \n\t>Sounds like you cannot say that  Greenway managed to keep out ALL Slavs

column250: etc.  Indeed
column251: he might have happily let in a few with the right recommendations?\n\nI would say that is true.  So many of those arriving on the Range were illiterate and unskilled and what Greenway really needed at the core of his operation which became one of the biggest mining efforts in the world at that time were skilled workers who were dependable and I think his town was designed more with them in mind than in racial discrimination. He did support an Americanization program at the local school and all foreign newcomers were welcome.  He was the consummate master of paternalism and unlike other aloof and hard boiled Range superintendents he won the loyalty of his work force and his district was the only one to remain free of the early Western Federation of Miners efforts to organize (partly by force though

I think Greenway 'complacently' thought he had done well to bring to Coleraine the composition it achieved under his direction.\n\t> Can you say that he kept out all blacks?  Maybe of course none applied? > (Although I am struck that in WI

column176: We received an email from a source familiar with the Twin Cities metro neighborhoods: \"Presently racial exclusion in Edina is achieved primarily through segregation by income
column177: a \"more acceptable\" and certainly more covert means than city ordinance.  This of course \"appears\" to create a social stratification
column178: but in essence also creates a racial one.  Even the shopping scene is socially\/racially exclusive in that \"commoners\" cannot afford to shop at the local shops and groceries (one of which has carpeted aisles!).  There is of course Black exclusion.  However the Minneapolis\/St. Paul area is home to a large community of Native Americans as well as many Somalis
column179: Ethiopians and Hmong
column180: all of which are excluded from the confines of Edina.\"\n\nAccording to a former resident
column181: \"I once saw the ordinance while doing research for a civics class.  The ordinance went something like this: \"No colored person shall be on the streets of Edina from after sundown until dawn unless going straight to or coming straight home from servant's quarters.\"  At the time (early 1970's)
column182: it was still on the books.  Once
column183: in the late 1960's
column184: there was a neighborhood meeting held at our house.  The subject was the African American doctor and his family who wanted to purchase a home in the neighborhood.  The comments from my neighbors were appalling.  They of course mentioned that property values would go down if this was allowed
column185: never mind that the guy was a physician.  Many of the neighbors mentioned that they moved to Edina specifically to avoid the 'coloreds' and to avoid having mixed race grandkids.\"\n\nA longtime resident said Edina's sports teams and their fans would throw food stamps at visiting players from poorer towns. At a meeting addressing concerns about a new housing development being built in Morningside
column186: a suburb of Edina
column187: he asked if there was any city mandate requiring affordable housing to be built. The other people at the meeting went silent at this question and many were visibly upset. This resident also recalls a time in the late 1960s when a friend's parents bought a house and read documents stating that the house could not be resold to Jews.\n\nMany residents joke that Edina stands for \"Every Day I Need Attention\" and is known for its refusal to build affordable housing to prevent low income residents.

Every time a small crime was committed the Police would knock on our door first because they knew it \"had\" to be my brother-in-law.  When they caught the guilty person there was never an apology.  My niece was treated poorly in school

We are looking at the 3rd generation with little change in the community.\"

It was antislavery and became the only real Republican stronghold in the state outside of St. Louis.\n\tWithout getting to deeply into its rather interesting Civil War history...Napoleonic veterans and all...the town also witnessed a very gutsy defense organized by its schoolteachers (even the militia had been ordered out) against a Confederate division in 1864.\n\tThis continued into the 1870s

I remember that my parents expressed open shock and disgust that a group of local Christian white men

My father who had fought in World War II felt that the white men's behavior was particularly cowardly because they terrorized an innocent family in order to make themselves feel important and powerful.  There was no danger and no reason whatsoever to do what they had done.\"\n\n

column176: \tMonett's whiteness is tied to violence in 1894
column177: according to MO Southern State College source.\n\n\tAs Pierce City declined

column179: and perhaps success bred emulation.  In 1894
column180: Monett started the chain of racial violence in Southwest Missouri.  In that year
column181: a black man named Ulysses Hayden was lynched for the murder of a young white man.  Hayden was taken from police custody and hanged from a telephone pole
column182: despite the probability of his innocence in the crime.  After the lynching
column183: all blacks were forced to leave Monett.   Monett%u2019s prosperity despite the absence of a black community likely contributed to the racial animosity present in Pierce City.\n[Murray Bishoff %u201CMonett%u2019s Darkest Hour: The Lynching of June 28
column185: %u201D Monett Times
column186:
27-28 June 1994.]
     
    "All the people in Monett had managed to forget about it.  Even the oldest resident."

column177: MO in a small farming town named Smithville.  With the history of the town

column179: there were no people of color
column180: no Jewish
column181: no Asians.  Just white protestants and Catholics.  There was one black student who graduated in 1979
column182: but I would have had no idea because I was only a year old at that time.  There would be a black student here or there but they would stay for one day before moving on to the next town north.  Most people escaping the city would move as far north as Plattsburg because they would not survive in Smithville.   I graduated high school in 1996 and there were only whites in the school district.  It was the year after I graduated that a set of black students came to Smithville High School and actually stayed.  They were either Sophomore or Junior transfers
column183: but I don't remember since I was in college.  Once Marcus Allen (the football player) moved to Smithville

column187: heritage and religion (although I still don't believe that there are any Jewish people).   Hope it helps.\"

From a conversation with a former resident: 
	In 1930 in Ste. Genevieve
column184: three blacks from the Deep South (two men
column185: one woman) apparently killed two white lime kiln workers.  \n\tSt. G. was the oldest town in MO
column186: founded 1750 by the French. It had blacks from its onset
column187: as slaves
column188: also free blacks
column189: including interracial (with French
column190: Spanish) and Catholic blacks.  There were newer migrants after c.1918
column191: a separate group.\n\tEither the three blacks were trying to rob the whites or the murders followed a fist fight after the men made advances toward the black woman.\n\tWhites ordered all blacks to leave.  Three families did not.  Two were sheltered by a Catholic priest.  At one household of three brothers and a sister
column192: the brother being a rural postal carrier
column193: the white mob appeared and tried to force him out.  The sheriff intervened and arrested the mob leaders
column194: but they got off with fines.  Then the U.S. intervened
column195: because they were postal workers.\n\tSoon whites invited some of the older-resident blacks to come back
column196: and some of them came.  But St. G.
column197: which had c.200 blacks in 1930
column198: had just 16 in 1960. \n       Technically
column199: Ste. Genevieve was not a %u201Csundown town
column200: %u201D although my high school history teacher once told the class that in the early 1950s the town supposedly had a sign on its outskirts reading something to the effect
column201: %u201CNigger
column202: Don%u2019t Let the Sun Set On You Here.%u201D  But I haven%u2019t been able to confirm this bit of local legend.  In any event
column203: Ste. Genevieve witnessed a race riot in October 1930 that resulted in an almost complete exodus of black residents from the town.  Eventually
column204: Ste. Genevieve civic leaders invited around seventy of those residents (described in the newspaper accounts as %u201Ccertain native property owning blacks%u201D) to return to their homes and
column205: as far as can be determined
column206: almost all of them did come back to town.  But over the course of the next three decades
column207: the African American population in Ste. Genevieve dwindled.  By 1960
column208: only sixteen black residents remained in the town.  Recently
column209: however
column210: the black population of Ste. Genevieve County has experienced a comeback.  According to the 2000 Census
column211: the number of African Americans living in Ste. Genevieve County jumped from 45 in 1990 to 128 in 2000
column212: and personal observation from my visits back to town
column213: suggest that the city of Ste. Genevieve has a larger black population than when I was growing up there
column214: but I don%u2019t have any exact figures on this. \n\t...As I recall during the 1970s only one extended black family lived in the town.  This is my recollection as a kid
column215: and I haven't check the official census data to verify this.  Although Ste. Genevieve had a token black presense
column216: my hunch is that in all other respects it may well have been a sundown town...  \n       The extended family
column217: which still resides in Ste. Genevieve
column218: includes two of the sons of the church custodian who
column219: according to legend
column220: was protected by the local Catholic pastor during the 1930 race riot.  As I recall
column221: growing up in Ste. Genevieve in the 1970s
column222: this was the only African American family living in the town
column223: but I'm certain that if you looked at the 1970 and 1980 census
column224: you would find more black residents living there than I remember.\n\n

column176: email 2\/2008\ni'm a university of missouri senior journalism student. i'm working on a writing project about a time when the kansas city monarchs negro leagues baseball team came to my hometown in southwest missouri

column191:
and wondered if you had any insight on this.

thanks for any advice you can give me on this. please feel free to call or just shoot me back an e-mail if you have time.
