column176: Famous black town in Bolivar County.  Founded by Isaiah T. Montgomery.  Many books written about it. I (Loewen) lived in Mound Bayou in 1969. So did a few other whites. It was not a sundown town (vis-a-vis whites) then.\nSo far as I know
column177: it had no tradition of keeping whites out
column178: although in the 1920s
column179: etc.
column180: probably none lived there.\nMound Bayou ran its own voting polls
column181: its own precinct. In a travesty of democracy
column182: after its votes were reported to the county seat
column183: Cleveland
column184: they would be written in red ink or pencil. All the [white] candidates had agreed beforehand that only vote totals entered in black would be summed and would determine the countywide winners or be reported on to the state. I personally saw this and took time to sum up the votes in some election(s) in the early 1960s.\nEven worse
column185: vote totals in Mound Bayou (and in the only other black-run precinct
column186: which was Prentiss Institute
column187: I believe
column188: in far SW Hinds County) would be reported to the press. Then whatever candidate for governor had carried the two black precincts would have that fact hung around his neck like an albatross. Often it caused him to go around the state during the 3-week period between the first primary and the primary runoff
column189: being TWICE as racist as the other candidate
column190: trying haplessly to remove the stigma of \"nigger-lover\" that his opponent would hang on him
column191: based on his success in Mound Bayou. Sigh!\n\n

But they imbibed a form of racism based on not 
having much if any  normal contact with blacks in their

were the stories of sex and mayhem

column181: however

column183: reflective of the absence of blacks in their \ndaily lives. But when they did
column184: they were likely to \nexpress fear and loathing
column185: and to use the 'N' word \nfreely.\"

email 11\/25\/07\r\nThis town is absolutely unreal.  Everything seemed to be fine at first
column177: when we bought it and visited
column178: and when I came to do renovations last winter by myself.  We \"settled\" here this summer
column179:
and all was well until they "found out" (their terminology) that my wife and kids are Jewish.

Members of the city council have in my presence referred to us as "fucking Jews" and they liked the idea of us buying this church until they found out that "a bunch of Jews were 'gonna buy it."  

My daughter was picked on so bad in school that her hair started falling out
column180: and she's starting to attend
column181: of all things
column182: the Catholic school tomorrow.  She was being told things like \"you don't really believe in God\" etc.  The last time I spoke with the principal of the school I explained that we had to actually have a talk with her about suicide
column183: and he glibly said \"oh that's too bad.\"\r\n\r\nAnd it's not just us.  We've been told a lot how the \"Black Family\" was treated so well when they lived here
column184: though nobody knows their names other than \"the Black Family\" and that they were only here for nine months.  \r\n\r\nThis fall a Latino family moved in
column185: and the father didn't speak English.  His 12 year old daughter had to translate hate mail from English to Spanish to her father
column186: and they left after only five or six weeks.\r\n\r\nWe had a contractor working on the church until August
column187: when they quit for reasons we weren't aware of
column188: and then we found out it's because the rumor was \"They're cheap and won't pay.\"  \r\n\r\nMy son has autism
column189: and on Halloween my daughter went trick or treating
column190: and took along an extra bag for her brother
column191: and someone in this town thought it would be amusing to not give him candy
column192: but a half eaten greasy sausage.\r\n\r\nWe get tailgated when we pull into town
column193: and the other day my wife was followed for miles
column194: and we don't know who it was.\r\n\r\nAfter we move
column195: I think Wynot would be a definite addition to the list.\r\n\r\nThe town also \"has a siren that goes off at 7AM
column196: Noon
column197: and 6PM.  As a matter of fact
column198: all the other little towns around here
column199: except for Hartington
column200: has a 6PM siren.  Someone told me it's because the farmers need to know what time to stop working
column201: another person told me they had no idea why there's a siren.\"\r\n\r\nI was reading some of your future research questions and had some ideas I wanted to share with you about this town (and sorry for 'jumping the gun' on the response).  Wynot (in Cedar County
column202: Nebraska) has old newspaper articles accusing people of being \"unAmerican\" for not being able to speak either German or English
column203: and I believe this was from a story from 1913.\r\n\r\nWhen we first moved here I got lots of anecdotes from people about \"being made fun of\" for being Danish
column204: rather than German
column205: which the town overwhelmingly claims as first heritage.  (This is somewhat an address to the homogeneity question.)  When someone first told me this
column206: I thought they were exaggerating
column207: and likened it in my own mind of a White person saying they were a \"victim of racism\" because \"The Black lady at Burger King was mean to me.\"  \r\n\r\nI've since found out that this town in its past has redlined Irish people
column208: though I can't find anything documented.  \r\n\r\nI did indeed ask about the sirens.  The very first thing I was told back in January when I came up on a \"renovation vacation\" was that it was for my \"safety\" and I assumed it meant tornadoes
column209: thinking it was testing.  THEN I was told it was for the farmers
column210: but the town's only 100 years old
column211: and not sure what the purpose of it would be after Timex started mass producing.\r\n\r\nAnd then something else actually struck me while reading excerpts from the book.  When the lady who did the article interviewed me
column212: she said at two different times:  \"Nobody needs to know that.\"  The first was that I was born overseas
column213: the second was that my family is Jewish.  The town seemed genuinely fascinated with my \"Buddhist-ness\" but obviously not so much with my wife and kids' Judaism.\r\n

Message 01\/07\/2008: \n\nI grew up in Hinsdale
column177: less than a half an hour from Keene
column178: right across the border from Brattleboro. Granted
column179: the area is mainly white--that's the truth.  Why it has remained so? Not due to some implied racism
column180: but due to a horrible housing market. How horrible?  People with decent jobs living at camp grounds and out of vehicles because there's such a lack of housing in the city and what is there is overpriced. I'd also like to say that in my tiny town near Keene
column181: there was a black family and several of South Asian and Pacific Islander descent. Yes
column182: only one black family at the time I moved- but I went to school with the kids
column183: one was in my \nFrench class

column185: there might have been

column187: if there was racism

column189: and in a town that size
column190: everyone knows everything about everyone else. Adults
column191: too. There were Islander boys recently welcomed into the Fire Department. I know
column192: I know- this is Hinsdale I speak of

column196: and are a rather different culture.\n\nAs for your question still sundown?  Please- the ALAANA festival takes place less than a half hour from its borders

column176: 7\/2007\r\n\r\nYour presentation on sundown towns recalled for me an incident related to me by friends who had experienced blatant racism in Bordentown NJ while on a trip from Washington
column177: DC to New York City in 1934 or 1935.\r\n \r\nThe two young men
column178: both students at The Ohio State University in Columbus
column179: OH (where they could not live in a dormitory on the OSU campus) had visited a friend who was a student at Howard University in Washington.  On the next leg of their trip -- to take in New York City -- they drove up through NJ
column180: and decided to stop in Bordentown to get a bite to eat.  They were told that not only would they not be able to do that
column181: they had better get outof Bordentown \"before the sun went down\".  Needless to say
column182: they did just that. \r\n \r\nNeither of them ever forgot the pure hatred evidenced in the looks and manners of the restaurant owner and his customers as the two of them beat a hasty retreat from the premises.  The two young men went on to graduate from college and to have successful professional careers in their home town
column183: Cleveland
column184: Ohio.  \r\n \r\nI checked the details of this story with the survivor
column185: and he confirmed my recollection of the details as related to me many years ago.\r\n \r\nYou must remember that there were segregated schools in New Jersey when the Supreme Court decided Brown in 1954.  There were many \"white\" towns in NJ well into the 1970's -- not by law
column186: but just because everybody knew if you were \"colored\"
column187: \"Negro\"
column188: \"Black\" or African American -- you weren't welcome.\r\n

column176: Email 1\/2008 lponter@gmail.com\r\n\r\nI lived in Cherry Hill before it was Cherry Hill:(1957-1975) We were one of the first Jewish families in what is now a predominantly Jewish town.  Nobody cared about our religion other than the local Baptist minister. And he didn't bother anyone.  There were many Christian sub-groups.  There were several sub-divisions that were \"black.\" It was a farming town
column177: a rural area adjacent to Camden and the site of the \"white flight\" that occurred from that town in the late 60's.  The schools were integrated where they were close to the black neighborhoods but the others were all-white. My family members were part of the all-volunteer fire brigade. The sirens at all sub-stations were blown twice daily as a test. Any other suggestion is nonsense. Cherry Hill quickly integrated as Black people gained enough wealth to move into town. There is currently a \"richer\" side and a \"poorer\" side which creates some static.  It is also interesting to note that there is a large Asian and Indian population
column178:
with many professionals.  All have integrated with some ease.

"In the early 1960s my family moved into a new 
subdivision in the township of Cherry Hill
column179:
New Jersey.  
Every day at 6:00 am and 6:00 pm a siren from the 
Ashland fire station sounded. New residents were 
never given much more of an explanation other than it 
was a tradition of that firehouse. At that time
column180: the area \r\nwas very segregated and located near the black town \r\nof Lawnside.\"

column176: Former NJ resident:\n\nWhen I lived in New Jersey one of my friends (of Italian descent) was from Clark
column177:
NJ and told us that blacks driving in Clark after sundown were routinely stopped by police for being "dark after dark."  Clark was residentially all white.  This was in 1980.

Long-time Black resident of neighboring town of Westfield:

There was an African-American couple in Westfield who actually helped us a lot as we were getting settled into Westfield.  They used to tell stories about Clark and especially about the White Diamond.  The KKK used to have meetings there.  We don't know if it was folklore or true
column178: but nobody ever checked.  We didn't challenge it.  We were told to make sure that you were not anywhere near Clark when it started getting dark.  When they would have friends that were coming into Westfield
column179: they told them to drive around.  If you were coming off the parkway
column180: you would probably get off in Cranford and then come into Westfield instead of going through Clark.  If you did go to Clark
column181: you made sure you followed every single speed limit possible.  I have no idea how it started
column182: but I know that I passed it down to my kids.  It didn't help -- I want to say it was in the 1990s -- when a Black family moved into Clark
column183:
and then they had a cross burned in front of their house.

column176: Former Green Brook resident: \n\n\"Green Brook was one of a cluster of exceedingly small towns in Somerset County. I have always been struck by how each \"white\" town in the area seems to have a twin
column177: \"black\" town
column178: which of course is relatively poorer. For Green Brook
column179: it was South Plainfield; for Livingston
column180: it was East Orange
column181: etc. I honestly can't say
column182: though
column183: whether this segregation at the municipal level was explicit (I doubt it) or enforced through the usual home loan\/real estate combo. \n\tThe New Jersey government includes an additional layer
column184: the township
column185: between the county and the municipality.  The township can include several municipalities or parts thereof
column186: or it can cover only section of an existing municipality (in which case it would lie below the latter
column187: not above
column188: in any organizational chart).  The existence of such a structure can make it hard to decide exactly when a town was created
column189: or why.  The land that Green Brook comprises today has at different times lain in the Counties of Middlesex and Somerset
column190: and the Townships of Piscataway
column191: Bridgewater
column192: Warren
column193: and North Plainfield.  The area first came under a single
column194: albeit larger administration just before the Civil War
column195: when it was designated part of Warren Township.  Green Brook went along with North Plainfield when it seceded from Warren Township in 1872
column196: only to secede itself in 1932.  The area that became Green Brook Township was at that time the most agricultural section of an area much changed by industrialization.  Many of the tract houses were not built until after World War II.\n\tThe question for our purposes
column197: of course
column198: is
column199: why the secessions    especially in 1932? Based on the township government's own site
column200: Green Brook would seem to have sat in a cthonic stupor from the seventeenth century until the Revolutionary War.  Then
column201: of course
column202: its Brave Sons joined the local militia to a man and helped General Washington espy the British movements in and around New York (at the top of the Watchung mountain range in town lies Washington Rock state park
column203: whence the good General allegedly did his snooping).  Come independence and peace
column204: the area lapsed back into somnolence.\n\tDigging around a little more
column205: I found some other things.  Most of the area on the east side of the town was once part of the Vermuele Plantation
column206: one of the farms that helped make the county's population at least one tenth slave and indentured servant right up to abolition in 1846.  The area was lukewarm toward the Union cause in the Civil War; one neighboring town's history site describes the busy trade in substitute recruitment after the introduction of conscription.  Yet a plantation\/copperhead past does not a sunset town make.  Plenty of all white neighborhoods have seceded from all white towns and cities in New Jersey before.  Down the shore
column207: mainland towns withdraw from townships because they don't want to effectively subsidize the utilities and beach costs of the island towns.  The more established island towns in turn resist incorporation because they don't want to be swallowed up by the larger
column208: more populous towns on the bay.  Green Brook's neighboring town of Dunellen was carved out of the township of Piscataway in the 1860s by a group of real estate speculators who had a sweetheart deal with the rail company to build a new station nearby.  In 1932
column209: Green Brook was a backwater farming section of North Plainfield
column210: itself one of the dozens of small cities in that part of the state that had developed a stock of industries.  Amid the financial and social crisis of the Depression
column211: who can say why they made the decision to secede? \n\tAll of this is a roundabout way of saying that I'd need to do more research
column212: preferably with copies of the local newspapers.  I had previously thought that Green Brook had incorporated after WW2
column213: which would be more of a red flag.  Through the early 80s
column214: no black family that I knew of had bought a home there
column215: but I cannot say whether this is due to the circumstances surrounding the town's creation
column216: or simple racism.\"\n

	Asbury Park is a predominantly black town on the beach.  Just south of Asbury is Ocean Grove.  Ocean Grove has a long history as a religious town
column182: complete with a huge auditorium that has religious revivals during the summer (this still continues today).  But the inside word is that the town has always had a history of racism.  The border between these two towns is a tidal lagoon with several footbridges over the lagoon connecting the two towns.  There has always been a tall gate at the Ocean Grove end that was locked each night.  Within the past 10 years there was a big controversy surrounding the gate.  Asbury neighbors complained that it was a sign of racism

	*Introduction*
Clarence Stein's and Henry Wright's work on the New Jersey suburb of Radburn was a fundamental departure in terms of built form
column191: ideological paradigm
column192: and design process
column193: from American planning traditions in the 1920s. Since its partial completion in 1929
column194: Radburn has served in turn as a model for the form

column197: I will briefly trace Radburn's history and theoretical underpinnings

	William H. Jacobsen Jr. remembers hearing the 6 p.m. whistle blow daily in Carson Valley.
	It was the sound of segregation.
	"When the whistle went off
column180: the Indians had to get out of Minden and Gardnerville
column181: \" said Jacobsen
column182: a retired University of Nevada
column183: Reno professor
column184: renowned linguist
column185: author and pioneer in the study of tribal languages.\n\tThe whistle is gone. The Washoe tribe remains
column186: and so does Jacobsen. He is recognized as the foremost expert on the ancient and complex language of the Washoe tribe that has occupied Carson Valley and surrounding areas for thousands of years.\n\tAs a graduate student at the University of California in the 1950s
column187: Jacobsen came to Carson Valley to learn Washoe
column188: or Washo
column189: the spelling most often used by linguists.\n\tFor centuries
column190: Washoe had been a spoken language. Jacobsen learned to speak it fluently. It wasn't written down. Jacobsen wrote it down.\n\t\"He's responsible for the writing system we are using
column191: \" said Laura Fillmore

column193: who retired from UNR's English department in 1994

column195: \"Beginning Washo
column196: \" in 1996 based on his life's work
column197: which started when he wrote a 700 page dissertation on Washoe grammar.\n\t\"William Jacobsen has devoted four decades of research and scholarship to the analysis of the Washoe language and is recognized as its foremost linguistic expert
column198: \" Warren L. Azevedo

column200: said when the book was released.\n\t\"All recent students of Washoe culture are indebted to him for his generous guidance in accurate translation of terms and texts

column202: \" Jacobsen said with a chuckle. \"I'm not as good as he thinks I am.\"\n\tBut Jacobsen
column203: who came to Carson Valley when the road between Gardnerville and the Washoe community of Dresslerville was unpaved

column178: Where Freed Slaves Could Make a Home\"  \n(newsday.com\/community\/guide\/lihistory\/ny-historytownÔøΩhist0044
column181: print.story)\n\n\"When Irwin Quintyne went shopping for a new home in Levittown after World War II
column182: the real estate agents told him bluntly that blacks weren't wanted. So Quintyne turned to the only community he knew of that would welcome his family   North Amityville.\n\t\"I saw an ad in The Amsterdam News for homes in Amityville. I got a GI loan and bought a house on Bayview Avenue
column183: \" said Quintyne
column185: who now lives on Madison Avenue. \"It seemed we were almost guided here by the racism on Long Island. After Levittown
column186: North Amityville was it.\"\n\tActually
column187: there were several communities on Long Island where blacks settled in the postwar housing boom. Besides North Amityville
column188: there were Wyandanch
column189: Roosevelt
column190: Hempstead and West Hempstead. Quintyne wasn't aware of those places at the time; he picked North Amityville because he had a friend who lived there.\n\tAs political scientist Andrew Wiese argued in a paper on racial segregation on Long Island presented at a Hofstra University conference in 1993
column191: black suburbs like North Amityville grew out of historic black enclaves in unincorporated areas that were out of reach of municipal restrictions designed to exclude blacks. In North Amityville's case
column192: the hamlet was excluded when the predominantly white village of Amityville incorporated in 1894.\n\t\"In these places
column193: race acted as a double edged sword
column194: carving small spaces for black families adjacent to older communities largely because developers for whites would not risk building there and no concentrated white elite was in place to organize against them
column195: \" Wiese wrote. \"Developers ceded these places to the African American market.\" This is in contrast with a place such as Gordon Heights
column196: a community in Brookhaven Town that was deliberately marketed to blacks starting in the early 1900s.\n\tThe history of North Amityville as a black enclave actually goes back to the 1700s
column197: when the first blacks came to Amityville as slaves
column198: freed slaves or indentured servants. While some may have worked their own land
column199: others were employed by the wealthy white farmers who settled along the shore. By 1815
column200: they were meeting in one another's homes for religious services
column201: forming one of the oldest black congregations on Long Island: the Bethel African Methodist Church. (The church was originally founded as the Bethel Church by Indian families who had settled the community earlier.)\n\tAs slaves were gradually emancipated across New York
column202: many in communities like Amityville continued to work the land as tenant farmers or as laborers. Some moved into commercial or retail industries
column203: providing services for the burgeoning black enclave. They opened barber shops
column204: liveries
column205: groceries and delicatessens. And when Amityville became a premier summer destination for vacationers
column206: many blacks were hired to work in the hotels and resorts built by the sea.\n\tLillian Miller
column208: recalls the stories her late husband
column209: Norman
column210: used to tell about his earliest ancestors. \"His grandmother was born on the Shinnecock reservation
column211: and his grandfather used to go to the bay every day to fish. He kept cows and chickens
column212: \" said Miller
column213: who still lives in the house her husband built on Columbus Boulevard in 1932. \"And Norman's mother used to work as a servant for old Dr. Luce in the village.\" Lillian's own parents
column214: John and Lillie Kenney
column215: moved to North Amityville in 1923
column216: when Lillian was 9 years old. Originally from Charleston
column217: S.C.
column218: the couple spent the early part of their marriage in upper Manhattan until they could afford a move to the country. When they had enough money
column219: Lillie opened a deli on Albany Avenue near Smith Street and John had a barber shop nearby.\n\tThese three families
column220: the Millers
column221: the Kenneys and the Quintynes
column222: neatly illustrate the dominant patterns of black migration on Long Island. First
column223: there were the freed slaves who farmed the land just as the early white settlers did. Then there were families like the Kenneys
column224: who came in the early part of this century to work as servants in the large summer hotels or open businesses catering to the growing summer populations. And
column225: finally
column226: middle class families like the Quintynes were driven to these same communities where earlier black families provided an anchor.\n\tIn a 20 year span ending in 1960
column227: the African American population on Long Island grew by 50
column228: 000. But the bulk of that growth didn't come until the 1950s when new suburban developments such as North Amityville's Ronek Park were sprouting up to cater to the black middle class.\n\tMany of the streets that would make up Ronek Park
column229: like Bayview Avenue and Emerald Lane
column230: were carved out of a farm the Von Nessen family ran for more than 30 years. Bought by Thomas Romano
column231: the land that once produced fruits and vegetables sprouted ranch homes that sold in 1951 for $8
column232: 400 apiece. Romano eventually built more than 1
column233: 000 three bedroom homes in Ronek Park
column234: according to Wiese.\n\t\"When we built our house
column235: there were a few others
column237: they're all around. As they years went by
column238: it all filled in. I guess people just kept coming.\"\n\n\tWhere to Find More: \"A History of North Amityville
column239: \" by Walter G. Clerk
column240: available in the Amityville Public Library. \n\nAlso

	Roosevelt is a classic example of a community in a "color class conflict
column238: \" brought on by the American racist society in which all minorities of color live. The Blacks in Roosevelt reflect the aspirations and frustrations of all Afro Americans. Color
column239: not class
column240: was the primary factor of opposition in the color transformation of the Roosevelt community. Whites rejected not only the severely poverty stricken Blacks
column241: but Blacks of middle and upper income status as well. All were treated as \"racial castes\" segregated to themselves
column242: housing districts away from the white residents of the community. The situation became intensified when welfare recipients began entering the community. This drastic shift in the economic status of the community created a massive exodus of \"frantic white flight
column243: \" spurned on by the alarmist tactics of greedy Real Estate Brokers who exploited the racist fears of the white residents for their own financial gain. While many forces are essential to correct this problem in race relations
column244: certain strategies must be developed which speak directly to it. The Black leadership in the Black community must begin to assume the basic role of confronting those forces
column245: which would prevent black  middle and higher income groups from entering previously all white higher communities.\n\tThey must seek to initiate pressure against the political forces that be
column246: and as one renowned political official
column247: Mr. Herman Washington has said
column248: \"They must enforce the Anti Discrimination Laws
column249: Housing and local Zoning Laws and legislation which will protect the rights of all the residents in the community.\"\n\tIn brief
column250: the economic and social conditions in Roosevelt are not only separating Blacks but threatening the \"security\" of the whites in and around the community as well. It is not an overstatement to suggest that the rebirth of direct social action be it riots
column251: marches \"long hot summers\" or otherwise
column252: could be probable and even justifiable if the economic conditions in Roosevelt are permitted to continue as is. History now challenges both whites and blacks in Roosevelt to learn from the past and move to transform their community for the betterment of all its residents as a sign in the process of transforming America.\"\n

column176: Former Seaford Resident:\n\nI also recall as a child in the mid 50s and early 60s hearing a fire horn sound at noon and at 6:00 pm. I don't know whether this was just to allow everyone in town (Seaford

column187: many were saying that their parents would not let them go to school if HE was going to be there. (I had grown up in NYC public housing and public school through the third grade and couldn't at the time understand what all the fuss was about

Former Tonawanda Resident:

"I was wonderng if you looked at the town I grew up in.  Tonawanda
column204: NY.  It is located along the Niagara River about half way between Buffalo and Niagara Falls.  There are three Tonawandas.  One is the Town of Tonawanda in Erie County which has a population of 60K
column205: the next is the City of Tonawanda in Erie County with a population of about 20K
column206:
and the city of North Tonawanda in Niagara County. 
	The two cities are the focus.  They are split by the mouth of the Erie Canal into the Niagara River.  The cities have a much older waterfront/downtown areas which have homes from early 1900s or earlier. The rest of the towns built up during the late 50s and early 60s. Tonawanda is fully developed (since the late 60s) and North Tonawanda still has land for new home exurb development.
	The homes within about 5 blocks of the water are over 100 years old.  The others were built in the 1950s and 1960s.  In North Tonawanda there are some new development sections.  The cities have had a football rivalry in high school that has gone on for over 100 yrs.
	The older sections of the towns have a small town mentality even though it is in the heart of suburbia.  The older sections of the cities generally are made up of multiple generations where people stay close to parents and everyone knows gossip about everyone else.
	During the early 60s the city of Tonawanda was known for the racism with cross burnings.  When I went through school in the mid 70s and 80s there were probably a handful of minority families.  The high school had about 2 4 minorities (from 2 families
column207: one black an one Laosian).  \n\tThe one thing the city did out of tradition was the ringing of the the 9pm horn that would be heard throughout the old section of the city. Its purpose was to vacate the town of backs after dark.\n\tIn the city the racism has changed. Instead of being overt
column208: like you could find in the rural south
column209: it has become more subtle.  It is more based on stereotypes and the idea that one could work with
column210: or talk to blacks but they wouldn't see themselves being close friends with them. They would also say stuff when in private that were more stereotypical than blatant racist. \n\tI grew up in the outer
column211: relatively newer areas of the city of Tonawanda.  My parents following many who moved to the suburbs from the city.  My father spent his career as an inner city Buffalo high school which was mostly minority or immigrants and English was a second language. \n\t When I was a kid and you would hear that horn
column212: you started realizing a pattern of it going off at the same time.  Initially it may sound like an emergency siren.  I don't even recall when I learned
column213: but I learned about it from friends or their parents that the horns original purpose was for the town exit. I may have learned about it roughly when I was when I was around 12
column214: early 1980s.\n\tAs I said before
column215: people would talk in private some things that they would never say in public.\"\n

column176: %u201CPublic Housing Does Not Diminish Property Values
column177: %u201D from National Low Income Housing Coalition: The NIMBY Report
column178: November
column179:
2000.

Abstract:

	%u201CIn the midst of Baltimore's ongoing struggle to desegregate its public housing
column180: a Harvard University scholar has raised three critical points that seem to be lost in the midst of a NIMBY charged debate about where several thousand low income African American residents will live. First
column181: newly developed
column182: scattered site public housing does not reduce neighbors' property values. Second
column183: such housing does not diminish the social fabric of neighborhoods. Finally
column184: new public housing does not compromise community safety. \n\tDrawn from the experience of Yonkers
column185: New York
column186: where a landmark 1986 federal court order required the city to create scattered site public housing to remedy a decades long problem of housing and school segregation
column187: a comprehensive study conducted by Xavier de Souza Briggs
column188: of Harvard's John F. Kennedy School of Government (%u201CIn the Wake of Desegregation
column189: %u201D Journal of the American Planning Association
column190: Winter 1999.)
column191: found that the traditional grounds for objection to public housing are not supported by fact. The study examines the impact of 200 units of low rise
column192: townhouse style public housing built in seven neighborhoods between 1992 and 1994
column193: and occupied primarily by very low income African Americans and Latinos. The five year study
column194: focusing on home sales and surveys of hundreds of adjacent homeowners
column195: concludes that there is no evidence that scattered site housing had negative impacts on receiving neighborhoods in terms of property values
column196: neighbors%u2019 plans to move or their sense of community cohesiveness. The study also found no evidence of %u201Cwhite flight.%u201D The study provides strong support for advocates who want to combine the results of academic research with a coordinated program of community acceptance strategies.%u201D\n

Message 01/06/2008:
Having grown up in Oberlin as an African American (Colored born in 1934)person.  We always knew that we didn't have any business in Amherst. In So. Amherst there was a liquor store. My father ran a barber shop in Oberlin and many of his customers came from Amherst
column194: you could hear the rhetoric in the barber shop.  It was just a place we didn't go.\n\nAccording to a former resident
column195: \"No question
column196: as late as the mid-60s when I was a kid there
column197: this was a sundown town.  Black people driving down Rt. 58 from Lorain to Oberlin didn't stop in Amherst
column198: even for gas or fast food.  There was a 6 o'clock whistle
column199: from the city hall or the neighboring fire station if I recall.  Many older adults told me that was \"time for the niggers to be out of town\".  Some of these conversations took place at the time I lived there -- others 20 and 30 years later.  There were no people of non-Caucasian ancestry living there in the mid 60s.  Nor any as far as I know for at least 10 or 15 years after that.  I was never aware of any formal codification.  It was all word of mouth and behavioral.\"\n\nAnother former resident
column200: growing up in Amherst in the late sixties and seventies explains: \"It wasn't until 1978 or 1979 that my parents told me why the fire alarm went off everyday at 6:00pm. The phrase was 'The sun never sets on a N____ in Amherst.' I heard racial slurs and signs of prejudice frequently.\" \n\nTestimony from another resident: \"My husband was a fireman since the early 1960s. The 6 o'clock whistle was a siren check every day for the firemen. In itself
column201: it had nothing to do with telling certain people to get out of town.  I think the legend persisted when racists started using the sound of the siren by saying it was a call [for minorities] to leave town... but the siren was never used for that purpose.\"\nHence
column202: although the siren may have originally served some other purpose
column203: some residents of Amherst and surrounding areas have come to define it that way.

column183: some Southerners that had been slave owners who owned lands in this area

column185:
then took a canal boat on the Miami & Erie Canal to a town (St. Marys) the next county over (Auglaize).  They were met by German farmers with guns and told in no uncertain terms to stay on the boat and head back to Cincinnati.  Our local paper did an article on this some years ago.

I would have to do some digging in the local history room of the library but I seem to recall that our town (Celina) was one such town back then.

email 3/2008 -- student researcher and resident

Doylestown is a small village within Chippewa Twp. The manuscript census figures for Chippewa Twp. and the Silver Creek mine
column180: where the strike was
column181:
was also in the twp.

The first record of a black family living in the township was a barber named Lewis Holmes and his wife Flora in 1870.

In 1880 in Chippewa
column182: during the Nadir of race relations
column183:
122 black and
biracial men suddenly appeared on the town's census. By the 1900 census the township was again 100% white.  

In Chippewa in 1880 there was a coal
mine strike where the miners wanted more money form their employer Erastus Loomis. So Loomis decided to bring black strike breakers from Virginia to his Silver Creek Mines in Chippewa and Wadsworth. A local map from the 1950's
shows it as the "Niger Mine."  

Loomis hired carpenters to make a
stockade to keep the blacks safe.  The white miners were upset and they
talked about driving the blacks out of the mine. 500 men stoned the
barricade around the mines and injured one of the constables.  

Eighty soldiers were stationed at the mine for six weeks to protect the
miners. 

There is no record that any further violence occurred.  Local historians
say simply that they eventually drifted back to the South and a few
moved to nearby Wadsworth.  


Doylestown/Chippewa Township: 

Chippewa Township 1870 blacks 4

Chippewa Township 1880 blacks 122

Chippewa Township 1900 blacks 0

Chippewa Township 2000 blacks 4

column176:
Unpleasant Meadows:  A development battle exposes the racial divide in Cuyahoga Falls where today's ally is tomorrow's enemy.
By Sarah Fenske
	
        They call it Caucasian Falls.
	No one knows exactly how the name arose
column177: but no one seems offended by it either. It is
column178: after all
column179: a white town. Former Mayor Bob Quirk taught in the Cuyahoga Falls schools for years and never had a black student.\n\tToday
column180: the city is about the size of Lakewood
column181: and just as white
column182: with fewer than 1
column183: 000 black residents. But while Lakewood's whiteness seems benign
column184: [not at all; see below!] Akronites have long viewed Cuyahoga Falls much as Clevelanders do Parma.  Blacks used to joke that driving through Cuyahoga Falls meant automatically being pulled over. When a black friend asked how he could help Quirk's campaign
column185: Quirk had a ready quip: \"Campaign for my opponent!\"\n\tMark Anderson understands the sentiment. In April
column186: Anderson was relaxing at home when a Cuyahoga Falls policeman saw an open door and burst in
column187: believing something was amiss. The cop interrogated him aggressively
column188: refusing to leave until Anderson pulled out his badge
column189: says attorney John Scanlon. Anderson just happens to be black    and a Summit County Sheriff's deputy.\n\tWhen Anderson called the city to discuss the incident
column190: Scanlon says
column191: he was ignored. So he filed suit
column192: alleging false imprisonment triggered by racism.\n\tTellingly
column193: Cuyahoga Falls sought to move the suit to federal court. \"We think they're looking for a jury pool from a larger area
column194: so they can find people who don't have preconceived notions about the city
column195: \" Scanlon says. \"Hey
column196: I was born and raised in west Akron. I've heard about Cuyahoga Falls all my life.\"\n\tFormer state Representative Wayne Jones
column197: a lifelong resident
column198: shares that perspective. The city's racism is nothing overt
column199: he says
column200: but \"you don't need to come out and say it. People know. There are certain buzzwords. They know how to use them.\"\n\tJones recalls how his 1997 mayoral bid was sabotaged by a whisper campaign indicating that    horror of horrors    he wanted to integrate the city. \"I couldn't tell you the things my kids were called
column201: \" Jones says
column202: wincing. \"N   lover
column203: things like that.\"\n\tJones has since left politics. \"I'll never put my family through that kind of attack
column204: \" he says.\n\tThe Buckeye Community Hope Foundation knew none of this when it chose to build a low income apartment complex in Cuyahoga Falls. It was the Columbus non profit's first foray into Greater Cleveland
column205: and it was still fairly new to the housing game. \"We didn't know about Cuyahoga Falls
column206:
" says Executive Director Gil Barno. "We didn't know it had been 'Caucasian Falls' for generations."
	Buckeye's education would be brutal.
	                                                                        
	It all started in 1995
column207: with six wooded acres and a $300
column208: 000 mortgage. The land was tucked into the part of Cuyahoga Falls still known as Northampton
column209: named for the township that once held it. While Cuyahoga Falls is suburban at its core
column210: Northampton feels rural
column211: with twisting two lane roads and farmhouses.\n\tAt the time
column212: Buckeye was just finding its niche in Ohio's tax credit housing program. The state issues tax free bonds
column213: which developers sell to investors to cover construction costs. In exchange
column214: developers agree to restrict their complexes to people who earn less than the county's median income.\n\tCuyahoga Falls Mayor Don Robart didn't seem concerned when Buckeye President Steve Boone sent him details of his plan to build a 72 unit complex called Pleasant Meadows. When Boone called Robart to see if his package arrived
column215: the mayor seemed nice enough. So Buckeye charged ahead. In November 1995
column216: it secured a loan with National City Bank; in January
column217: Ohio awarded the project $3.1 million in tax credits. (Robart did not return repeated calls for comment.)\n\tThe city's planning director recommended approval
column218: but Councilman George Potts began distributing fliers around Northampton
column219: advising residents of upcoming hearings on the project
column220: which
column221: to his understanding
column222: would be developed with \"federal funding.\"\n\tPotts may have been off on the details
column223: but his flier seemed to announce
column224: in Caucasian Falls code
column225: something Robart apparently missed: The tenants wouldn't be wealthy
column226: and not all of them would be white.\n\tThat changed everything. On any given day
column227: someone in some suburb is fighting a development. No one wants to see his home investment erode
column228: or his little piece of paradise invaded by newcomers. But public housing is a different battle entirely. To a certain brand of suburbanite
column229: it means \"black.\" And in Caucasian Falls
column230: black ain't pretty.\n\t                                                                        \n\tAkron's biggest neighbor is a mix of affluent commuters and blue collar workers who are born here
column231: stay here
column232: and polish traditional prejudices like family heirlooms.\n\tThey packed the city's hearing for Pleasant Meadows. \"We walked into a hornet's nest
column233: in terms of the room was full of people who did not want our development
column234: \" Boone would later recall in court. \"And it was pretty    it got pretty ugly at times.\"\n\tThe buzzwords Jones cites were in full supply
column235:
all recorded for posterity in council minutes:
	/"They know what kind of element is going to move in there."/
	/"There will be a different class of people living there."/
	/"This project is already being called Pleasant Ghetto . . . You know I do not want that in my community anymore. We already have enough of that." /
	/"I do not see that our school system is correct for these children that would be there." /
	/"When they get that boom box going
column236: it will be loud.\"\/\n\t\"We have all read the police blotter. We all know the number of calls that come out of Prange Drive and some of the other places
column237: \" said Mayor Robart
column238: referring to a neighborhood with public housing and the city's only concentrated minority population.\n\t\"Lord knows that the social engineers back 20 or 25 years ago brought us busing. That was a wonderful project in Cleveland
column239: wasn't it? We all know now that Cleveland has the worst school district probably in the country. We went down that road
column240: didn't we?\"\n\tBut Pleasant Meadows fit the zoning code. Planning commission members believed they had no other option
column241: so they approved it.\n\tThe proposal went to city council. Again
column242: the room was packed. Again
column243: it was ugly. \"They were spilling out into the hallway
column244: \" Boone would later recall. \"It was just a lot of anger would be a good word
column245: and hatred.\"\n\tAt one point
column246: a resident suggested throwing Boone out the window. (\"I'm just fortunate we were on the first floor
column247: \" he joked.)\n\tCouncil delayed the vote for two meetings. Finally
column248: at the third
column249: members bit the bullet and gave Pleasant Meadows its approval.\n\tThey were less than effusive. \"I would hate like hell to have to look you all in the eyes and vote yes for this project
column250: \" said Councilman Sandy Rubino. \"But unless I hear something to the contrary from the law department
column251: I do not have any choice.\"\n\tYet Mayor Robart still wanted to play hardball
column252: lawyers be damned. \"The reason I recommend that we turn this down is because then the ball is in Mr. Boone's court
column253: \" he said. \"Will he sue? I do not know. A couple things we know. If he sues
column254: guess what? We have got an in house law department.\n\t\"Now I would doubt Mr. Boone has an on staff law department to file a lawsuit. Which means what? He has to spend dollars. Well
column255: if he has to spend dollars
column256: he has to make a decision as to how long does he want to pursue this thing.\"\n\tResidents took his words to heart. Soon after council's decision
column257: a group gathered petitions
column258: demanding a referendum to let voters decide Pleasant Meadows' fate. The city
column260: halted all building permits.\n\tSo Buckeye sued
column261: just as Robart predicted. What Robart didn't foresee: The nonprofit would find a lawyer willing to handle the case free of charge.\n\t                                                                        \n\tBuckeye didn't ask for a trial. It just asked Summit County Common Pleas Judge John Murphy to issue building permits. The facts: City council had approved the project
column262: and citizens didn't have a right to challenge that.\n\tMurphy
column263: not surprisingly
column264: refused to halt the referendum. Thwarting constituents' right to vote    besides seeming downright un American    isn't good business for an elected official. And the law was also murkier than Buckeye wanted to admit.\n\tIndeed
column265: the appeals court
column266: which Buckeye petitioned immediately after Murphy's decision
column267: didn't buy its argument either. In December 1996
column268: one month after voters rejected Pleasant Meadows by a large majority
column269: appellate judges ruled the petitions legal. The vote would stand.\n\tBuckeye headed to the Ohio Supreme Court. At first
column270: the justices issued a decision agreeing with the two lower courts
column271: but at Buckeye's request
column272: they reexamined their decision two months later. This time
column273: Justice Evelyn Lundberg Stratton changed her mind.\n\tForcing voters to decide Buckeye's fate
column275: was deferring \"the minutiae of everyday administrative decision making to the whim of the voter at the moment. The unpopular development
column276: the disfavored contract with a school principal
column278: or any other decision could be subject to voter disapproval if an angered voter was organized or well funded enough.\"\n\tThat
column280: could be disastrous: \"Chaos and instability could result.\"\n\tStratton's flip meant a 4 3 Buckeye victory. Cuyahoga Falls was forced to issue permits. Four years after buying the land
column281: Buckeye finally started construction.\n\t                                                                        \n\tPleasant Meadows was finished by 2000. The place proved neither scary nor remarkable--just a block of two story townhouses
column283: pale gray siding on top. But the conflict wasn't over.\n\tBuckeye had filed a second lawsuit at the same time as the first
column285: seeking cash for the delay. Its argument was novel: Cuyahoga Falls' recalcitrance discriminated against the African Americans and families who would live in the complex
column286: a violation of the Fair Housing Act.\n\tThe tactic was the brainchild of Diane Citrino
column287: a senior attorney at Housing Advocates Inc. The nonprofit firm agreed to handle the case on a contingency basis. If Buckeye lost
column288: the firm made nothing. If it won
column289: the city would have to pay its legal fees.\n\tIt wasn't immediately clear if Citrino's strategy had legal merit. But the three year delay had certainly hurt Buckeye. The foundation almost went bankrupt. \"We had all our seed money tied up in this

column298: the so called Buckeye Rule. If courts rule that a tax credit project is stalled through no fault of its own

column301: 000 in added bonds to cover rising construction costs. Yet the company still thought Cuyahoga Falls should pay. \"The city took them out of the pipeline for years
column302: \" Citrino says. \"This was their life. This is what they did

column305: deciding Buckeye deserved a trial.\n\tThe judges homed in on comments made by residents at public meetings. Some
column307: could be referring to lower economic status. However
column308: \"These statements can just as easily be seen as expressions of racial bias against blacks
column309: especially given the fact that racial stereotypes prevalent in our society associate blacks with crime
column311: and lower class status.\"\n\tCuyahoga Falls Law Director Virgil Arrington is confident he can prevail in court. But in his mind
column312: the case could trigger a dark future of citizens' rights curtailed
column314: and the end of democracy. So he fired off a Hail Mary appeal to the U.S. Supreme Court.\n\tThe Supreme Court agrees to hear only a fraction of the petitions it receives
column315: but the justices saw something in Arrington's plea. They accepted the case
column316: which will be heard early next year.\n\tMuch is at stake
column317: Citrino says. \"For the whole greater Ohio community
column318: affordable housing is a major problem. To stop something like that is a tragedy.\" Worst of all
column320:
was the city's motivation: racism.
	                                                                        
	It's hard to find anyone who believes opposition to Pleasant Meadows wasn't based at least partly on prejudice.
	That may be because the likely suspects are silent. Arrington has warned city council not to talk to the media. And petition gatherers
column321: who long insisted their fight was about crime and property values
column323: no longer seem interested in discussing the matter.\n\tBut even Arrington doesn't defend the city's reputation. Instead
column324: he opts to defend residents' right to be racist. \"I hate to put it this way
column325: but even bigots have a right to free speech
column327: shrugging.\n\tBuckeye's argument rests on two things: racial code words
column328: and the impact of the city's rejection on minorities and families.\n\tNeither is a slam dunk. Technically
column329: it was citizens who made racist statements
column331: but Buckeye isn't suing residents. The developer's target is the city. All the city did was allow a vote to go forward    a vote that never mentioned race.\n\tBuckeye's view also implies that cities are responsible for the actions of their residents
column332: says Arrington: \"If the city can be held liable for one comment
column333: the city has liability for one citizen expressing his right to free speech.\"\n\tYet Citrino argues that Robart was quietly involved in the referendum    hand in glove trickery that allowed the city to keep officially clean while residents did the dirty work.\n\tRobart's own words may be Citrino's best argument. In front of his constituents
column334: he often postured as if he had done everything he could to derail the project    and paid the price. As he reminded residents
column337: and I'm the only one hanging out there with a $50
column338: 000 lawsuit on my head.\"\n\tHis campaign tactics against Jones in 1997 didn't help. As a state rep
column339: Jones co sponsored a constitutional amendment allowing Ohio to sell bonds for affordable housing. It wasn't controversial; voters approved it by a quarter million votes.\n\tBut weeks before the election
column340: Robart plastered Cuyahoga Falls with brochures
column341: the message clear even in its clumsiness: \"How Did Wayne Jones Vote in Columbus for Helping Put More Low Income Housing Projects in Cuyahoga Falls and Other Suburbs?\"\n\tThen came the follow up phone calls. \/You know Wayne Jones supports low income housing
column342: don't you?\/ Some listeners heard a more explicit message: \/It will bring minorities to Cuyahoga Falls.\/\n\t\"People said

Arrington says delicately that the complex \"has not been without problems.\" Police reports indicate several domestic scuffles
column349: but few reports of drugs or vandalism. Code enforcement officer Ted Williams received a complaint this summer about the grass being too high. That's about it. In other words

Other neighbors decline to talk. Even those living in the complex's shadow have little to say. Neighbor Timothy Lee claimed in 1996 that the complex would produce drugs
column352: and boom boxes. He's more conciliatory today.\n\t\"We're far enough away

we don't get up every day and think about it first thing . . ."
	                                                                        
	Two distinct ironies have developed in the years since Pleasant Meadows opened. One is that neighbors don't find the place all that terrible. The other is that tenants do.
	Allegations of racism
column362: have shifted away from the city. They now target Buckeye.\n\tJanet Hutchins
column363: a feisty single mother who left a nursing job to get her master's degree at Case Western Reserve
column364: thought she'd live in Pleasant Meadows while in school to save money.\n\tHutchins
column366: didn't like what she'd heard about Cuyahoga Falls
column367: and she wasn't too happy when a police officer followed her through the city to keep an eye on her. But her anger pales next to what she now feels for Buckeye.\n\tThe problems began with her chair
column368: which she set in front of her house to watch her little girls play. In April
column369: the chair disappeared. A neighbor said the apartment manager removed it.\n\tHutchins trotted over to the manager's office. According to the complaint she would later file
column370: Hutchins was told that \"You people need to be more responsible\" and \"I don't want you people to have chairs in front of your houses.\" The manager's husband then pushed Hutchins out the door; she ran home and called police. When she filed a complaint with the Ohio Civil Rights Commission
column371: she got an eviction notice    a fact she refuses to chalk up to coincidence. (Hutchins never pressed charges
column372: and the company later dropped the eviction.)\n\tSince then
column373: Hutchins has filed three additional complaints and encouraged neighbors to do the same. Eight others have done so

column378: claims an apartment manager called several black residents \"niggers.\" Others claim they've been discriminated against for having children

column381: they received eviction notices.\n\tIn the city's fight with Buckeye

column383: and they're not paying us anything
column385: who has filed several complaints. \"Why should the City of Cuyahoga Falls have to pay them anything?\"\n\tHutchins's latest complaint is against Clark
column386: the fair housing director. She claims that Clark tried to dissuade her from filing a claim because of Buckeye's suit against the city. \"She said
column387: 'You don't want to see Cuyahoga Falls get away with this.' I was like 'Screw your case
column389: then turns serious. \"I know Cuyahoga Falls is prejudiced
column390: but this company should not get a penny

column393: says Buckeye has a right to ban skateboarding. But retaliating against tenants who complain is illegal. It's also stupid.\n\t\"Instead of trying to suppress these complaints
column394: they should know the residents have a right to file them

column396: \" Curry says. \"That's what they don't get.\"\n\tBarno says the complaints are money motivated. Buckeye gave a cash settlement to a wheelchair bound resident who filed a complaint over accessibility last year. Since then
column398: everyone's been filing. \"It's been my experience that low income people are pretty good at playing the systems they come into contact with. This may be one of those cases.\"\n\tWhen pressed for evidence

Barno defers to another staffer. Then he calls back shortly to decline all comment.
	Citrino is none too worried. "Any 72 unit complex is going to have people who are unhappy. That goes with the territory."

Lebanon
email on 1/1/08 
I was a student a LUHS in the late 60's. My Dad was hired by Crown Zellerbach as Personnel Manager of the mill (now closed down). Affirmative Action came into being during this time and he went to Salem to recruit blacks to meet the 10% quota established by the government. He also helped them find housing and get settled in the community. I think that she was the first black student and her husband was among the first black residents of the community.  Dad went to Salem once a week and met with people at the state employment office until the quota was filled.  As a Junior at LUHS it didn't affect me much. I did show the woman around school and sit next to her in class for a while. She was a nice girl and I was a guest in her house. It was a good job for her husband.  Entry level wages were more than $7.00 per hour then with good union benefits.  You could live on that in 1969.  Dad thought the husband was a good kid and a hard worker.  There were some problems at the plant with the other workers at first as more people were brought in.  That was worked out between the Union leaders and Dad. The bottom line was that if the mill didn't comply with the Affirmative Action Program
column180: everyone would lose out.  The economics overcame the racism in the end.  Although Dad represented management in his heart he was always on the side of the worker.\n\nThe residents further recalls in a later email conversation (07\/2014) that \"I know there were others who moved to the area as the quota was filled

column180: Ohio for well over one hundred years (which is a long time in an immigrant community)

column185: exclusion) of African Americans has flourished here is a pervasive atmosphere of lawlessness.  Organized crime dominates every aspect of public and private life.\n\tIn my memory
column186: there have been no legal challenges made to local real estate  practices. Indeed

column189: of course
column190: are racist themselves and refuse to build for African Americans. Even the housing that is subsidized by HUD is almost all White. These practices will continue until they are challenged in federal court
column191: which I both look forward to and fear

column196: so that I might ask people if they remember a sign on Warren Ave or  wherever. It would give me a eighborhood to start with. I am highly skeptical of this since 1.) in the 1950s
column197: there was no reason for any black  person to be in Niles for any reason due to employment discrimination. If  you needed a domestic servant
column198: there were plenty of poor hillbilly Irish women with their out of wedlock children. Few people in Niles could afford domestic servants.  2.)By the 1950s there was little employment within the city limits....  The large steel mills in Warren and Youngstown attracted African American workers
column199: as would the auto assembly plants in the 1960s and '70s
column200: but the plants in Niles were not hiring many workers....\n\tThe main thing that was going on here in the 1920's 1930's was race violence directed at Catholics. My grandmother expended a lot of energy trying to inculcate me with a fear and hatred of the Klan
column201: who lynched her uncle.  My father says that he remembers a photograph that hung in his grandfather's real estate office of a militia of local men
column202: including my great grandfather
column203: standing in front of a convent with guns
column204: the caption underneath reading
column205: \"The Knights of Columbus.\" I know that there were armed sentries posted at particularly tense times on a few of the roads that lead into Niles to route  any \"outsiders\" that would try to come into the city...I cannot imagine were I  African American in the 1920s that I would choose to move to a town where  white people are engaged in armed combat with other white people.  You know
column206: you move up from the South to get AWAY from the Klan.\n\tI do not believe that covenants were used in Niles. In Niles
column207: the racial  composition of the town was controlled informally by real estate. These real  estate developers have to cooperate with the city and organized crime in order to operate. Many of the people in real estate in this city were part of the structure of organized crime
column208: or at least relied on their support to  operate their businesses. If you build a house and sell it to a Black family
column209: you will not ever get a building permit again. My mother's uncle was a local contractor and real estate developer
column210: and according to him
column211: it was  well known to local contractors that it was a waste of time trying to build in Niles city because there were only two contractors who were issued  permits. I think this practice has loosened within the past ten years due to attention being brought onto the city's policies through lawsuits against these two contractors for shoddy workmanship... \n\tInformal exclusion of African Americans from real estate seems to be basically
column212: just saying
column213: \"No. Sorry.\"  At some point
column214: this subterfuge developped where if an African American family tried to purchase a property in Niles
column215: the owner would be able to produce a fake deposit check as proof that the property was no longer for sale.\n\tThis deposit check business is interesting to me because Fair Housing law was fairly unenforceable until 1988
column216: and so what was the harm in simple stating
column217: \"I refuse to sell my property to Negros?\"  My dad said that in his opinion
column218: there was no real threat of any Black people integrating Niles neighborhoods because
column219: \"It would not happen. And they [african americans] knew better.  White people would refuse to sell their property to a Black family because they [the white sellers] knew they would be retaliated against.\"  and so I ask
column220: if Black people knew better than to try to buy properties
column221: then what is the purpose of the fake check?  he said
column222: \"In case some rich Black from Cleveland tried to come down with a lawyer and buy a house...\"  Again
column223: this makes no sense to me
column224: because Title VIII of the Civil Rights Act didn't take effect until 1971.... and even then
column225: no penalties were provided.  The NAACP
column226: which is what i believe \"rich Black from Cleveland\" must be code for
column227: couldn't do anything....    \n\tThe Erie Depot is still standing
column228: but passenger service ended there in 1962. My father says that he does not remember there being such a sign posted there in his lifetime
column229: but that his grandfather told him that such a sign had been posted at one time at the Pennsylvania Terminal on the south side of Niles
column230: a location that would be better described as \"the outskirts of town\" than Erie Street. Passenger service was ended on that line sometime around 1958.  No trace of it exists today
column231: but it was located at the intersection of Third and Brown.   Perhaps then there were two similar signs
column232: one at each railroad passenger depot.  \n\t...I think its purpose (why it wasn't taken down) was rather than trying to exclude African Americans
column233: who were excluded other ways
column234: it served as a way for \"native\" Whites to feel like they were in control when they were clearly being overrun by the irish and the italians and the \"mill hunkies.\"  They could say to themselves
column235: as they increasingly were becoming minorities
column237: at least there are no niggers here.\"\n\tHere is another variable that helps to explain the dearth of African Americans in Niles prior to WWI. There was a very large destitute horde of Russians squatting in tents and shanty houses along the Mahoning River south of the railroad tracks between Summit Street and Belmont Avenue. These Russians--and I am not sure if they were all Russian or if they were Slovak or what because there are almost no Russians living in Niles today--took all of the jobs that African Americans could hope to get
column238: unskilled labor and such.  Their presence depressed local wages for all unskilled labor.\n\tI imagine that these eastern european immigrants eventually took better jobs in the larger steel mills in Youngstown and Warren
column239: and when African Americans did move into Niles
column240: they moved into this area by the river that the Russians previously had occupied.  This area is bound by the city limits
column241: but nobody cares what goes on down there. It stinks and the insects are bad in the summer. I do not think that Klan type Whites would have objected much to African Americans squatting over in Russia Field
column242: but I don't think the Russians would have tolerated it. \n\tOne of the few African Americans to move to Niles was a man named Dixon. (could be spelled Dickson?) at least by 1940. He was a plasterer and concrete finisher. He was well respected as a skilled tradesperson

column244: which Dixon did. According to my father
column245: he lived in a house in the Russia Field area.\"\n\nNiles had a battle between the KKK and the Knights of the Flaming Circle

1/9/2002
MIKE JONES "OTTAWA HILLS RACIAL BIAS CASE SETTLED"
A controversial three year old case that sharpened perceptions that Ottawa Hills is unfriendly to African Americans was settled out of court yesterday.
	 The case
column212: filed in U.S. District Court
column213: involved black construction worker Suron Jacobs
column214: who alleged he was falsely arrested in 1998 while standing at an intersection in the upscale Toledo suburb. \t Mr. Jacobs' lawsuit contended that Ottawa Hills police had a discriminatory policy against nonresident African Americans.\n\t No monetary award was included in the entry.\n\t Alan Konop
column215: an attorney for Mr. Jacobs
column216: said his client asked that the amount remain confidential. However
column217: several reports placed the settlement at $165
column218: 000.\n\t \"Mr. Jacobs is just very glad it's over. He's a very private person and was thrust into the public arena by the acts of the Ottawa Hills police
column219: \" Mr. Konop said.\n\t Joan Szuberla
column220: attorney for the village
column221: said she would not discuss damages
column222: which were paid by an insurance company.\n\t Mr. Jacobs was arrested in September
column224: when he began to walk away from questioning by an officer at Indian and Evergreen roads.\n\t Officer Michelle Miller had been dispatched to the scene
column225: and Mr. Jacobs
column226: who was working on a renovation project at Ottawa Hills High School
column227: told her he was waiting for his brother. After the officer began to ask more questions
column228: Mr. Jacobs said he was being harassed because he is African American and began to leave.\n\t Officer Miller tried to detain him
column229: and Officer James Knallay arrived and assisted in making the arrest.\n\t The arrest and subsequent dismissal of all charges by Municipal Judge Denise Dartt ignited a controversy that the village has struggled with for three years.\n\t To improve its image and help determine whether police were ticketing minority drivers unfairly
column230: Ottawa Hills surveyed more than 18
column231: 000 drivers at five village intersections in 1999 to determine their race.\n\t At the time
column232: Village Manager Marc Thompson commented on the view of the village for minorities.\n\t \"We have known for some time that there is a perception that we are not welcoming to minorities. We have a concern
column233: and we want to make sure to be fair and just to everyone in the community
column234: \" he said.\n\t In 2000
column235: the village's human relations committee established a program in which \"diversity dinners\" were held so people of various races and ethnic origins in the village could get together and share experiences.\n\t Mr. Konop said that after Mr. Jacobs' arrest he did not have an attorney and didn't intend to file a lawsuit. He said that as the case progressed his client became determined to file the lawsuit.\n\t \"It's important to note that he went to the police department the day after the arrest to indicate he was dissatisfied with the treatment by the Ottawa Hills police and sought an apology and hoped for disciplinary action
column237: the attorney said.\n\t Village officials said they are relieved the case has been resolved.\n\t \"I'm very glad it's over
column238:
" Mayor Jean Youngen said. "It indeed was an unfortunate incident."
	 She said the village has made strides in promoting diversity and has recruited minorities when there are openings in its safety forces as well as other employment in the village.
	 She said any information concerning the amount of damages would have to be obtained from Ms. Szuberla.
	 Ms. Youngen said the village is covered by insurance for these types of lawsuits.
	 Mr. Thompson said the village is covered through the Ohio Governmental Risk Management Plan

Testimony of a former resident: 
"All my family still lives in Piketon and Waverly
column201: and my older aunt and uncle hold a wealth of information about the stories of Waverly and Pike County. In fact
column202: I don't think there's anything these two have forgotten. \n\tMy uncle has told me many times about the sign at the city corporation limits and in fact
column203: showed me the exact place the sign stood on old State Route 23.\n\tHe has also told me a story about the daughter of James Emmett (one of Waverly's founding fathers) who became pregnant by a black man in the late 1800's. This was a terrible scandal and of course
column204: the girl was disowned by her family. The story goes that the black man was hung beside the courthouse in Waverly
column205: but no blacks were allowed to be buried in the Waverly cemetery.  The story continues that they finally buried the black man over the hill from the cemetery and I think his grave is still there. There is a great story about the Emmett girl and her family.  Her father was so determined to have her forever erased fron creation that he had her birth records at the courthouse destroyed. In fact
column206: on her grave marker in the Waverly cemetery
column207: there is a statute of a girl pointing a gun directly at her father's (James Emmett) grave. I only know bits and pieces of the Emmett family story
column208: but it's very fascinating.\n\tAs far as the taxi story goes
column209: my grandfather
column210: Ray E. Pettit
column211: started and maintained the Liberty Cab Company in Waverly until his death in the 1960s. I can remember my mother telling me stories about how my grandfather would transport blacks out of the city limits
column212: should they be in town too late to make it on their own
column213:
so they would not be punished. I think many of them knew they could depend on him should they be caught short on time."


According to a local librarian:
"I don't believe there was a county in OH where the anti-abolition sentiment was stronger than in Pike County.... I can remember the time when it was not safe for a man to even whisper abolition sentiments in Pike County...Strange as it may seem
column214: the bitter anti-Negro feeling
column215: that reached its climax in the expulsion of Thompson
column216: the [abolitionist] lecturer
column217: has never been eradicated in this community
column218: and today there is not a Negro resident within the corporate limits of Waverly; and what is more
column219: a Negro has never been allowed to live in Waverly. The fact that a Negro cannot live here seems to be so thoroughly understood that no one of that race longer attempts to take up a residence here. Negro house servants are not even tolerated
column220: and a Negro barber would not be allowed to do business here for a day. Even Negro hackmen from surrounding cities who are called here on business are not allowed to linger around any longer than is absolutely necessary... This peculiarity is all the more remarkable
column221: when it is known that many of the hills surrounding Waverly are fairly infested with Negroes

According to the memoirs of one of the town's founders:
"This antipathy to the Negro at Waverly dates back to the earliest settlement of the town. When Waverly was still in its swaddling clothes
column223: there was a 'yellow nigger' named Love
column224: living on the outskirts of the town. He was a low-minded
column225: impudent
column226: vicious fellow
column227: with the cheek of a mule. He was very insulting
column228: and made enemies on every hand. His conduct finally became so objectionable
column229: that a lot of the better class of citizens got together one night
column230: made a descent upon his cabin
column231: drove him out
column232: and stoned him a long way in his flight back to Sharonville.  He never dared to come back. Our first acquaintance with Negroes about Waverly
column233: was with rather rough
column234: objectionable members of that race
column235: and many things occurred to intensify the prejudice which many of our people always held against the Negroes. Dr. William Blackstone was a strong exception to the general rule. He was a friend of the Negro
column237: and the prejudiced whites accused the doctor of 'encouraging the d---d niggers to be impudent and sassy to us.' Opposed to Blackstone was a strong family of Burkes
column238: and a number of the Downings
column239: who thought that the only correct was to treat a Negro was to kill him.... There was a splendid fellow
column240: a darkey
column241: named Dennis Hill
column242: who settled at Piketon and established a tanning business
column243: who was almost harassed to death by the Negro-haters.  He finally left this section and went to MI
column244: where he grew rich. \n\t\"A lot of VA Negroes settled up on Pee-Pee Creek
column245: in the neighborhood of the Burkes and the Downings.  Some of them prospered nicely
column246: and this enraged their white neighbors.  Tim Downing ... was the leader of the gang that made almost constant war on these Negroes.  Downing's crowd got to burning the hay and wheat of the colored farmers
column247: harassing their stock
column248: interfering in their private business
column249: and doing everything possible to make life absolutely miserable to the colored people.  They concentrated the brunt of their hatred against the most prosperous of these colored farmers...  One night they organized a big raid into the colored settlement with the avowed purpose of 'clearing out the whole nest of d---d niggers.' They went fully armed
column250: and didn't propose to stop short of doing a little killing and burning
column251: if necessary to accomplish their purpose...\"  They attacked the rich black
column252: fired upon his cabin
column253: and he fired back
column254: hit Tim Downing's brother in his right leg
column255: cutting an artery; Downing bled to death.  The jury refused to find a specific Negro guilty
column256: and the Downings \"afterward had the Nero arrested and tried for murder
column257: but he was acquitted.\"  Tim Downing lay in wait for the black farmer's son to come out of his cabin
column258: opened fire on him
column260: fracturing his skull and allowing a portion of his brain to ooze out.\"  Downing ran.  The young man finally recovered.  Then the Negro moved away. \n\tThen he tells how Downing's brother found a black man chopping fence rails.  \"He told the darkey to make his peace with God
column261: as he was going to kill him right there.  The darkey knew that Downing meant what he said
column262: and ... made a dash at Downing with his ax
column263: striking him full on the side of the face and shattering his jaw....  The Negro was arrested and tried
column264: but was acquitted.  This only enraged the white gang more
column265: and they made life in this neighborhood entirely too hot for the Negro.  It was under such circumstances as these that the bitter anti-Negro feeling at Waverly had its origin.  After this
column266: the feeling against the Negro was fostered and extended so effectually that even moderate-thinking people on any other subject came to believe that they couldn't stand the presence of a Negro in Waverly.\" \n[James Emmitt
