  %u201CPublic outcry prompts item's removal from list%u201D
   By BRAD BOLCHUNOS
   The Daily Astorian 
VERNONIA    This community's links with the Ku Klux Klan are stirring controversy   eight decades later.
 A listing of notable milestones in Vernonia's history riled some residents
because it included the KKK. But the person behind the list   No. 20 on a sign at Vernonia Lake   says it should not be ignored. 
Now the dispute has led Enid Parrow
column179: who dreamed up the sign
column180: to remove the Klan reference from the list on a public information kiosk.\n The concerns were expressed by residents were about the 1922 notation about Vernonia's first organized meeting of the KKK.\n Vernonia was by no means alone among Oregon communities which saw the KKK rise to prominence in the early 1920s
column181: fanning a hatred of immigrants
column182: communists and Catholics. Local records indicate the Klan swept the 1922 political ticket in Astoria
column183: for example
column184: where at the time the KKK published its own newspapers and had 2
column185:
000 members.
 But the reference to the Klan in Vernonia's historical display   an item residents said was neither widely noticed nor discussed when it was installed several months ago   troubled a relative newcomer to town who observed it recently.
 %u2026.
 A long time volunteer for community beautification and historic preservation through the nonprofit group Vernonia Pride
column186: Parrow selected the dates from the book
column187: \"Vernonia: A Pocket in the Woods\" by Ann Fulton of Portland State University.\n \"We're trying to promote Vernonia rather than find fault
column188: \" she said. The goal was simply to provide a roster that would sample local historical events
column189: she added.\n \"These things happened. It was part of history in Vernonia
column190: \" Parrow said. \"If people don't like the history
column191: then so be it.\"\n The portion of the display referring to the KKK has been removed by Parrow and colleagues earlier this week and something else will replace it
column192: Parrow\nsaid. She hopes people will simply drop the issue
column193: she added.  For a place trying to promote its trails
column194: parks and other amenities \"it's unfortunate there was this misunderstanding
column195: \" said City Manager Mike Sykes.\n %u2026.\n Throughout Oregon
column196: much of the Klan activity was directed primarily against Catholics
column197: said Lisa Penner
column198: curator at the Clatsop County Historical Society. In the 1920s
column199: the Ku Klux Klan spread through local political offices across the United States.\n %u2026.\n In the appropriate context
column200: remembering such troublesome times is important
column201: Penner said. A few years ago the historical society's exhibits grew to include a Klan robe and sword
column202: donated by local a resident who was startled to discover the items among the possessions of a deceased family member.\n \"We were lucky to get these items to put them on display
column203: \" Penner said. She acknowledged \"there are people not happy we mention (the subject of the Ku Klux Klan)
column204: because they would just as soon forget.\"\n But part of the effort to remember reveals how the height of local political power of the KKK was relatively short lived. It failed to flourish.\n \"It was an unhappy episode
column205: but it died quickly
column206:
" Penner said. "They didn't last very long."

column203: Richmond VA (from from Hope in the City \nwebsite
column204: 2\/2001)\n\"...Perhaps for most of you
column205: when you hear of my \nbirthplace you think of forests and salmon
column206: \nprogressive environmental and social legislation
column207: \nbut there is a deep shadow in our paradise. We who \nare of European American heritage
column208:
have been 
pretty successful in keeping people of color - 
especially African Americans - out of our state.
	"We started this project in 1849 when the 
white male settlers who made up the Oregon 
Territorial Legislature specifically prohibited 
"negros
column209: mulattos
column210: and colored persons\" from our \nstate. Now
column211: as a white woman
column212: I would love to be \nable to say that it's all the white men's fault. The \ntruth is
column213:
I don't believe that the white women of the 
community were standing outside the door of the 
room with pickets in their hand protesting the 
action.
	"... As near as I can tell from reading the 1849 
resolution
column214: this exclusion came from a place of fear   \none of the worst motivators in our human \nemotional range. The resolution essentially reads
column215: \n\"We're surrounded by Indians. We let in African \nAmericans
column216: they're going to cause trouble
column217: stir up \nthe native Americans
column218:
and then we'll all be dead.
	"I find this statement fascinating because 
buried within this law is an acknowledgment that 
the Chinook people
column219: the Klamath People
column220:
the Nez 
Pierce and all their brothers and sisters throughout 
the Americas might have a reason to be unhappy 
with the loss of their land and their way of life.
	"When Oregon finally came into the Union
column221:
we 
made a decision to be a free state rather than a 
slave state   not so much out of a concern for the 
suffering of our brothers and sisters in bondage but 
more from a sense of just 'don't let these kinds of 
people here.' No black folks
column222: no problems. Our \nstate constitution very specifically limited the \nparticipation and presence of African Americans
column223:
Asian Americans and Native Americans. Political 
and economic power was clearly to remain in 
European American control.
	"... Last spring Senator Gordly and I were 
granted an opportunity to participate in a powerful 
and symbolic act of healing
column224:
A ceremony called the 
Day of Acknowledgment. It was a symbolic act by 
Oregon's political leadership. The resolution -- 
passed by both the House and the Senate -- simply 
but clearly acknowledged Oregon's racist history. 
Sen. Gordly and I worked with many others to shape 
and move the resolution through the process. It 
was not easy nor automatic. It took a great deal of 
sensitivity and patience. But in the end
column225: It was an \namazing
column226: transforming
column227: and healing event
column228:
an hour 
long bill signing ceremony held in the House 
chambers. I asked for the opportunity to introduce 
some of our speakers and to sit at the podium 
because I wanted to see the room
column229:
I wanted to see 
the people's faces.
	"At first we reserved the desks on the floor for 
the members of the House and Senate and their 
staff. Oregonians of all ethnic and racial heritage 
sat on the side and in the gallery. They filled the 
room and overflowed to other rooms where they 
could watch on TV. As is the nature of legislative 
life
column230:
not all the members were present at the 
appointed hour so the speaker of the house invited 
the people of Oregon to come out and sit on the 
floor
column231: to take their seats in the Chamber. For a \nmoment
column232: they hesitated
column233:
and then Oregonians -- 
Oregonians of all colors and dress -- came and 
took joyful possession of their chamber. For years I 
have seen white
column234: economically successful men and \nwomen sit in those desks and I have known that \nsomething was missing
column235: that the pallet was \nsomehow too monochromatic....\n\t\"Four o'clock in the afternoon of April 22
column236: \n1999 is a moment in history where change \nhappened. And like all moments of change
column237:
it is 
only when we look back can we see how the forces 
of water and earth came together to form this new 
bend in the river. Citizens of Richmond Virginia -- 
you were there. Your work with your own walk of 
history
column238: your pioneering work in racial dialogues
column239:
came to Portland two years ago and showed us -- 
at a conference on racial issues at Portland State 
University -- that there is something we can do 
together to heal not only the wounds of the past 
but also to forge a new future. We formed dialogue 
groups based on the Hope in the Cities process and 
we were supported both financially and 
intellectually by Hope in the Cities as an ad hoc 
group of activists who came together to first plan 
the celebration of the Day of Acknowledgement and 
now to form our own non profit organization called 
Oregon Uniting.
	"We are not done. Oregon Uniting is 
continuing 
racial dialogues based on the Hope in the Cities 
discussion guide. We will be training 13 new 
facilitators in January. We will be extending our 
dialogue process out to the rest of the state....  We 
are discussing changing or adding murals to our 
1930s state capitol in order to make them more 
inclusive of all Oregonians. We are preparing to 
mark our own racial history so that we can teach it 
to ourselves and future generations. We will 
encourage the passage of similar resolutions in our 
sister territorial states of Idaho and Washington.%
u201D
 
LaLande

%u201CRacial prejudice stains the history of 
Oregon
column280:
1999
	By Gwenda Richards Oshiro of The Oregonian 
staff 
Available at: http://www.oregonlive.com/news/
99/04/st042105.html
	%u201COn Thursday
column281:
Oregon's leaders will 
take an unprecedented step toward racial healing: 
They will formally acknowledge that this state's 
history is pockmarked by discriminatory deeds. And 
they will acknowledge that the injustices endured 
by Native Americans
column285: Pacific Islanders and others \nresulted not just from individual bigotry
column286:
but also 
from laws passed by Oregon leaders and supported 
by its citizenry.
	The observance coincides with the 150th 
anniversary of one of the most discriminatory of 
laws: the Territorial Exclusionary Act
column289:
making it illegal for "negroes and 
mulattoes" to live in the Oregon Territory.
	Lawmakers said then the law was needed to 
keep blacks from "instilling (in Indians) . . . feelings 
of hostility against the white race."
	The law was never fully enforced
column292:
blacks expelled 
from the territory because of it. But the law 
discouraged many black pioneers from settling in 
Oregon
column293:
scholars say.
	Pioneer days 
It was actually the second exclusionary act passed 
by territorial lawmakers. The first was adopted in 
1844
column294:
a year after Oregon leaders passed an anti-
slavery law. The exclusionary act mandated 
whipping and then public auction of blacks who 
refused to leave. Repealed in 1845
column295: the law was \nadopted again in 1849.\n\tRepealed again in 1853
column296:
the provision to 
exclude blacks was ultimately included in the 
Oregon Constitution ratified by Congress in 1859. 
And even though the 14th Amendment to the U.S. 
Constitution in 1868 effectively superseded the 
Oregon law
column297:
the exclusionary act remained in the 
state constitution until 1926.
	It wasn't the only law that excluded minorities 
from opportunities enjoyed by Oregon's white 
pioneers. The Donation Land Act passed by 
Congress in 1850 restricted Oregon homestead 
land; often wrested from Indians
column298: to white settlers \nand \"American half-breed Indians.\" Poll taxes were \nlevied against blacks
column300: Hawaiians and \npeople of mixed races.\n\tAnd in 1866
column301: in a law that would endure until \n1951
column302: state leaders prohibited marriages between' \nwhites and those with one-quarter or more black
column303:
Chinese or Hawaiian blood or those having more 
than one-half Indian blood.
	20th century Because so few blacks lived in 
early Oregon
column304:
bias often was directed at other 
minorities.

%u201CI grew up in Oregon [1950s] after moving from my native Alabama at the age of 6.  While living in Portland I heard from older black acquaintances about "sundown towns" they passed through in southern Oregon when traveling between Oregon and California.  The two towns that I recall hearing the most about are Grants Pass and Medford.  If what I heard is true
column215: you might also find that Roseburg had a similar character.  I actually visited these towns as early as the 1950s
column216: and encountered no incidents.  While an undergraduate at the University of Oregon
column217: I spent a weekend at the home of one of my classmates in Grants Pass; but this may not be representative since his father had a local reputation as a non conformist.  Later
column218: I did find the local law officials less than friendly even in the 1960s when I drove through such towns on occasion in my visits home from graduate school in Berkeley.  Their attitude could
column219: however
column220: have just been reflective of a general suspiciousness of obvious outsiders
column221: rather than any type of racial bias.\n\tThe most graphic descriptions I recall from older people who had experienced the racism directly were of signs they read posted on the highway.  One in Grants Pass allegedly read: \"Nigger
column222: don't let the sun go down on you in this county.\"  Another I heard about
column223: regarding a town I no longer recall
column224: was said to have read: \"Nigger
column225: read this and keep running.\"  To place this somewhat in context
column226: despite Oregon's well-deserved reputation as a pioneering progressive republican state with a strong tradition of political and
column227: to some extent
column228: social liberalism
column229: it was also at one point a bastion of the Ku Klux Klan.  In this instance the main groups targeted were Jews
column230: Asians
column231: Mexicans and Indians
column232: rather than Blacks
column233: because the number of Blacks present was negligible before the war industries transported thousands of families like mine there in the early 1940s.  That other type of racism produced such signs as: \"No dogs
column234: Jews
column235: or Chinamen
column236: \"  which I have only seen in photographs
column237: not in action.\n\t\"Blacks were afraid to drive through Grants Pass or Medford.\"  In the mid-1960s
column238: \"a black friend of mine put a loaded pistol on the front seat of his car when he drove through those towns.\"  \"Not until the late 1960s or 1970s could black people feel safe and secure in taking long car trips.\" (owing to regular segregation)\n\tIn Medford:  The police stopped the car in which I was getting a ride
column239: and we were not speeding
column240: but were a mixed car
column241: two whites and a black
column242: and they searched the car cursorily.  \"Where are you going?\"  \"What are you carrying?\"  Made us get out of the car.  \"The first thought that came to my mind was the fact that I'm black.\"  My friends had never been stopped before

without me.%u201D

column176: An Oregon resident writes:\n\t\"After WWII one of the best Chinese restaurants in the state started up outside the city limits of McMinnville because they wouldn't let the owner hold property inside the city.  When the city annexed the land around the restaurant he sold out and moved to Corvallis rather than be a citizen of that town.\n\tIt was illegal for Orientals to own property in many cities until after the Korean War
column177: though there was no state wide law forbidding it.  That's why there are so few minorities in Oregon   they weren't welcome
column178: and the  law made it almost impossible to live here.\n\tThere is a wealth of material for you here in Oregon.  The entire state was a \"sundown\" state.  Exclusion laws didn't really break down until the beginning of WWII.  When Oregon became a state in 1859
column179: blacks had 2 weeks to make it from one border to the other
column180: quite a trick at the time.  Up until the 1930's
column181: eugenics laws were in force.  Mental patients and some criminals were sterilized so their genes would not contaminate the population.  After the Civil War
column182: thousands of southerners who were carpet bagged out of their land in the south moved west to start over.  For decades
column183: one of the strongest forces in Oregon politics was the KKK. \n\tOne of the most shameful episodes in state history was the expulsion of the Chinese after the railroads were built.  Thousands of Chinese were rounded up and shipped back to China
column184: including natural born American citizens.  In 1870
column185: every town near a railroad had Chinese laundries
column186: Chinese servants and other laborers.  By 1890 they were all gone.  I have heard that the ships didn't actually take them back to China
column187: they just sailed out into the Pacific
column188: threw the Chinese overboard
column189: and sailed back for another load. That is just a rumor.  I have not been able to verify its truth
column190: but I would not be surprised. \n\tThe eugenics laws faded out in the 1930's.  To the credit of the medical profession
column191: the state was unable to find any doctors willing to perform the sterilization procedures.  The shipyards at Swan Island brought the first viable black population to Oregon.  At the time
column192: there was very little protest.  When I was growing up in the 1950's
column193: race was not really on my social horizon.  I was a freshman in college before I ever spoke with a black person
column194: though I did compete against a couple in high school athletics.\n\tBecause of the war with Japan
column195: the prejudice against Orientals lasted longer than the prejudice against blacks.  Towns like McMinnville continued to exclude Orientals while allowing black people to live inside the city limits.  The particular oriental you are asking about owned the Oriental Garden restaurant
column196: which I believe is still in operation.  The guy's name was Roger
column197: and I think his last name was Chen or Chong.  He was a native born American citizen.  I had an aunt and uncle who were good friends with him
column198: which is how I heard the story.  The Oriental Garden was known for its excellent food
column199: and was quite a commercial success.\"\n\n

column176: Medford was a sundown town
column177: said to have had a sign until the 1960s!  See below.  \n\nMedford residents openly discuss discrimination against non-whites
column178: but disagree whether there were official %u201Csundown%u201D laws.\n\nIn the Medford Mail Tribune News is a section entitled %u201CSince You Asked
column179:
%u201D which printed the following about sundown laws in 1998:

%u201C%u2018Sundown law%u2019 hasn't been found 
    During the 10 years I have lived in Southern Oregon
column180: I have had many discussions with teachers
column181: lawyers
column182: police officers and lifelong residents of Medford on the history of African Americans here. Inevitably the topic of \"sundown\" laws comes up. Were there actually laws that excluded blacks and other ethnic groups from residing in Medford and other surrounding communities? Or was this an unspoken custom? %u2014 Lewis J.
column183: Gold Hill\n\tThere's no evidence that sundown laws actually existed on the statute books anywhere in Oregon
column184: Lewis
column185: but that didn't make the discrimination any less real. \n\t\"I have never been able to find a written sundown law
column186: \" said Darrell Millner
column187: professor of black studies at Portland State University. \"I'm still looking
column188: but I haven't found any.\" \n\tHowever
column189: Millner said there were often signs posted outside towns where nonwhite strangers weren't welcome. \n\tArticle 35 of Oregon's 1859 constitution prohibited black people from residing here. Things began to change after the Civil War
column190: when Congress approved the 14th Amendment to the Constitution
column191: which guaranteed equal protection to all citizens under the law
column192: and by extension gave black people the right to reside in Oregon. \n\tMillner said Oregon did not drop the discriminatory language from the state constitution until 1927. \n\tMillner said many Confederate sympathizers drifted in and out of Oregon during the War Between the States
column193: and large numbers of them settled in Southern Oregon after the war ended in 1865. \n\tAs recently as the 1920s
column194: few black people lived in Southern Oregon. Bill Alley of the Southern Oregon Historical Society says one black man
column195: George Washington Maddox
column196: was often noted in newspaper columns of the day. Maddox
column197: a dwarf
column198: shined shoes at the Medford Hotel. \n\tAlley said there were probably more Japanese living in the Rogue Valley than blacks in the 1920s
column199: but the entire nonwhite population was still very small. \"When we talk about the `Japanese community' we might be talking about two families.\" \n\t(Send your questions to \"Since You Asked

column218: Oregon
column219: from 1930 to 1933 in the context of past local populism since the 1890's and concurrent nationwide political protest during the early depression.  The depression in Jackson's lumber and orchard economy in 1930 reawakened populist dissent and pitted one faction of the county   small orchard operators
column220: blue collar workers

column222: and the scattered population of the hinterlands   against urban Medford and the orchard owning elite.  In the fraud ridden election of 1932

Below is the text of a speech given by Anitra K. Rasmussen
column200: 11th District
column201: Oregon State Legislature. Location: Metropolitan Richmond Day
column202: November 18
column204: Richmond VA (from from Hope in the City website
column205: 2\/2001)\n\n\"....It is our stories
column206: our history that brings us together this morning. History already recorded
column207: and history waiting to be made. I come from a part of the country where written history
column208: European American history
column209: is very thin. We are not quite 200 years away from the great Lewis and Clark expedition to the west coast
column210: and almost all of us are from somewhere else within the last two generations. It's not that there isn't history spanning millennia in my state
column211: it's just that it wasn't recorded in a way that was respected by the European American settlers of the last century
column212: and thus almost all was lost as entire tribes died due to white men's diseases.\n\tI enjoy thinking about history. I enjoy thinking about how it was that we came to be here now. I think that careful examination of our history gives meaning to our present lives and grounds our future choices. But not all of our history is pleasant or heroic. Our personal history
column213: our family history
column214: our cultural history all hold events that must be healed in order to move forward in health and wholeness. So this morning
column215: I'm going to tell you some history. Some of it is my own personal history and some of it I must claim simply because of where I live. And then I will tell you how history was changed on one afternoon in April of last year.\n\t ....Perhaps for most of you
column216: when you hear of my birthplace you think of forests and salmon
column217: progressive environmental and social legislation
column218: but there is a deep shadow in our paradise. We who are of European American heritage
column219: have been pretty successful in keeping people of color   especially African Americans   out of our state.\n\tWe started this project in 1849 when the white male settlers who made up the Oregon Territorial Legislature specifically prohibited \"negros
column220: mulattos
column221: and colored persons\" from our state. Now
column222: as a white woman
column223: I would love to be able to say that it's all the white men's fault. The truth is
column224: I don't believe that the white women of the community were standing outside the door of the room with pickets in their hand protesting the action.\n\t.... As near as I can tell from reading the 1849 resolution
column225: this exclusion came from a place of fear   one of the worst motivators in our human emotional range. The resolution essentially reads
column226: \"We're surrounded by Indians. We let in African Americans
column227: they're going to cause trouble
column228: stir up the native Americans
column229: and then we'll all be dead.\n\tI find this statement fascinating because buried within this law is an acknowledgment that the Chinook people
column230: the Klamath People
column231: the Nez Pierce and all their brothers and sisters throughout the Americas might have a reason to be unhappy with the loss of their land and their way of life.\n\tWhen Oregon finally came into the Union
column232: we made a decision to be a free state rather than a slave state   not so much out of a concern for the suffering of our brothers and sisters in bondage but more from a sense of just 'don't let these kinds of people here.' No black folks
column233: no problems. Our state constitution very specifically limited the participation and presence of African Americans
column234: Asian Americans and Native Americans. Political and economic power was clearly to remain in European American control.\n\t.... Last spring Senator Gordly and I were granted an opportunity to participate in a powerful and symbolic act of healing
column235: A ceremony called the Day of Acknowledgment. It was a symbolic act by Oregon's political leadership. The resolution -- passed by both the House and the Senate -- simply but clearly acknowledged Oregon's racist history. Sen. Gordly and I worked with many others to shape and move the resolution through the process. It was not easy nor automatic. It took a great deal of sensitivity and patience. But in the end
column237: transforming
column238: and healing event
column239: an hour long bill signing ceremony held in the House chambers. I asked for the opportunity to introduce some of our speakers and to sit at the podium because I wanted to see the room
column240: I wanted to see the people's faces.\n\tAt first we reserved the desks on the floor for the members of the House and Senate and their staff. Oregonians of all ethnic and racial heritage sat on the side and in the gallery. They filled the room and overflowed to other rooms where they could watch on TV. As is the nature of legislative life
column241: not all the members were present at the appointed hour so the speaker of the house invited the people of Oregon to come out and sit on the floor
column242: to take their seats in the Chamber. For a moment
column243: they hesitated
column244: and then Oregonians -- Oregonians of all colors and dress -- came and took joyful possession of their chamber. For years I have seen white
column245: economically successful men and women sit in those desks and I have known that something was missing
column246: that the pallet was somehow too monochromatic....\n\tFour o'clock in the afternoon of April 22
column247: 1999 is a moment in history where change happened. And like all moments of change
column248: it is only when we look back can we see how the forces of water and earth came together to form this new bend in the river. Citizens of Richmond Virginia -- you were there. Your work with your own walk of history
column249: your pioneering work in racial dialogues
column250:
came to Portland two years ago and showed us -- at a conference on racial issues at Portland State University -- that there is something we can do together to heal not only the wounds of the past but also to forge a new future. We formed dialogue groups based on the Hope in the Cities process and we were supported both financially and intellectually by Hope in the Cities as an ad hoc group of activists who came together to first plan the celebration of the Day of Acknowledgement and now to form our own non profit organization called Oregon Uniting.
	We are not done. Oregon Uniting is continuing racial dialogues based on the Hope in the Cities discussion guide. We will be training 13 new facilitators in January. We will be extending our dialogue process out to the rest of the state....  We are discussing changing or adding murals to our 1930s state capitol in order to make them more inclusive of all Oregonians. We are preparing to mark our own racial history so that we can teach it to ourselves and future generations. We will encourage the passage of similar resolutions in our sister territorial states of Idaho and Washington.%u201D

column251: Jeff.  \"DIXIE\" OF THE PACIFIC NORTHWEST: SOUTHERN OREGON'S CIVIL WAR.  Oregon Historical Quarterly 1999 100(1): 32 81.  Abstract: Interest in Civil War events was intense among residents of the Pacific Northwest
column252: despite their geographical isolation from the conflict.  Official war correspondence and eyewitness accounts dominated the region's newspapers.  Arguments over states' rights and slavery were especially bitter in southern Oregon whose population was comprised mainly of settlers from Missouri
column253: Kentucky
column254: and the conservative districts of Ohio
column255: Indiana
column256: and Illinois where residents
column257: fearful of a despotic central government run by abolitionist and urban capitalists
column258: were sympathetic to the Confederacy.  During this period the region's voters gained a reputation as \"prone to extremes.\" Though they abhorred the idea of a strong central government
column259:
most residents were Unionists at heart.




Slavery in the Oregon Country
	Although slavery was declared illegal in 1844 by the Oregon Provisional Government
column260: many people brought slaves with them across the Oregon Trail. Due to the length and difficulty of the journey
column261: most slave owners brought only a few slaves West    often a single longtime family servant and generally no more than a family of slaves
column262: or at least those members deemed able to survive the trip.\n\tThe ban on slavery in the Oregon Country had nothing to do with abolitionist leanings. In fact
column263: the opposite was true: slavery was outlawed as a means of keeping the black population in Oregon to a minimum. Owning slaves was widely tolerated in the Northwest
column264: and while some slaves successfully sued for their freedom or the freedom of loved ones
column265: no whites were ever forced to free their slaves upon entering the Oregon Country after 1844. However
column266: some did follow through on promises to free their slaves upon arriving in Oregon
column267: and a small population of free blacks gradually became established in the Pacific Northwest.\n\tSlaves were too valuable for many owners to willingly give them up. Nathaniel Ford
column268: an emigrant of 1844
column269: brought with him three slaves: Robin and Polly Holmes
column270: and their daughter Mary Jane. Ford had promised to free the Holmes family after he had established himself in Oregon
column271: but in 1850 he agreed to free only Robin
column272: Polly
column273: and their infant son    the Fords kept three other children
column274: including Mary Jane. After a long court battle
column275: the other children were freed by order of the court in 1853. The judge cited the fact that slavery was illegal in the Oregon Territory as the reason for his decision. Although she had been freed
column276: Mary Jane Holmes continued to live with the Fords until her marriage in 1857
column277: and her husband had to pay Nathaniel Ford $750 for permission to marry her.\n\tNot all relationships between slave and master were so antagonistic. In 1849
column278: Rose Jackson willingly traveled the Oregon Trail inside a box in the back of a wagon
column279: coming out only at night to stretch and get some fresh air. Her owners credited her work as a laundress with helping the family survive their first winter in Oregon.\n\tRacism became more widespread and severe along the West Coast in the years leading up to the Civil War. The California legislature almost passed exclusion laws twice
column280: prompting many free blacks to head north to British Columbia. Some slaves still held in the old Oregon Country escaped north
column282: a boy held by James Tilton
column283: Surveyor General of the Washington Territory
column286: and was protected by the British. Nothing came of Tilton's protests to the Secretary of State in Washington
column287: DC.\n\tPro slavery groups agitated repeatedly to form a new federal Territory    and eventually a new slave state    out of what is now southwestern Oregon and northern California. Significant gold strikes in that area in the early 1850s lent some weight to the forces backing this plan
column288: but the first effort in 1854 was thwarted when California refused to cede any of its land. A second attempt in 1857 also failed.\n\tAlso in 1857
column289: a bill was proposed in the Territorial Legislature to protect the property of slave owners. While ostensibly intended to protect the interests of slave owners passing through the Territory
column290: it was almost certainly intended to legalize slavery by allowing slave owners to keep their slaves after moving to the Oregon Territory. Forces opposed to the bill defeated it by arguing that it would grant special rights to slave owners which were not enjoyed by the citizens of Oregon.\n\tWith statehood imminent in 1858
column291: Oregonians elected their first slate of state officers and legislators. Pro slavery forces made an excellent showing at the polls
column292: as Governor \"Honest John\" Whiteaker and other officials elected that year were well known for their views on blacks. Still
column293: Governor Whiteaker was judged to have ably guided the newly minted State of Oregon through the Civil War. One of his contemporaries
column296: \"Old Whit ... Wrong in the head in politics
column297: he is honest and right in the heart.\"\n\tOregon's early racial politics were dominated by a wish to simply be free of the problem altogether by not allowing blacks to settle here. There was a degree of consensus between the pro slavery and abolitionist sides to ignore the problem and hope it stayed in the East. Ignoring the problem
column299: gave tacit approval to scofflaws who continued to hold slaves after settling in Oregon.\n\tExclusion Laws\n\tIn June
column301: the Provisional Government of Oregon enacted its first laws regarding the status of slaves
column303: in the Oregon Country. Slavery was declared to be illegal
column304: and settlers who currently owned slaves were required to free them within three years. Any free blacks age 18 or older had to leave the area
column305: men within two years and women within three. Black children were permitted to stay in the Oregon Country until they reached age 18.\n\tThe original exclusion law was the infamous \"Lash Law\" which subjected blacks found guilty of violating the law to whippings    no less than 20 and no more than 39 strokes of the lash    every six months \"until he or she shall quit the territory.\" It was soon recognized that this punishment was far too severe
column306: and the law was modified before it went into effect.\n\tThe new version
column309: replaced the whippings with forced labor. If a black person was tried and found guilty of being in the Oregon Country illegally
column310: he or she was to be hired out publicly to whomever would employ them for the shortest amount of time. After the period of forced labor expired
column311: the \"employer\" had six months to get the black individual out of Oregon. Failure to do so was punishable by a fine of $1000. This law was to go into effect in 1846
column312: by which time those who wrote it doubtless hoped that most blacks would have left Oregon
column313: but it was repealed in the 1845 session of the Provisional Legislature.\n\tAnother exclusion law was passed in September
column315: which simply forbade blacks from settling in the newly declared Oregon Territory. Any already in residence were permitted to stay. In 1851
column317: a Salem boarding house and saloon owner
column318: became the only person known to have been exiled from the Territory under Oregon's exclusion laws. The law under which he was charged and sentenced was repealed in 1854.\n\tOregon ratified its state constitution in November
column319: 1857. On the popular ballot for the constitution
column320: there were also two other referendum issues on which citizens were asked to vote. Oregonians rejected slavery but approved adding a new exclusion law to the constitution. This law became part of Oregon's original Bill of Rights.\n\tWhen Oregon's constitution was submitted to Congress for approval
column321: some Northern legislators complained about the exclusion law. However
column322: others saw it as a structured way to avoid bloodshed over racial issues and the spread of slavery. Thus
column325: Oregon became the only state admitted to the Union with an exclusion law in its constitution. After several unsuccessful attempts
column326: the state constitution was finally amended in 1926 to remove the exclusion law from the state Bill of Rights. Over 60
column327: 000 voters declined to vote on the issue when casting their ballots. A separate clause in Oregon's constitution banning black suffrage was repealed the following year. Of course
column328: these laws had long since been superseded by federal laws and amendments to the US Constitution following the Civil War
column329: but they remained enshrined in the state constitution for 60 years.\n\tExclusion laws seems bizarre and reprehensible today
column330: but they were not uncommon in the Nineteenth Century. Settlers in the Oregon Country brought the idea with them from their old homes in Missouri
column333: and Ohio. Both Illinois and Indiana had exclusion laws on the books in the Nineteenth Century
column334: and all four states denied free blacks the right to vote and restricted their ability to testify in court. Some state and local laws required blacks to post a bond to guarantee their good behavior or to produce proof of their freedom upon the demand of any white person.\n\tThe fact that exclusion laws were enacted in Oregon was attractive to some prospective emigrants. Oregon seemed so far away from the United States in the 1840s that some of the settlers probably thought that they really did have a chance to avoid the issues of race and slavery by simply legislating away people of African descent. Some Oregonians supported the laws because they feared the violence that surrounded the politics of slavery in the East; others feared that enslaved blacks would take their jobs if they were brought West in significant numbers; still others were simply out and out racists.\n\tFollowing the killings at the Whitman Mission in 1847
column335: a wave of racial paranoia swept through the Willamette Valley. Many Oregonians convinced themselves that blacks and Indians might collaborate
column336: joining forces to wipe out all the whites in the Oregon Country. Some went so far as to argue that without the exclusion laws
column337: African Americans and Native Americans might intermarry and eventually reduce the white population to a threatened minority. As noted
column338: the modified \"Lash Law\" was repealed in 1845; the fear following the Whitman Massacre led to the exclusion law of 1848.\n\tThere was no organized abolitionist movement in Oregon the way there was in the East
column339: but many white friends of black settlers submitted petitions to the Provisional and Territorial Legislatures asking for exemptions for their friends. In addition
column340: there were many petitions to repeal the exclusion laws submitted through the years. They even succeeded once or twice
column341:
but the laws were never out of force for long.

Former Seneca Resident re: Salem: 
	I am originally from Seneca
column187: SC (located approximately 15 minutes from Salem).  I grew up in Seneca and lived there till about 1997.  As far back as I can remember
column188: I'd always heard that Salem was a prejudice town and that black people were not allowed.\n\tI mostly heard this from friends
column189: other students in high school (I graduated from Seneca High School in 1993)
column190:
and my parents may have mentioned it a few times.  Seneca and surrounding towns (except Salem) had a good ratio of blacks to whites.  I remember there being what seemed like half black and half white in my high school.  A lot of the black folks that I went to school with believed Salem to be very prejudice and would never go there.  They were fearful of what might happen if they went there.  After I graduated I worked for a manufacturing company (at the time called Dynacast) and worked with a fellow that lived in Long Creek SC.  He would always tell me about how Salem was not a place for black people.  He even recalled there being a sign in Salem that made reference to "whites only" but had been torn down.  Unfortunately I have not kept in contact with this person over the years.   
	Everyone around me that I associated with during that time seemed to have the same prespective about Salem (black or white).  That was 6 to 7 years ago.  I have not heard anything about Salem since I moved away from SC.  I certainly hope that things have changed in Salem.

column176: email 11\/2007:\r\n\r\nMessage: Shandon was an early 20th century streetcar suburb of Columbia.  It is not surprising that it would have been a sundown town at the time.  Shandon has long been integrated into the City of Columbia proper
column177: and is now in fact a relatively progressive in-town urban neighborhood near the University of South Carolina.  However
column178: I don't perceive it as a particularly racially diverse area - it is the closest thing to a \"white liberal yuppie\" neighborhood as you'll find in Columbia
column179: at least by Deep South standards (i.e.
column180: this is not quite Boulder
column181: Colorado or Northampton
column182: MA
column183:
but comes closest in South Carolina's decidedly different socio-cultural context).  Home prices in this picturesque neighborhood are pricey by Columbia standards - this is a gentrified area.  African-Americans by and large live in other areas of Columbia.

***

Shandon(now an in town suburb of Columbia) advertised itself as the "whitest town in America" when built in the 20s. See John Hammond Moore's history of Columbia and Richland County. Shandon was no longer a town by 1990.

profiting by

column211:
1915 
NORTH CHATTANOOGA IS EXCLUSIVELY WHITE 
NOW.
Chattanooga Daily Times
Segregation Ordinance Passes Third and Final 
Reading.
	In North Chattanooga it is against the law for a 
negro [sic] and white person to live on the same 
block.  That is to say
column212:
a negro [sic] cannot occupy a 
house on a block where the majority of residents 
are white people.  Neither can a white person live 
on a block where the majority of resident are 
negroes [sic].  As there are now no negroes [sic] in 
North Chattanooga
column213:
it might be called the only town 
of its size in the country where the population is 
exclusively white.     
	This ruling became a law last night at the 
meeting of the city commissioners.  The ordinance 
was prepared sometime ago by Mayor Voigt and 
passed its third and final reading last night. The 
ordinance was carefully written
column214: based on similar \nordinances in other cities
column215:
but with all the 
objectionable or unconstitutional features 
eliminated.  It places restriction on one race as 
much as it does the other
column216:
the one that happens to 
be in the majority receiving the chief benefit. [sic].   
	Mayor Voigt has received many compliments 
on his segregation ordinance.  The North 
Chattanoogans seem to feel proud of the fact they 
not only are to soon have a complete sewerage 
system
column217: but they are also the only exclusively white \ncity of its size in the state.  When Jan. 1 [1916] rolls \naround
column218: and it will not be very long
column219: either
column220: it will be \nagainst the law for hogs to run around on the \nstreets of North Chattanooga.   In fact
column221:
it will be 
illegal for anyone living within the corporate limits  
of the city to raise hogs on their property.   
[reproduced on SouthernHistory.net]

column176: \"One Negro family moved into Oneida in 1925
column177: but dynamite was dropped on their house and they were severely injured.  They soon left and no others ever came in. One Negro hobo [between the world wars] got off a freight train in Oneida; police and civilians started toward him and he started running. His bullet-riddled body was brought back out of the woods in about an hour. One young pilot from Scott County in WWII who saw the Negro after he was killed remarked on his return from the War
column178: 'You know
column179: as I watched the blood flow from the wounds of the dead and dying Negroes on our transport planes
column180: I thought of that old Negro who was killed in Oneida.'  Living in one world and seeing all Americans making the supreme sacrifice for America has taught the younger generation that the color of a man's skin has nothing to do with what's in his heart. There is not a colored family living in Scott County at the present time.\" \n[Esther S. Sanderson

column176:
Many towns in Comanche County are or were 
sundown towns.

   In 1875
column177: a black man named Moses Jones killed two \nwhite and two black children and set fire to his white \nemployer's house. He was apprehended and shot
column178: but \nwhite townspeople determined that he was the only \n\"mean Negro\" in Comanche County
column179:
"so the others 
were allowed to remain."

   Nearly a decade later
column181: another local black \nman was lynched for the killing of a white woman. \nDuring his lynching
column182: a local white man announced \n\"Boys
column183:
this is the second killing of white people by 
Negroes and it's more than the people will put up 
with.  I propose we give the Negroes a reasonable time 
to get out of the county %u2014 never allow them to 
return
column184: and never allow one of color to settle here.  All \nwho are in favor of my proposition
column185:
come close about 
this stump." The entire audience crowded around his 
stump. The black population was given ten days to get 
out. A few white people attempted to protect their 
black servants
column186:
but it was ineffectual. Black porters on 
the Houston & Texas Central would hide in the 
baggage car when the train went through Comanche 
County.

   A 1907 pamphlet for the county advertised "the 
population of Comanche County
column187: Texas
column188: according to \nthe census of 1900 was 23
column189: 079. This population
column190: it \nmust be remembered
column191: is entirely and absolutely all \nwhite; there is not a negro in the county
column192:
and the 
chances are there will not be any for many years to 
come. There are many ways in which the negro can be 
of service to the white race
column193:
but as a rule the 
community is better off without him. Wherever 
negroes are numerous there crime abounds and all 
sorts of trouble. But in Comanche County he is an 
unknown quantity
column194: and the court dockets are \nconsequently light
column195: there are no election frauds
column196: and \nwomen are safe. Wherever the negro herds
column197: there \ncrime has a breeding place.  Comanche county is free \nfrom this black curse
column198: not by any written statute that \nshe has enacted
column199: but by an unwritten law which the \nnegroes throughout the length and breadth of the \nState understand.\"

Highland Park is one of Dallas's most exclusive \nsuburbs. President George W. Bush lived there at \none time
column177: and Dick Cheney still maintains a home in \nHighland Park. When it was developed in 1913
column178: \nrestrictive covenants applied to every home. After \nBrown v. Board of Education in 1954
column179:
Highland Park 
sent its few black students to school in Dallas 
rather than allow them to attend Highland Park 
schools. Eventually this was overturned on the basis 
of Texas's desegregation laws
column180:
to which an 
alderman suggested that the city ask homeowners 
to fire their live-in servants (the parents of those 
black schoolchildren). In 1961
column181: the city of Dallas \nstopped accepting children from the suburbs
column182:
and 
at least one white employer paid rent for a Dallas 
address for her black servant's children.

   In 1981
column183: 104 people filed a class-action lawsuit \nagainst the town
column184:
alledging discrimination and 
racial profiling by police against African Americans 
and Hispanics. The police often charged people of 
color with being "drunk in car"
column185:
a crime which the 
plaintiff's lawyers pointed out was not actually on 
the books. Although the police denied the 
allegations
column186: the Justic Department became involved
column187:
and reached an agreement with the town to halt the 
practice.

   A black couple who purchased a house in 
Highland Park in 2003 are believed to be the first 
black homeowners in the city. According to a June 
2003 Newsweek web article
column188: the local paper ran a \nstory about the couple on the front page
column189:
with the 
lead "Guess who's coming to dinner? and staying 
for a while?" The article also referred to the female 
of the couple as "girl". The woman
column190: Karen Watson
column191:
told Newsweek she was "disappointed" with the 
lead but happy that race and racial discrimination in 
housing were being discussed. Watson is a 
mortgage officer and reported that she had seen 
racial discrimination in her work.

   The city has a reputation for exclusivity applied 
any outsiders
column192:
not just people of color. Eating lunch 
and picnicing is forbidden the local parks. The city 
also required fishing permits to fish in any waters 
within Highland Park
column193:
which violates Texas law as a 
state fishing permit is good for all public waters in 
Texas and all of the waters within Highland Park are 
public. Usage of tennis courts in the public parks is 
forbidden to Dallas residents. Two white graduate 
students were arrested for violating this ordinance 
and offered the choice of a $5 fine or a night in jail. 
The students chose jail.

"Significantly below" was in bold.
David Adams
column181: co owner of Bigg Homes
column182: removed the racial information from the site after being contacted by The Salt Lake Tribune on Thursday.\r\n\"We apologize if that offended anybody. It wasn't our intention
column183: \" he said. \"Frankly

column188: \" Adams said. \"We asked him to remove it.\"\r\nThat was two months ago. \"It wasn't done. It's done now

column178: 2005:\nA recent Tribune article (\"Murray Racism Claim Is Disputed\") quotes Murray historian Su Richards calling my book
column179: Sundown Towns
column180: \"bad history\" because it included Murray in a listing of towns in the West that in their history kept out African Americans.  \n\nI based this mention of Murray on Larry Gerlach's Blazing Crosses in Zion:  The Ku Klux Klan in Utah.  Gerlach tells how
column181: \"during the night of March 30
column183: a group of Murray men\" terrorized Murray's only black resident
column184: Jim Bing
column185: a shoeshiner.  They dressed up in Klan sheets
column186: burst in while he slept in the shoe repair shop where he worked
column187: captured him
column188: beat him \"severely
column189: \" and then took him to the city cemetery.  There they forced him to \"'offer up prayers for his ancestors' while 'kneeling upon a grave with his hands clasped around a tombstone.'\"  \n\nSince Bing had only recently come to Murray
column190: he surely received this harassment because he was its \"lone black resident
column191: \" as Gerlach puts it.  When he left town soon after
column192: Murray reverted to its all-white status
column193: and not accidentally.  My definition of sundown town is \"a town that was for some years all-white on purpose.\"  If Gerlach can be believed
column194: Murray qualifies.\n\nThe article tells that Utah historian Ronald Coleman was surprised I had listed Murray as a sundown town \"because descendants of a prominent black family
column195: the Bankheads
column196: still live in the city today.\"  The manuscript census indeed reveals that the Bankheads lived in Murray in 1900
column197: along with several other black families.  By 1910
column198: however
column199: the published census lists only four African Americans in Murray
column200: only two of whom can readily be located in the manuscript census
column201: including Carrie Bankhead
column202: a servant in a white household.  The rest of the Bankheads lived elsewhere
column203: most in Salt Lake City.  \n\nBy 1920
column204: the Census shows zero African Americans in Murray
column205: and none again in 1930.  Murray's demography thus resembles other sundown towns across the North
column206: many of whom had small black populations until some point in the period 1890-1940.  It is possible
column207: of course
column208: that the census was wrong and missed African American residents of Murray
column209: including the Bankheads
column210: but so far
column211: I think the preponderance of the evidence is that
column212: formally or informally
column213: Murray did keep out blacks for some years around the 1920s and '30s.  \n\nIn this regard
column214: Murray was not unique or even unusual.  I suspect a number of other Utah towns did the same thing.  I suggest readers get Sundown Towns
column215: read it
column216: check out their own community in this regard
column217: and then send me the results
column218:
positive or negative.%u201D

Historian of Murray City writes:

1. The first African American settlers arrived in South Cottonwood in 1848 as slaves of Mormon settlers from the southeastern United States (the "Mississippi Saints"). These included the Flake
column251: Lay
column252: Crosby and Bankhead families. In fact
column253: three African Americans
column254: later associated with South Cottonwood
column255: were members of the \"Pioneer Company\" which arrived in the Salt Lake Valley in July 1847. This is generally agreed among authorities on Utah's pioneer era history. \n\n2. Inferential evidence exists that one of the first three settlers in South Cottonwood was an African American (Green Flake
column256: who was also a member of the 1847 Pioneer Company). This is supported by documentary evidence in the form of testimony given under oath during an important water rights case tried between 1907 and 1914.\n\n3. Several African Americans did leave of their own volition (no record of any attempt to drive anyone out) when their owners moved on to settle San Bernardino in 1850; others relocated (again
column257: of their own volition) to other Utah settlements; and some (e.g.
column258: the Bankheads) remained in the South Cottonwood. The Bankheads are
column260: today one of Utah's most \"senior\" of pioneer families. (Slavery was abolished in Utah in 1862.)\n\n4. There is considerable evidence that
column261: by the time Murray was incorporated in 1903
column262: there was a small established African American community in Murray with more living in the immediate area. Although there was discernable discrimination toward African Americas by their white neighbors
column263: those descended from the pioneer generation were deemed to be \"old residents\" and not regarded as objects of either suspicion or derision.\n\n5. There is positive evidence from the local newspaper (American Eagle) that discrimination against African Americans was viewed with disfavor by many whites and those who practiced blatant prejudice were subject to some measure of public censure. There is also evidence
column264: in both the local newspaper and the minutes of the city council
column265: that some (but probably not all) members of the African American community were accorded a conditional measure of personal respect
column266: held to be responsible citizens
column267: and entitled to the benefit of doubt and due process when accused of improper conduct. \n\n6. In 1903
column268: an African American constable was appointed to \"serve among his own people without pay.\" Discrimination? Certainly. A move in the right direction? Just as certainly. When the constable was discharged for exceeding his authority (he had disciplined a white child during a public disturbance)
column269: he request and was granted a hearing before the city council. Upon on his representation that he had acted under written authority from the town marshal (which the marshal confirmed
column270: at the same time recommending constable be reinstatement)
column271: the council reversed itself and reinstated the constable  without pay. Significantly
column272: the council apparently let stand marshal's instructions regarding the constable's authority to discipline whites. \n\n7. No documentary or antidotal evidence has been discovered which suggests the city council enacted legislation requiring African Americans to be absent from the city at specified times
column273: or imposing restrictions on their movement inside the city  other than various curfew ordinances applicable to all juveniles. Neither is there evidence of an \"unwritten\" Jim Crow Code in Murray. This is not to say that particular African Americans may not
column275: have been ordered out of town. There are many news reports of vagrants
column277: known petty criminals and other \"trouble makers\" being escorted to the streetcar and sent on their way at city expense. Some of these people may have been African American but
column278: in all cases where the race or ethnic background of the exiled offender is mentioned
column279: none appear to have been African American. \n\n8. In point of fact
column280: there was ethnic discrimination practiced in Murray through the early years of the 20th century
column281: but the target of that discrimination appears overwhelmingly to have been \"Greeks\" (a category which generally included Italians) and \"Austrians\" (a generic designation assigned to Slavic peoples but which did not usually include German speaking subjects of the Austro Hungarian Empire). Even so
column282: one city attorney went on record to advise the city council that enacting discriminatory legislation toward the \"Greeks\" was a \"bad policy.\" And it is at least arguable
column284: that the motive for such discrimination was less ethnic that economic. These \"new immigrant\" groups comprised the bulk of Murray's itinerant industrial proletariat of that day
column285: a class historically regarded in Utah with suspicion and disdain. \n\n9. Sources documenting the status of African Americans in Murray
column286: South Cottonwood and Salt Lake County include:  Third District Court records (in particular The Progress Company
column287: et al. v. Salt Lake City
column288: et al.) County land and mining district records. Murray City records (Minutes of the City Council and Compiled Ordinances of Murray City). Newspaper articles (American Eagle
column291:
Deseret Evening News/Deseret News). Anecdotal evidence (personal histories and reminiscences published in various formats).%u201D
	

	%u201CIt is my understanding that members of the Bankhead family resided in (or near) Murray from the late 1840s until the present.  If there was a "window of absence" it was brief and not the result of forceful expulsion. Moreover
column292: there is reason to believe the Bankheads were not the only African Americans in Murray
column293: although they are the best remembered. Perhaps Murray's small established African American community has become historically invisible because in many records they are not specifically identified as \"black\" or \"colored\" or \"Negro\" because their race was know
column294: they were accepted and further identification was superfluous. This
column296: would not be the case with a recent arrival
column297: such as Jim Bing.\n\tAs to the treatment of Bing
column298: such atrocities occurred elsewhere in Utah and there is no reason to suppose similar assaults could not have occurred in Murray. This
column300: is an open question in my mind and to be settled by reviewing Gerlach's sources.  One issue to be resolved is the identity and residence of the attackers.  I am suspicious that locals would choose their own cemetery as the venue for an assault; after all
column301: to them it would be hallowed ground and not to be desecrated.  Pending the identification of the perpetrators
column302: Murray and its citizens are entitled to a presumption of innocence and the benefit of doubt.  If that can be overcome by even a preponderance of the evidence
column303: then they and it are historically accountable.%u201D\n\n\t%u201CAs I tried to make clear in my first e mail
column304: Murray was not immune to prejudice and discrimination
column305: and the usual focus of these attitudes were immigrants from southern and central Europe (including Greeks
column307: and Slavs).  Mayor Stratton's distain for and vitriolic verbal attacks upon these people is well documented by the \"Desert Evening News\" and the \"Salt Lake Tribune\" (although each of these journals had its own reasons for reporting these events and did not necessarily reflect the concerns of Murray and its people).  There are entries in the City Council minutes that cannot be fully understood without taking these reports into account.  The immediate cause of the Mayor's anti immigrant outburst was an assault upon a \"white\" woman by a person or persons unknown (but presumed to be a Greek or Austrian). The attack
column309: was only the trigger which detonated an explosion of resentment and fear which had been building up in Murray over several years.  My interpretation of the available evidence is that the Stratton episode represented an anomalous \"spike\" in ethnic tension
column310: which then subsided but did not immediately disappear.\n\tStratton advocated
column312: that the American Smelting & Refining Co. be persuaded (or compelled) to hire only \"old residents\" or immigrants of Anglo Saxon
column313: Scandinavian or Germanic descent.  I do not recall that he went so far as to advocate the forcible expulsion of Murray's Greek
column315: but I have no doubt he saw employment restrictions as furthering that end.  Nor was he alone in his views.  It is difficult to quantify
column316: but there is evidence of considerable animosity on the part of many local people toward the new industrial immigrants.  In my view
column318: this was less an instance of ethnic or racial bias than it was an example of \"class based\" suspicion of an itinerant industrial proletariat  a transitory
column320: social condition. As the \"old Murryites\" became familiar with the new arrivals
column322: Italians and Slavs became vested in the community by assumed the roles of businessmen
column328: the class based fears faded and the social animosity abated.  It didn't happen quickly
column330: and it may not even happen completely
column331: but it did happen.\n\tAs to early Murray City ordinances and their purpose
column332: having read the pro incorporation material published by Martin A. Willumsen in his \"American Eagle\" newspaper
column335: I am not convinced that \"regulating the . . . the new immigrants\" was a significant concern of Murray's residents and officials.  Better police protection was the number one issue touted by incorporation advocates like Willumsen
column336: but it was protection against conventional criminals and the \"rough element
column341: and \"tough kids\" that most concerned Murrayites.  Concern about the industrial immigrants is evident
column344: these stereotypical categories could as easily be applied to the industrial immigrants as to anyone else
column345: but this does not seem to have been the case.  Newspaper accounts of criminal activity and reports of police court proceedings do not reveal special interest in the activities of the industrial immigrants.  Even in the super charged arena of liquor retailing
column346: the names of offenders are as likely to reflect American or Scandinavian heritage as Greek or Slavic.  Some ordinances (relating to Sunday closing
column348: etc.) were drafted specifically targeting Greek coffeehouses and their proprietors
column349: but the attention given these  Mayor Stratton notwithstanding  is marginal when compared with the time and effort devoted to developing municipal infrastructure
column351: and revenue.  More ink was poured into the council minute book recording the machinations the railroad
column352: streetcar and telephone companies than those of the \"foreigners.\" And
column353: as I mentioned in my first e mail
column354: when the City Council did turn its attention to the Greeks
column355: there were voices to speak out in favor of equity and uniform application of the laws.%u201D\n\t\n%u201CI can understand the assumption that if Murray prohibited Greeks
column357: it is unlikely to have permitted African Americans.  But Murray did not prohibit Greeks
column358: Italians or Slavs per se  although there were doubtless many like Mayor Stratton who
column360: would have liked to.  Such prohibitions that were imposed  rightly or wrongly  were prohibitions of behavior
column361: coupled with the unwarranted assumption that certain groups of people are characterized by certain kinds of behavior.  It is a prejudice founded on ignorance and so erodes as the ignorance erodes.  One thing I have learned from direct observation
column362: Murrayites are not particularly attached to their prejudices
column363: at least for any extended length of time.  In point of fact
column364: African Americans acquired pioneer status  and in Utah \"pioneer\" is a word to conjure with  at the very inception of Euro American settlement (ethnic irony noted) and
column365: the unfortunate experience of Jim Bing notwithstanding
column366: I have found no compelling evidence that they ever gave it up.  Along similar lines
column367: most of the industrial immigrants did not choose to remain in Murray.  Were they run out?  Some may have thought they were  and likely had good reason for thinking so.  Many more likely concluded Murray was no worse than any other Anglo town and stayed until better prospects appeared elsewhere.  Quite a few
column369: remained to became part of the community and
column371: acquitted their new home of the odium of being a \"sundown town.\"  Some of these
column373: recalled with some bitterness the time when they were outsiders
column374: not really part of Murray  but
column376: they let no one ran them out.%u201D\n\n\t%u201CAs mentioned in my last e mail
column377: I spent a couple hours at the Murray City Museum going through the \"Compiled Ordinances;\" I found a few which imposed prohibitions based on age and gender
column378: but not on race.  The compilation was published in 1911 and contains all city ordinances enacted since 1903 that remained in force in 1911. Obviously
column381: would not be included  which leaves a lot of time unaccounted for.\n\tReplying specifically to your comment regarding the 1900 through 1930 censuses
column382: I have not checked these returns.  Such a study would help to settle the residency issue but
column384: would not be conclusive.  I have spent too much time chasing people through the census (people I knew from collateral sources to have been living in a particular place) to base a conclusion on census data alone.\n\tAs to collateral sources and the Bankhead family.  Documents compiled in connection with Murray City's incorporation record that several Bankheads were living within the proposed corporate limits of Murray City in 1902.  These were George A.
column385: George E. (\"A.\" and \"E.\" may or may not be the same individual)

column196: Utah (AP) \n\t%u201CCraddock Matthew Gilmour was 15 years old when a mob lynched a black coal miner accused of killing a marshal.\n\tOn Saturday
column197: the 88-year-old Gilmour was able to put the past behind him as residents of this south central Utah community of 9
column198: 000 dedicated a headstone at the previously unmarked grave of Robert Marshall.\n\t%u201CIt was absolutely wonderful
column199: beautiful; a lot of tears fell here and there
column200: %u201D Gilmour said.\n\tHe organized a ``Day of Reconciliation'' for Marshall to coincide with the 30th anniversary of the assassination of civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr.\n\tAbout 300 people
column201: including several religious leaders
column202: took part in a ceremony inside the Notre Dame school gymnasium.\n\tAfterward
column203: the crowd walked two blocks to the Price Cemetery to dedicate the headstone
column204: which read: %u201CRobert Marshall
column205: lynched June 18
column207: a victim of intolerance. May God forgive.%u201D\n\tHundreds of people witnessed the lynching that was in retaliation for the murder of J. Milton Burns
column208: a marshal in the mining town of Castle Gate.\n\tTwo children said they saw a black man running from the scene of the crime.\n\tThe killing outraged many residents of predominantly white Price
column209: particularly members of the Ku Klux Klan who were then active in the area.\n\tThree days after the murder
column210: Marshall was caught by mine company officials at a cabin and taken to the Price jail. But he never made it to a cell.\n\tGilmour remembers stopping at a store with his father and seeing a man with a rope who said
column211: %u201CWe're going to hang him.%u201D\n\tWhile the lawmen were inside the jail building
column212: a crowd reportedly took over the car in which Marshall was sitting and drove out of town. By the time deputies arrived
column213: Marshall's body was hanging from a tall cottonwood tree.\n\tAbout 1
column214: 000 people gathered at a tree between Price and nearby Wellington to watch Marshall die.\n\tThe deputies cut the rope and as Marshall groaned the crowd reportedly yelled
column215: %u201CString him up again.%u201D The officers were overpowered and Marshall was rehanged.\n\tLater
column216: Marshall's body was put on display at the funeral parlor. Photos of the hanging were sold for 25 cents.\n\tEleven men were arrested for the lynching. But when it came time to present evidence before a grand jury

The only way on or off Chincoteague was by a drawbridge

column176: Not a sundown town
column177: but it attempted to become wholly residentially segregated:\n\"In 1890 the Town Council of Falls Church voted to give away most of the African-American owned land in the town from Tinner Hill to Arlington Boulevard back to Fairfax County. This virtually cut the size of the town by 20%. Today
column178: this land is bounded by Hillwood Avenue
column179: Annandale Road
column180: and Route 50. \nIn January 1915 the Falls Church Town Council went even farther by adopting an ordinance establishing residential segregation. The Town Council formed a Committee on Segregation to create a map of the segregated areas of town.  The committee had six months to do this and then the law would go into effect a year after its original passage.  A 1912 law from the Virginia General Assembly allowed passage of such segregation laws. African-American families already living in the parts of the town designated for whites could stay but no new African-Americans could move into these segregated areas.  They could be found guilty of a misdemeanor and be fined from $5 a day the first week of the violation (but not to exceed $50) and then $2 a day after that. \n\t  The same month Dr. Edwin Bancroft Henderson called a meeting of local African-American families. In Jaunary 1915 Dr. Henderson and Joseph Tinner organized the Colored Citizens Protective League. The group wrote letters to the Falls Church mayor and city council and hired an attorney to try to block the ordinance saying that it violated their 14th Amendment rights of equal protection under the law.  Dr. Henderson also wrote to W.E.B. DuBois in New York to get a local branch of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) formed. As a result the town council reversed its position and did not enforce the segregation ordinance. In November of 1917 the segregation law was formally nullified by the Virginia State Supreme Court. The Falls Church City Council did not formally repeal the law until February of 1999. In June 1918 the Falls Church and Vicinity Branch of the NAACP was formally given a charter becoming the first rural branch of the NAACP in the United States. The Falls Church and Vicinity Branch of the NAACP also helped organized other branches in Arlington
column181: Fauquier
column182: Prince William
column183: and Loundon counties in Northern Virginia as it to reach out into other rural areas where the Ku Klux Klan and racial bigotry were the strongest. \n\tOver the next 50 years
column184: Tinner and Henderson organized civil rights activities that set a precedent for the rural South. The Hendersons
column185: Tinners
column186: and other brave local citizens of the Tinner Hill area risked their lives and livelihoods to defend the principals of the Constitution and Bill of Rights. Beyond fighting against the segregation ordinance
column187: they helped foster a movement that has had far-reaching consequences. \n\tAfrican-Americans in Falls Church area also fought for such conveniences as a larger segregated elementary school (1947)
column188: door-to-door postal service (1949)
column189: public sanitary sewers and water (1955)
column190:
and street lights (1968). The first integrated Girl Scout and Cub Scout troops that were allowed in Falls Church were also started. 
	Tinner Hill   Joseph Tinner
In the late 1800s Charles and Mary Tinner bought land in Falls Church and the area has been known as Tinner%u2019s Hill ever  since.
	The Tinner Hill Monument 
      To commemorate their actions
column191: the Tinner Hill Foundation has constructed a monument at the intersection of Tinner Hill Road and South Washington Street (Route 29; at the corner of the SAAB car dealership). The Monument was designed by Falls Church resident and teacher
column192: John Ballou
column193:
and was built of the same pink granite as quarried from the base of Tinner Hill. The arched monument was dedicated in 1999 and stands 14 feet tall and has two plaques commemorating the residents of Tinner Hill and the NAACP.

column185:
herding them 
down to the train station. There the Asians were

   "On February 7
column187:
a throng  of workers rounded 
up virtually every Chinese in Seattle and herded them 
to the Ocean Dock at the foot of Main Street for 
passage out of town on a waiting steamer. The mob 
and its frightened charges were met at the pier by 
police and a contingent of the volunteer Home Guard.  
A stalemate ensued when territorial governor Watson 
Squire prevented the ship from leaving."  Thus Seattle never quite became a sundown town vis-a-vis Chinese

column189: by tradition and force

column191: but as an entity
column192: Seattle never prohibited blacks from living within the city limits.

	Novelist Edna Ferber made an extraordinary claim for Appleton
column181: Wisconsin

column185: 000 people; tree shaded

column192: who was Jewish
column193: may not have encountered anti-Semitism in Appleton
