column195: she had to know that their absence was by design.  As historian James Cornelius put it
column196: \"When I went to Lawrence University
column197: that's one of the first things I learned
column198: that Appleton was a sundown town.\"   \n\n[C. Eric Lincoln
column199: \"A Brown Man in the House\"] \".... I finally realized the full implications of being called a nigger in a way that never quite came through to me before. I was in graduate school at the time
column200: but my newfound understanding came from the lips of a child. Here is how it came about.\n\tI had traveled with my white classmate from the University of Chicago to visit his family in Appleton
column201: Wisconsin. As we drove in the evening before
column202: it seemed a pleasant enough little town despite the fact that it was the hometown of Senator Joe McCarthy whose notorious red-baiting almost succeeded in making the arts synonymous with communism during the post-World War II era. I did notice that I saw no black people on the streets
column203: and remarked about it. \"None here
column204: \" my friend explained. \"A few Indians now and then from one of the reservations
column205: but I don't remember ever seeing anybody else until you get down to Green Bay. You're breaking new ground!\" I did not feel elated.\n\tThe next morning I woke with the queasy feeling that somebody was in my room. As it tamed out somebody was in my room. As a matter of fact
column206: there were half a dozen somebodys
column207: each with a pair of solemn
column208: questioning eyes intently trained on my black face ensconced in a fat white pillow. I was to learn later that little Barbara
column209: the seven-year-old doyenne of the house
column210: had been out scouring the neighborhood almost since sunup to round up her small-fry colleagues for this exquisite moment of show and tell.\n\t\"You see there!\" Barbara was urging as I finally realized that I was not straggling to come out of some delayed fragment of a dream. \"You see there? I told you we had a brown man at our house! My Uncle Jay brought him from Chicago. He's ours!\" She announced with possessive finality. \"He's ours
column211: and we're going to keep him right here. Now go on and say `good morning' to him. My Mommie says you have to do it!\"\n\tAt first the row of little urchins hesitated a moment as if uncertain as to just how to go about such an unfamiliar task. Finally one of them stepped forward
column212: bowed solemnly
column213: and mumbled \"G'moming! Brown Man
column214: \" and promptly escaped through the bedroom door. After that the whole line followed in turn...until a little tow-haired tyke named Jerry made his way up closer to the bed to see for himself. Suddenly
column215: my big moment in preprandial show and tell was over.\n\t\"Hey! Wait a minute!\" Jerry cried as soon as he could get a clear look. \"Hey!\" He said pointing an accusatory finger at the inert hulk cowering between the frilly covers of the guest room bed. \"Hey! That there ain't no `brown man.' That there ain't nothing but a great big ol' nigger
column216: and we don't have to say nothing to him.!\" With that he stuck out his tongue at me
column217: waggled his thumbs in his ears
column218: and strode triumphantly from the room like a pint-sized conquering hero.\n\tI lay there in the morning light pondering the considerable learnings that had already been thrust upon me for the day. First
column219: I had been a little amused by the adroitness of my host's description of convenience. I had never been called a \"brown man\" before
column220: but in an all-white town like Appleton
column221: I supposed that \"brown man\" was a reasonably innocuous alternative to some more conventional choices that might explain my \"difference\" to little Barbara and her playmates. Since I was obviously not \"white
column222: \" and \"black\" (at that time) was considered pejorative
column223: brown was intended to satisfy the racial inquiry America's children have to make before they can play together. I don't know where or even if little Jerry had ever seen what he identified as a \"nigger
column224: \" but it was clear that he had been taught to conceptualize one
column225: and to make critical judgments in keeping with that conceptualization.\n\tWe don't have to say \"Good Morning\" to a nigger because a nigger is a nonperson. Or
column226: as Humphrey Bogart would probably have put it
column227:
"a nigger is what you are when you aren't anybody at all."

WANNABE: LIFE AND DEATH IN A SMALL TOWN GANG documents the gang related murder/suicide that took the lives of four teenagers in the quiet community of Appleton
column282: Wisconsin in May of 1995. This compelling one hour documentary explores issues of race
column284: and youth violence in the context of one small
column285: middle American city. \n\nProducer\/director John Whitehead returns to his home town to investigate the rise and fall of a teen gang called the D Mac Crew.  The gang's five teenage members   four white and one black   took their name and marching orders from their leader
column286: 17 year old high school junior Derek \"D Mac\" Barnstable. A white
column287: middle class kid from a troubled home
column288: D Mac lived an MTV inspired fantasy of thug life
column289: idolizing the hard core rapper Eazy E and imitating the black \"gangsta\" style and slang he picked up from movies like \"Colors
column290: \" \"Boyz in the Hood\" and \"Menace II Society.\"\n\nIn the spring of 1995
column291: the D Mac Crew's \"wannabe\" fantasies turned tragically real.  The gang had begun hanging out with Jermaine \"Jazz\" Gray
column292: a 17 year old black youth who had recently moved to Appleton from the meaner streets of Milwaukee.  Jazz fell out of favor with the gang over a disagreement about drug money and also because he \"dissed\" the small town crew's gang aspirations
column293: telling them they were \"too white.\"  At D Mac's urging
column295: three members of the D Mac Crew lured Jermaine to a remote cabin and brutally murdered him.  Ten days later
column296: with the police about to arrest them
column297: the three chose to end their own lives rather than face prison.  They drove to a local park where one shot the other two and then ended his own life.\n\nThe crimes detonated a shock wave of grief
column300: 000.  Gang style shootings were expected in L.A. or Chicago but not in Appleton
column301: a town of quiet neighborhoods and leafy parks
column302: an idyllic place with a low crime rate and booming economy.  The circumstances in Jermaine Gray's murder were the exact opposite of what mostly white Appleton would have expected: the victim was a black kid from Milwaukee; the murderers were local whites.  As the police sorted out the bodies and puzzled over motives
column303: the townspeople of Appleton faced a deeper
column304:
complex question: what was happening to their community?  One local woman commented that no Appleton resident is untouched by the issue of teen violence.  "There are kids of all kinds from all economic backgrounds in this community and all races who are involved in violence."

Conversation with the resident:
	My question:  why did the black population of so many WI towns decline drastically beginning around 1890 or thereafter?  In Fond du Lac
column184: for example
column185: 178 blacks in 1880 decline to 112 in 1890
column188: and just 5 in 1940.  Why?\n\tGreen Bay shows a similar decline
column189: beginning in 1910
column190: from 45 down to 10 in 1940.\n\tAppleton never had many but fell from 18 in 1900 to 0 by 1930.\n\tMeanwhile
column191: the white populations of these towns increase
column192: so it's unlikely that \"lack of jobs\" is the cause.\n\tI have several possible theories and would like your insights. 1.  \"Natural\" decline.  These towns are
column193: after all
column194: far from black population centers in the South
column195: are cold
column196: are isolated.     Problem:  they are no further north in 1930 than they had been in say 1890.  Also
column197: if the population grew from a tiny base
column198: such as Fond du Lac's 19 in 1860 to 179 by 1870
column199: why then could it not grow from the larger base of 178 in 1890.  Instead
column200: it shrinks.\n\t2. Labor unions.  These were exceedingly racist around 1900 and may have gotten more power
column201: thus shutting blacks out of employment on the railroad
column202: in the mines
column203: or in lumbering.\n\t3. Social shunning.  We know that the period 1890 1925 or so is the \"nadir of race relations\" in the U.S.  (See my book
column204: LIES MY TEACHER TOLD ME
column205: 161 67.)  During these years whites in the North (and South
column206: of course) segregated blacks
column207: perhaps removing them from normal social intercourse
column208: such as church associations or simple friendship.  Coupled with their never large numbers
column209: that might have influenced blacks to seek larger cities.\n\t4. Violent expulsion or convincing blacks non violently that they should leave.  In many Illinois towns
column210: whites expelled blacks violently or more quietly convinced them to leave
column211: then passed formal ordinances or reached informal understandings that the community or neighborhood would be \"all white.\"  (I put \"all white\" in quotes because some \"all white\" towns actually allowed a black family or two to stay in them
column212: while decidedly UNwelcoming any black newcomers.)\n\tI lean toward #4 because of my knowledge of similar things happening in Illinois (and in Edina

column176: \tThird and final official Greenbelt town.  \n\t\nSTEVE WATROUS
column177: ARTICLE 6 17 99 \"New Urbanism and Nostalgia in Greendale\" \n\tTake the Norman Rockwell version of a small town hometown.  It brings a warm glow to some but a guffaw from others. Yet who gets to be the artist
column178: and who ends up in the picture?\n\tThat`s part of the debate over New Urbanism
column179: discussed by some 800 people from 20 countries at their \"Congress\" in Milwaukee June 3 6.  The proper role of nostalgia is also an issue in that most planned of Milwaukee suburbs
column180: Greendale
column181: with a major renovation of its downtown nearly completed.\n\tThe conference and the community coincided during an afternoon field trip to Greendale
column182: billed as a development containing \"many of the elements of New Urbanism.\"\n\tDespite the extensive planning
column183: said tour leader David Drews
column184: \"Greendale was always a real place.  But Reiman wants it pretty
column185: \" he added wistfully
column186: noting the hanging flower baskets and added on towers that appeal to upscale tourists.  Drews grew up here and became an architect but no longer lives in Greendale.\n\tRoy Reiman moved to Greendale over 30 years ago and built a publishing empire of magazines laced with nostalgia for country places and old ways
column187: such as \"Crafting Traditions\".  \"Greendale is our hometown now.  That`s what we`re trying to re create
column188: \" he said.  \"I remember growing up and listening to the band play in the gazebo on Saturday nights.\"\n\tReiman bought all the downtown stores in 1996 for $2 million when they were mostly empty.  He was the man with a plan and the money to make the village center come alive again.  \"We tried very hard to retain the original and blend it with the 1990s
column189: \" he said.\n\tThe hometown look is good enough that a crew working for Steve Forbes was filming around Greendale recently
column190: especially the gazebo
column191: presumably seeking the \"small town USA\" look for his presidential bid
column192: according to resident Nicki Johnson.\n\tNew Deal money and planning built this village from 1936 38 during the depression.  \"Greendale is a famous example of city planning
column193: \" said Frank Zeidler
column194: who helped with the engineering.  It was constructed along with Greenbelt
column195: Maryland and Greenhills
column196: Ohio as \"Garden City Communities
column197: \" a movement that started in England at the turn of the century.\n\tThe English idea was to build small cities
column198: \"where working men could live outside the industrial area
column199: separated by a green belt
column200: \" says Zeidler.  It was a response to the overcrowding
column201: slums and pollution of cities despoiled by the unplanned growth of manufacturing.   Housing for low income industrial workers was an important goal in Greendale.\n\tThe downtown
column202: designed to resemble colonial Williamsburg
column203: was within walking distance of all residents.  This is a principle of New Urbanism
column204: as is de emphasizing autos through narrow streets and compact housing. Walkways connect the neighborhoods here.\n\tThe small shopping area had difficulties from the start.  The original plan envisioned a much larger number of housing units
column205: but Congress cut the funds and Greendale was frozen at a size too small to support shops on Broad Street.\n\tThis main street was designed away from the heaviest car traffic and not well served by mass transit at the start
column206: both problems for New Urbanists. Supermarket chains later found the buildings and parking lots too small and abandoned the village center
column207: but the near fatal blow was Southridge.\n\tWhen the biggest and most profitable mall in Wisconsin arrived in 1969 on the edge of Greendale
column208: the commercial center began a decline similar to that of other small towns when Wal Mart builds on the outskirts.\n\t\"About two thirds of the businesses were closed when I bought the buildings
column209: \" said Reiman
column210: \"and the rest were on their last leg.\"  The absentee owners of the shops hadn`t kept them up
column211: he added.\n\tReiman sought to make over Greendale`s downtown in both the look and type of businesses.  \"We were very selective
column212: \" he said.  \"We didn`t want to duplicate Southridge.\"  He figured the niche was nostalgia: \"We have better history than Cedarburg!\"\n\tThe stores include The Candlemaker
column213: Ferch`s Malt Shoppe
column214: Great Harvest Bread Co.
column215: and the Original Coffee Company.  Planned for opening later this year is the Taste of Home Restaurant
column216: named after one of Reiman`s magazines.  Hundreds of tourists come daily to see Reiman`s visitor center and Greendale
column217: an many stay to shop.\n\tReiman won`t say how much the renovation cost but jokes that it won`t pay off in his lifetime.  The newer commercial building
column218: constucted on the east side of Broad Street in 1958 and resembling later long box strip malls
column219: hid the park behind it.  Reiman`s architects knocked out two storefronts in the middle to connect shoppers with the park
column220: which now has a footbridge and large flower garden.  \"We wouldn`t have done this if we were just in it for the money
column221: \" he said.  \"I wanted to bring the green back to Greendale.\"\n\tResident opinion about the changes is mostly positive
column222: said Kim Johnson
column223: a photographer who was a village trustee during the renovation.  The newer residents are favorable toward it. \"The old timers say it`s beautiful but not the original Greendale
column224: \" he said about an apparent case of dueling nostalgias.  \"Some people here are really enamored of the beautiful old Greendale.\" He lives in the original section.\n\tReiman said he hadn`t heard the complaint that the original Greendale had been lost.  \"They would have lost it anyway
column225: \" he said.  \"They would have had a bunch of empty buildings.  Only Ricardo`s Pizza would still be open.\"\n\tJohnson generally favors the renovation.  \"It needed to be done and Reiman could do it in a unified manner.  It`s not the old Greendale but that might not have worked commercially.\"  He couldn`t see any other option for revitalizing the downtown.\n\tThe original buildings were simple
column226: plain and utilitarian
column227: in Johnson`s opinion.  They needed the architectural embellishment they never got originally.  Although the built on towers and lattices make the commercial area look like an upscale development
column228: he said it had not become a theme park.  \"The design is more modern than Norman Rockwell
column229: \" he said.  Johnson can once again walk to shops useful in daily life
column230: such as the hardware store.\n\tSo how does Greendale measure up to principles discussed at the Congress? \"Nationally
column231: Milwaukee is seen as the New Urbanism utopia
column232: \" said environmental activist Jeff Poniewaz.  This is due partly to Mayor John Norquist`s national leadership on these issues
column233: and his honor spoke at several of the Congress sessions.\n\tWearing a red
column234: orange and gray Hawaiian style shirt at the Saturday mayors` session
column235: Norquist blasted federal policies for sapping the vitality of cities and encouraging ugly suburbs
column236: and lampooned planning too far ahead of market conditions.\n\tGreendale
column237: of course
column238: was built with federal dollars and policies
column239: completely outside market considerations.  So why a tour there connecting it with New Urbanism?  Greendale is a refreshing exception to usual federal policies
column240: explained Norquist.  \"It`s a triumph of aesthetics.\"\n\tBut Greendale is an incomplete triumph for both New Urbanism and the original plan.  Serving low income workers was modified as a goal when cost overruns and a funding cut off before completion raised the cost per dwelling.  The original housing was simple and Spartan
column241: but still cost too much for people at the bottom of the ladder.  \"I couldn`t afford it
column242: \" declared Zeidler.\n\tEven with federal subsidies
column243: it costs so much to build decent housing that a person making the minimum wage simply can`t afford it.  This will hinder New Urbanist dreams for a completely new city since poor people are likely to be around for awhile.\n\tIncome limits kept the rich out of Greendale at first and the majority of original residents were blue collar workers from the south side.  When the project was privatized in 1952
column244: row houses and modest cost lost favor during expansion.  First came 1950s ranch houses with big yards
column245: then luxury homes costing as much as $800
column246: 000.\n\t\"Greendale was a triumph of land use planning
column247: \" said Zeidler
column248: \"and because of the nice plan
column249: wealthy people started moving there. Now it`s a high cost
column250: upper income area
column251: \" he lamented.\n\tNeeding a car to get to work further justified changing the goal to serving \"modest\" or middle income residents. Light industry within walking distance didn`t come until years later
column252: a further disappointment for New Urbanism. \n\tDiversity is another principle of New Urbanism.  Eleanor Roosevelt visited Greendale under construction in 1936
column253: found much positive about it
column254: but wondered if it would serve blacks.  It did not and became as homogeneous as surrounding suburbs.  Left to their own devices
column255: many people prefer to live near others like themselves
column256: but not too close.\n\tGrassroots people power is not a top priority for New Urbanists
column257: and they can get annoyed at pesky local dissenters.   Greendale was planned top down from Washington with only a bit of input from potential residents.  Perhaps that`s the only way grand projects can work
column258: albeit imperfectly.  \n\tEarly Greendale residents often conflicted with outside managers
column259: but once on their own they moved away from both the original plan and New Urbanist ideas
column260: and they permitted Southridge to nearly kill the old village center.  It took a financial angel
column261: Roy Reiman
column262: to give the downtown a new chance at life.\n\tYet despite any imperfections
column263: as David Drews noted
column264: people consider Greendale to be a special place
column265: and property values are much higher than the buildings alone would warrant.  Even the small

In 1900 Census 5 of the 24 Black Janesville residents were live-in servants.

In 1920 2 of the 15 Black Janesville residents were live-in servants.

In 1988
column177: an article entitled
column178: \"Perceptions Die Hard
column179: Blacks Say
column180: \" interviewed a black Janesville resident who said
column181: \"if you came to Janesville [as a black] you came early in the morning and you left before dark.\" This person had black friends from nearby Beloit who would decline invitations to stay overnight. It was even rumored that there used to be a big sign between Janesville and Beloit that read
column182: \"No Niggers Allowed.\" (Janesville Gazette 1988).\n\nA Janesville Gazette article published in 1988 states that
column183: \"many claim that General Motors' hiring practices up to 1970 helped keep Janesville a white city.\" Even neighboring blacks in Beloit heard \"rumors of sunset laws that chased blacks out of town before dark.\" After some further investigation a cursory check of the historical records in the Rock County Courthouse
column184: \"restrictive covenants against minorities were found in the bylaws of city and county subdivisions as late as 1960.\" \n(March 15
column185: 1988)\n\nJohnny Lee
column186: a past black Janesville resident
column187: stated in a 1988 article that \"you didn't let the sun go down on you in Janesville if you were black.\"\n\nIn a set of interviews provided at the University of Wisconsin historical interviews
column188: William 'Blue' Jenkins talks about his investigation of union discrimination in Chevrolet plant at Janesville
column189: \"found Janesville had a \"ring\" made up of newspaper
column190: church
column191: business people and the city's one Black resident; they worked to keep Blacks out of the city.\"\n\nA Madison resident said that when her identifiably \"anti-war\" colleagues drove through Janesville in 1968 to stop for lunch and were beaten up by a group of men who said repeatedly
column192: \"no niggers or hippies allowed; get out of town by sundown.\" (November 2013).\n\nThomas Walterman
column193: previous dean at UW-Rock County Center was interviewed in the Janesville Gazette in 2004 claiming that
column194: \"he remembers a standard welcome (by the supervisor) he received when he moved to Janesville in 1973
column195: \"You'll like it here. We don't have any minorities.\" Walterman suspected that the racial makeup of Janesville was no accident. \n\nA different dean from UW-Rock County Center
column196: who was from Mississippi
column197: told Ted Kinnaman (who worked on open\/fair housing)
column198: \"Now
column199: you aren't going to bring any (blacks) in here
column200: are you?\"Kinnaman also recalled at the 1967 Rock County Fair hearing \"the Republican tent and Democrats in their tent bragging how they run some Milwaukee \"niggers\" out of town.\"\n(\"Liberals Promoted City Integration\" Janesville Gazette
column201:
3-14-88 article).

In 1988 one of the three black families that lived in Janesville said that the main reason that they were not discriminated against was because of the reputation the[y] established. This statement suggests that it was difficult for blacks to gain respect or legitimacy in the earlier 20th Century.

Testimony of a former resident: "Manitowoc WI was such a town. When I lived there in 1962-64 there were such signs on the main roads in and out of town. I worked at a stable after school. When an African American man who drove a horse van came through town and needed a place to stay the owner of the stable Larry Bowlin put him up and the ate brats
column193: drank beer
column194: and played poker. Larry told us kids not to tell
column195: that it would be very dangerous for his friend if he were caught. \n\tI didn't live in Manitowoc long  --went to junior high there for 2 years.  The sign fascinated me because my family moved to WI from Chattanooga
column196: Tennessee. There
column197: in a typical southern way
column198:
segregation existed but white black involvement was nevertheless constant in its spheres and the idea of making African Americans leave at sunset was bizarre. The folks in Manitowoc were so convinced all southerners were backward that they tried to put me in a lower school and kids asked if we wore shoes at home. (we said Never!) They also used the N word which caused me to ask my mother if they were all "trashy" or just some.  
	The signs were worded approx: NIGGER: Don't let the sun go down on you in our town!  I Think the words were in italics and painted across a picture of a green hill with the sun setting halfway behind it."

\"Oshkosh ... was a 'sundown town' where African Americans were not welcome to stay overnight. Those who challenged this custom put themselves at grave risk. In 1960 only 7 African Americans lived in Oshkosh. --Andrew Kersten and Jerald Podair
column177: review of \"Black Thursday Remembered
column178: \" exhibit in WI Black Historical Society Museum
column179: JOURNAL OF AMERICAN HISTORY 6\/2009
column180: 167.\n    ... the community and the university's administration and faculty were caught off guard by the arrival of 94 black college students. Oshkosh's citizens continued to practice a northern version of Jim Crow
column181: denying their new black neighbors housing
column182: entertainment
column183: employment
column184: and basic services.\" \"Matters climaxed in the fall semester of 1968.\" The college president responded to the occupation of his office by expelling all 94 students
column185: banning them from attending any OTHER state campus in WI
column186:
and notifying their draft boards (of the males). The administration also fired white faculty members who supported the students.  

August 2007: One reader reports "It was said the great Jesse Owens came to Oshkosh after the Olympics.  He was honored but quietly told not to hang around after it got dark."

Oshkosh unions had an agreement with the town
column187: not the employers
column188: to keep blacks out of town. \n\nWhat I have read somewhere in the past is\/ was that Oshkosh and the Fox river valley were slowly integrating
column189: but by the late 40s\/50s segregated (minorities leaving) because of lack of work (and acceptance) in an expanding economy. What I recall (accuracy??) is that it was part of the larger experience of what some labor historians (there are some sources to track: Buhle for instance ) have described as the triad: business
column190: white labor and govt: labor got higher wages but didn't cause labor problems and supported US expansion abroad and its foreign policy (AIFLD
column191: association with anticommunism
column192: CIA
column193: etc; Meaney's support of the Vietnam War
column194: etc). In return minorities were not integrated into the unions.  \n     I think this interpretation has been challenged to some degree;at least it was not so stark
column195: but it seemed logical to me to accept considering hearing about more integrated Oshkosh community in the past and then seeing how white it was when we lived there form 92 94. There is also the anomally (of sorts) of seeing so many union bumper stickers
column196: yet the area is so overwhelmingly Republican. \n     Also
column197: someone else told me that union workers had run black strikebreakers out of Oshkosh at some point.  He thought 1920s
column198: although the census shows big drops in black population between 1910-20 and again 1930-40.  Ever hear of this?\n\nI saw your query on H LABOR and have a tiny scrap of questionable info. to contribute to answering it.  I grew up in Oshkosh
column199: Wisconsin
column200: in the 1950s and 1960s
column201: when the town was all white.  This was largely treated as something natural and unremarkable.  However
column202: one piece of local oral history had it that African Americans had been brought in as strikebreakers and subsequently run out of town.  My very faint recollection about this places the strike in the 1920s.  I don't know anything about KKK activity in that area at that time but suspect that there might have been some. \n\nWe moved to Oshkosh when I was 6 in 1957.  For most of my growing up in Oshkosh
column203: the only African Americans I remember were those who came up from Illinois on vacation to fish in the Fox River and in some of the lakes connected to the Fox River system. \t\nIn junior high I remember being taught that in the 1920's the KKK was popular in Oshkosh
column204: with either 1000 or 5000 members (I'm not sure which). \n\nAt one time I remember asking my parents if any black families lived in Oshkosh.  My Dad or Mom said that there was one black family
column205: but I don't remember ever knowing or meeting them
column206:
or having them identified to me when I was a child. 

My Dad taught at the university (then Oshkosh State College) and claimed that he tried to get a Black friend of his who taught at Ripon College to take a job at Oshkosh State College.  But his friend didn't want to come to Oshkosh.  Dad said his friend didn't think then environment would be as accepting in Oshkosh as it was in Ripon. 

In Oshkosh High School
column207: there was a fellow in the class one year behind mine (he would have graduated in 1970) who was Egyptian
column208: and who fairly clearly was of mixed race
column209: with both white and black racial characteristics.  I think he was accepted by everyone except for some of my Jewish friends
column210: who disliked him not because of his having black racial characteristics
column211: but because of the tension between Israel and Egypt at the time.  There was another Egyptian in the same class who was fairly Caucasian
column212: whom my Jewish friends disliked too.  The mixed blood fellow went by the name Sam.  I think his name was Osama.  The thing I really remember about Sam was that he was an excellent chess player.  We played a few times and I could never beat him.  I think Sam was the only person in Oshkosh High School who beat me in chess.  Sam said that his dad was a chessmaster.  His dad maybe was a Professor at
column213: what was by then
column214: Oshkosh State University.  \n\nBy the late 1960's and early 1970's
column215: there probably were African American professors at the University in Oshkosh
column216: but I do not know for sure.  There definitely were African American students.  One year
column217: a number (maybe even 100 or more) of African Americans were expelled from Oshkosh State University for a demonstration in the administration building (Dempsey Hall) during which it was said that they damaged or destroyed property.  I don't recall clearly
column218: but maybe the African Americans occupied part of Dempsey Hall and issued demands and then when the demands were not met to their satisfaction
column219: started destroying or damaging property.  I don't really remember clearly though.  I maybe never really knew the details that well.  I don't recall what the demands were.  I don't even remember which year this was.  I maybe was in high school at the time.  \n\nIn about 1970 a group of African American investors came up from Milwaukee and bought the People's Brewery and eventually the Chief Oshkosh Brewing Company.  Through the temporary work company
column220: Manpower
column221: I worked (manual labor) for a week or two at People's Brewery.  I remember seeing the African American owners walking through the plant at least once.  I think they were giving some people a tour.  \n\nI think that many local people welcomed the investment in the two breweries
column222: because it was an attempt to save the failing breweries.  The attempt failed though
column223: because the major American breweries were selling their beer so cheaply
column224:
and the smaller breweries in Wisconsin were unable to compete at that time.  Many smaller breweries in Wisconsin failed before microbreweries became popular.  

I just saw your post about African Americans in WI.  I think we had a black foreign exchange student one year in my time at Oshkosh High School (1965-69).  My mother also graduated from Oshkosh High School (in 1941) and she had a Black guy in her class.  His last name was Shad and his family apparently lived in Oshkosh for some time. 

I have lived in Oshkosh all of my 80 years
column225: so know a little about its history
column226: I attended Oshkosh High School from 1937 to 1941.  There were only two non white families whose children were classmates of mine  The Shadd (not sure of spelling) was black and their son Bill was a great athlete so they were reasily accepted.The other family was the Lem family
column227: they ran an oriental restaurant.  Both families were very well thought of--the Lem kids were brilliant.\n\nI heard rumors about the Harlem Globe Trotters being invited to the home of one of the prominent families in town  by their daughter
column228: because they could not be housed in a hotel in the city.  This is only hearsay
column229: so I cant confirm this as gospel truth.\n\nMy first husband worked in construction before he went back to school on the GI bill.  He worked with five black men who were housed in an apartment provided by the construction company that employed them. Before we were maried
column230: my husband had one room in a home
column231: and ate all of his meals out   his fellow workers often invited him for a home cooked meal.  After we were maried
column232: they became our guests also.  They always encouraged my husband to further his education
column233: so that he would not end up like them working hard physically at a difficult job.  AFter my husband died
column234:
I kept in touch with two of the men for many years until I receive word that both had died.


I lived in Oshkosh from 46 to 72.
I know that no blacks lived in Oshkosh up to 1968. After that I'm not sure..
Oshkosh do not have any laws keeping blacks out.

column178: black students in Ripon got followed around in stores much more than white students. \n\nMy wife tells me in all the many documents she read over the years at the Ripon Historical Society (she was once president)

column180: she agrees with your comment that this sort of informal norm is rarely written down.  She mentioned that there are documents establishing that fugitives on the Underground Railroad were harbored in Ripon.  Perhaps it was the New England Yankee influence in settlement
column181: or the Fourierite influence of Ceresco
column182: once a separate town
column183: now the west end of Ripon
column184: which was founded in the 1850's as a commune (lasted less than a decade).\n\tHer preference is for the loneliness hypothesis
column185: especially in a town this size
column186: but I can also cite individual examples of subtle and sometimes not so subtle prejudice related to me by black and Asian students here over the years.  Yet
column187: one of the city's present aldermen is black (he's a development officer at the college).  So it is indeed hard to sort out.\n\t

2015 email from a former resident:
"...After a couple of months [in Sheboygan] I mentioned that I was surprised that I hadn't seen any Negroes (the polite term we used back then)in the city. They explained that Negroes were a rare sight because they weren't allowed to stay overnight in Sheboygan. I was amazed at the news--coming from Milwaukee

Testimony of a former resident:
  I lived and went to school in and around the wausau area from second grade all the way through high school.  (I graduated in 1966) I worked for a year after graduation in the county court house.  I never heard at any time of any law such as the one of which you asked.
 As a matter of fact I remember a distinct pro African American movement when I was in grade school and all of the problems were showing up in the south. I even questioned my father
column198: (who grew up here in SW Missouri) about the differences between the way blacks were treated here.  We had class discussions about the answers he gave me and NO ONE
column199: student or teacher
column200: ever defended the way black citizens were treated. \nA  number of  people from Wausau marched in Alabama.  They were people who could afford to travel and take time off from work
column201: but were backed by others in the community. \n I do know that back before W.W.II the Wausau Hotel would not allow a black person to stay there.  I am aware of this because my mother's friend was one of the families who opened their homes.  I do believe they were fined by the city each time they refused even back then.  I wish my mother was still alive to check this point.  If I am remembering correctly
column202: however that would mean the city did not approve of their policy and therefore the idea that it was some kind of law would be unlikely.\n I can tell you that there were no African Americans or Asians In our high school when I attended there.\n

column177: 1966.\"  Implies there had been charges of [suburban?] housing discrimination.   \n[WI Cartographers' Guild
column178: WI's Past and Present (Madison:  U WI P
column180: 26.]\n\nKnown informally as \"White-a-tosa\" acc. to Canoe Base staffer
column181:
8/2004. 

The Milwaukee Journal Sentinel Online  jsonline.com 
BRUCE MURPHY
column182: \"A generation ago
column183: bias was written on deed
column184: \" Posted: 2\/21\/2004 \n\t\"For decades
column185: any minority seeking to move into the Washington Highlands subdivision
column186: one of the elite areas of Wauwatosa
column187: would encounter this legal restriction: \"At no time shall the land included in Washington Highlands
column188: or any part thereof
column189: or any building thereon be purchased
column190: owned
column191: leased or occupied by any person other than of white race.\"\n\t\"I thought it was in the interest of the overall metropolitan area's well being to have an integrated society.\"   Gerald Schwerm
column192: Brown Deer village manager from 1966 to 1978 \n\tThis restriction was in effect from 1920 until the 1970s
column193: when the subdivision's covenant deleted this clause
column194: a notation at the Wauwatosa Historical Society suggests.\n\tLois Quinn
column195: who served as executive director of the now disbanded Metropolitan Integration Research Center
column196: researched such deed restrictions
column197: and found examples of them in 16 of 18 Milwaukee County suburbs. There were also subdivisions in the City of Milwaukee with such covenants
column198: she said.\n\tQuinn paid particular attention to suburbs such as Wauwatosa and Brown Deer
column199: areas adjoining the north and west sides of Milwaukee
column200: where most blacks reside. In Wauwatosa
column201: she found that in the past
column202: 51 subdivisions encompassing one third of all residential land prohibited non whites from renting or owning homes.\n\tWell into the 1950s
column203: even after the Supreme Court in 1948 ended judicial enforcement of such covenants
column204: they continued to be recorded in Milwaukee County. Places such as Wauwatosa also found other ways to discourage integration.\n\tNews stories show that in 1955
column205: an African American
column206: Zeddie Q. Hyler
column207: bought a new home in Wauwatosa
column208: only to be met with a deluge of hate phone calls and vandalism. The city's Common Council then passed a 30 day moratorium on building permits in the area.\n\tIn 1956
column209: William Kelley
column210: the black director of the Milwaukee Urban League
column211: was refused permission to build a home in Wauwatosa. In 1959
column212: two officials on the city's building board suggested that potential black homeowners should be required to meet more stringent aesthetic standards than whites.\n\tVel Phillips
column213: a champion of open housing who served as a Milwaukee alderman at the time
column214: says today that Wauwatosa \"was absolutely closed to black people\" back then.\n\t       *Progressive leaders*\n\tBrown Deer and Glendale
column215: both incorporated in the 1950s
column216: offered a marked contrast to an older city like Wauwatosa
column217: created in the the 1890s. Quinn found not one example of a restrictive covenant in Brown Deer but can remember finding at least one in Glendale.\n\t\"Brown Deer was more open
column218: with lots of farmland
column219: \" said Phillips
column220: and that kind of fluidity made it less resistant to change
column221: she added.\n\tBrown Deer also had some progressive leaders. Gerald Schwerm
column222: Brown Deer village manager from 1966 to 1978
column223: was a leader in pushing for low and moderate income housing in the suburbs. He chaired a subcommittee of the League of Suburban Municipalities that recommended suburbs should be open to low  and moderate income housing
column224: which inevitably would bring in minority residents.\n\tSchwerm and the Brown Deer Village Board soon created such housing in their city.\n\t\"I thought it was in the interest of the overall metropolitan area's well being to have an integrated society
column225: \" Schwerm said in a recent interview.\n\tBy 1976
column226: when Milwaukee's city schools were desegregated
column227: Brown Deer's population was 4% black
column228: which made it 200 times more integrated than Wauwatosa
column229: which was 0.02% black at the time.\n\tBrown Deer also became a leader in embracing city suburban student exchanges of Chapter 220
column230: the state law promoting integration.\n\tThere was a \"comfort level\" with the idea
column231: said former Brown Deer superintendent of schools Ken Moe
column232: partly because the village had \"some experience of success of people of different backgrounds living together.\"\n\tBy the 2000 census
column233: Brown Deer was 12.5% black
column234: and Glendale was 8%. Wauwatosa lagged far behind at around 2%.\n\tAffordability may help explain some of the difference. The median price of a Wauwatosa home is $187
column237: 000 in Glendale and $130
column238: 000 in Brown Deer. Surveys also show that most African Americans would prefer to live in neighborhoods with a representative population of blacks.\n\tAs for the old idea that home values will decline because of the presence of blacks
column239: the statistics for Brown Deer don't support this. The median value of homes owned by African Americans there is $144
column241: compared with $116
column242: 400 for whites.\n\tWhen asked about Wauwatosa's receptivity to homeowners of color
column243: Mayor Theresa Estness said
column244: \"Certainly
column245: that's something I speak about
column246: that we need to be a welcoming place for all people. Can any community do better? Well
column247: of course.\"\n\tVincent Lyles
column248: an investment banker with Baird & Co.
column249: is African American and lives on the northwest side of Wauwatosa
column250: in the Sheraton Lawns subdivision. Lyles said he was \"pretty impressed\" with the neighborhood
column251: and has been welcomed by neighbors. \"I would bet Wauwatosa is a little more sophisticated today
column252: \" Lyles said.\n\nAccording to a former resident
column253: \"We were one of three African-American families to buy and live there.  Apparently
column254: although I've never seen the evidence of this
column255: my father got a Jewish man to buy the land and then sell it to him
column256: in order to subvert what I think was a defacto real estate policy of not selling to blacks.  Our 'neighbors' were mostly unfriendly (a few stand out as being accepting). One of our immediate neighbors immediately planted a thorny hedge between our properties and never spoke to us. I was the only black child at my elementary school and many of the kids were racist. Looking back
column257: I had one and only one white friend throughout elementary school.  I'm fascinated that according to your statistics
column258: there were 200-300 blacks in Wauwatosa in the 1960s. I find this hard to believe and wonder where they were. There was a huge institutional complex - including a mental illness asylum and an orphanage - perhaps there were black inmates? Alternatively
column259: there was an old section of Wauwatosa where people were very wealthy - perhaps some of them had live-in black workers.\"\n\n

column177: The Spirit of West Bend (Madison:  Straus Printing
column178: 1980)]\r\n \r\n\"If there is any one character that everyone hears about sooner or later in connection with West Bend it is 'Snowball.'  He was Elmer Lynden
column179: a young Negro about 25 years old who lived and worked in the area.  On the Saturday night of July 19
column181: he had attended a dance at Nenno and was walking home toward Barton [adjacent to West Bend] when a local police officer
column182: Henry J. Lemke
column183: tried to arrest him after receiving a call about a disturbance.  He was alleged to be intoxicated.  According to later testimony the young man resisted arrest and in the melee drew a pair of pliers and struck Lemke.  When Chief of Police Berend arrived he tried to reason with 'Snowball
column184: ' who fled
column185: after allegedly 'using abusive language' and 'refusing to listen to reason.'  Two warning shots were fired
column186: but the third shot fired by Lemke hit Snowball squarely in the abdomen while he was backed up to the brewery wall.  He fell.\"   [Note:  if they shot to prevent his running away
column187: he had stopped.  If they felt threatened
column188: well
column189: they were two armed men and he had pliers.]\r\n\tMany West Benders \"were\t not satisfied with either the testimony or the resultant feeing of the police officer.  Especially incensed were the members of the Washington County Humane Society [!]
column190: so Charles Leins
column191: president of the organization
column192: brought charges of manslaughter against Lemke...\"\r\n\tKKK letter threatened DA.  Lemke was found not guilty.  \r\n\t\"Probably few people were as well liked as [Snowball]; it became difficult to find anyone who would speak ill of him. He had always appeared happy and cheerful; his beautiful singing voice and his skill on the banjo made him a popular entertainer.\"  \"\"He was so well known in this section that fully 1000 persons came to the undertaking parlor Monday and early Tuesday morning to view the remains...\"  Lemke became \"a virtual recluse.\"   Only one other black in the history of the area was an ex-slave whose wife made great cookies
column193: died in 1899.  \r\n\"West Bend simply did not like the [Civil ] war.\"  German farmers.  \"Most people were anti-Republican.  In Wisconsin
column194: this brand new political party was composed largely of Whigs
column195: who were noted for their anti-Catholic and anti-foreigner feelings.\"  \"A third reason was that West Bend simply was not interested in freeing the slaves.\"  West Bend newspaper editor:  \"\"We look upon this extensive immigration of these free blacks to mix with and compete with free white labor of the north as a most outrageous policy.  The equality will be formed by reducing the white to a level with the black.  It must be stopped soon
column196: if ever.  The only way to stop these sons of Ham from over-running our state and disgracing our race is left with the Democrats.  See that you vote for men who will free our state from the curse.\"\"   So
column197: West Bend supplied few soldiers
column198: little money
column199: to the cause.  It did pay lots for substitutes
column200: however.  \"Washington County had the lowest proportion of volunteers of any county in the state.\"   \r\n\tOn 11\/16\/1862
column201: a mob threatened the draft commissioner
column202: who fled to Milwaukee.  Port Washington also had a draft riot; the mob attacked the store of an abolitionist.   \"'Iowa voted to put blacks on an equal basis
column203: so let's send them the blacks from Wisconsin.'  These were the local sentiments.\" \r\n\tWest Bend \"was a solidly Democratic community at the outset....  The village never did have Free Soilers
column204: as did its Yankee neighbor Hartford
column205: for the communities developed in vastly different ways after the Yankees left West Bend to be replaced by the Germans
column206: while Hartford remained predominantly Yankee.\"  \"West Bend remained firmly in the Democratic camp for fifty years....  Why?  [Lincoln] favored Negro suffrage
column207: and the locals voted by a whopping 60% against the extension of the vote to blacks.\"  By 3 to 1
column208: voted for Tilden over Hayes
column209: 1876.  Disliked Grant
column210: earlier.  \"The people preferred Tilden because he promised to 'sound the death knell for military rule in the South
column211: '\" a sore point in West Bend [quoting the Democratic newspaper editor]. \r\n\tHuge celebration
column213: when Grover Cleveland was inaugurated
column214: first Democrat since Civil War.   But in 1896
column215: the Democratic newspaper supported McKinley
column216: while Bryan won
column218: the first close vote in West Bend history.   Coolidge won
column219: 5 to 2
column220: but Al Smith and FDR won.  In 1938
column221: Republicans won
column222: and after WWII Republicans always win.   \r\n\tNo Civil War monument.  Two big plaques of Civil War names were put on the WWI monument
column223: which also has two big plaques of WWI names.  [In proportion to population
column224: though
column225: Civil War probably killed more here.]  \r\n\tTo get government contracts
column226: the West Bend Company \"had to have minorities.\"  \"So they brought in five or six of the darkest ones they could find
column227: and kept them visible
column228: in case the government showed up.\"  \"Bad faith.\"  That would be in the early 1970s. \r\n\tMore recently
column229: in the 1980s
column230: they needed workers for the night shift
column231: so they bussed blacks in from Milwaukee.  None ever moved here. \r\n\tThe first black to move to West Bend was a black manager at K-Mart in about 1982.\r\n\t[Why none earlier?]  \"Everyone is German Lutheran.\"  Also
column232: \"Industries weren't hiring them.\"\r\n\tIn the last ten years
column233: though
column234:
we have had a black deputy sheriff.

August 2007

column176: A longtime resident said
column177: through email
column178: \"My mother's family is from Belington
column179: WV and I always heard they had the sign there. My Grandfather moved there in 1913 and from stories he told
column180: the sign was up then. I was born in 1961 and all I know is it was gone by my time but I have heard many stories. I have asked around and one person stated they saw the sign as late as 1954
column181: pulled up but leaning against the side of a building. My Grandfather also said that when he first came to Belington as a mines agent in 1913
column182: a wealthy man on the train had brought a black porter with him to carry his luggage
column183: but the porter was not allowed entry into the Lucerne hotel in Belington
column184: and had to move on down the line. Today
column185: I don't believe there are any blacks in the town
column186: though there are in nearby Philippi. I don't think this is due to racism anymore but because there just never were any black people out there
column187: never had slavery out there
column188: and the job market has been very bad for so many decades it doesn't draw new residents
column189: so there was nothing to draw anyone new.\"

column176: No clear reason is known why Chester has had such a small minority population. Interestingly
column177: other towns nearby do not have the same situation. East Liverpool is across the Ohio River and Weirton is further down the Ohio
column178: and both have large minority populations.

Keystone
column178: W. Va.
column179: Jan. 24 -- This pivot city here in this borderline state leaped into the limelight recently when the first lynching of record this year was chalked up against it following the discovery of the body of L. K. Kent
column180: in his parked automobile on the outskirts of town.\n\nKent
column181: well known and will liked in this community
column182: was found in a sitting posture in his automobile
column183: his face and limbs burned beyond recognition.  Police traced his identity through the license plates on his car....\n\nFriends of the Kents scouted the report which said the young man was seen leaving here
column184: with several white women in his automobile
column185: headed toward Davy
column186: 19 miles from Keystone.  It was also rumored among certain whites that he had been warned to stay away from Davy
column187: an all-white town....\n

column176: Email interview with resident of a nearby town: \"Two of our close friends who are black told me that the sundown rule was important in Lincoln County
column177: and they would never go there with me
column178: daylight or not.\"\n\nA public employee in Lincoln County said that there was an African American employee of the water company who was run out of town. She also said that there was a racial incident not too long ago involving a black man and a white woman living together. Some of the community members threatened to burn down the house
column179: and the couple left town.

column176: Testimony of a West Virginian:\n\"The rural town of Marlinton got its first black resident in the 1960s.  The power company sent a newly-graduated black electrical engineer and his family to work at their regional office there.  I heard that within a week someone had burned a cross on his lawn
column177: and overnight the power company moved him out for his own protection.\"

column176: A WV librarian notes that %u201Cthe whole state has a fairly homogeneous population%u201D and that there are %u201Cvery few people moving in or out.%u201D She also explained that Marmet was a coal-mining town that employed African Americans as miners. After the mines shut down
column177: there wasn%u2019t a reason for anyone%u2014much less African Americans%u2014to stay. She also reminded me %u201CMarmet is a very tiny place.%u201D The librarian%u2019s rationale for the reason behind the small minority population is a simple one: %u201Cthere are not jobs in Marmet.%u201D \n\n

column176: Email from a nearby resident: \"There was a sign there for years that said N****rs don't let the sun set on your asses here.\"\n\nPhone call with former
column177: nearby resident. She said that the only town she knew of that may fulfill the requirements of a sundown town was Milton. She said she understood it to be unfriendly to black people.

column176: A local librarian told me that I \"hit the nail on the head.\"She explained that the town does have Indian doctors
column177: but for whatever reason there are very few black families. Her thought was that the lack of minority population might stem from New Martinsville's location; there aren't any cities nearby and therefore nothing to draw people to the town. \"We knew everybody in town
column178: but it's not that way anymore.\"

column176: A local librarian grew up in Nitro
column177: but didn't believe there was anyone she knew of that could answer my questions. \"There's no good reason it's this way
column178: \" she kept repeating. She did mention
column179: however
column180: that \"Nitro's always been white and Dunbar's always been black.\" My investigation into Dunbar proved inconclusive.

column176: The mayor
column177: Barbara Hickman
column178: explained that there are a few Mexican
column179: Philippino
column180: and Chinese families in Oak Hill but didn't have a reason as to why there are so few black families in the area. She went on to assure me \"we don't have any race problems\" and that the citizens of Oak Hill get along with each other \"just as well as any place else
column181: if not better.\" \n\t \nEmail Interview with resident: \"Eventually as I got older I married (in 2000)my childhood sweetheart from Beards Fork and moved to Oak Hill.  It took several months before I saw an African American resident.  I remember asking my ex husband what was the population for blacks in Oak Hill and his response was that there weren\\'t that many. He went on to say that up until about the late 70s African Americans weren\\'t allowed to be in Oak Hill after sun down
column182: and that African Americans weren\\'t welcome as homeowners within the city limits. However
column183: if so desired
column184: the potential homeowner was pursuaded to reside in Harlem Heights (along Harlem Heights road) which is technically located just beyond the city limits and across the street from the Country Store on the corner of Old Summerlee Rd and E. Main St.  Also
column185: I was told from fellow African Americans that the house my ex!   husband and I purchased never housed a black family nor the neighborhood we resided in.\"\n\nEmail from 2015: \"I was born near the area. Oak Hill is NOT a sundown town. It is true that few African Americans live there
column186: but we do populate the surrounding areas and frequent Oak Hill. There is no night life to speak of
column187: however
column188: no one feels threatened by being there after dark.\"\n

column176: Email: \"Shannondale started out as a vacation home area along the Shenandoah River in Jefferson County WV.  While I was pursuing a debate with the planning commission I ran across an early 1970's (1974 I think) home owners association document that stated that 'no persons of non-Caucasian persuasion' would be allowed to purchase or build in Shannondale.  This matched a story I was told by an older African American friend of mine that when she was dating her husband they would take Rt. 9 across the Blue Ridge into VA.  She told me that he used to tell her that if the car broke down on the WV side of the mountain after dark they needed to run off the mountain.  I also remember seeing something like this in the Sterling Park area of Loudoun County VA in the late 1990's in the Newberry Condominium Association Bylaws from back in the 1970's.  Perhaps there may have been more 'sun down' communities that were not incorporated thus escaped notice of the public or the media.\"
A longtime resident said
column177: through email
column178: \"I know that growing up we did not have any blacks in the entire county (Pleasants) and that whenever there were any that tried to move in
column179: they were apparently informed they were not welcome
column180: and left shortly after arriving.  I remember being aware that blacks were not welcome...\"

column176: An incident occurred in Rock Springs in 1885 in which 28 Chinese were killed because they refused to join whites in a strike for higher wages. According Sucheng Chang
column177: author of \"US and Them: A History of Intolerance in America
column178: \" whites looted the 79 huts that the Chinese lived in while raping the women and beating the children. Eventually the whites burnt down the camp and 600 Chinese fled to safety.
